Matt Damon has been at the center of controversy after making comments about the "spectrum of behavior" in the context of sexual harassment and assault. In an interview with ABC News, Damon suggested that there is a continuum of behavior, ranging from rape and child molestation to lesser forms of misconduct, and that the latter should not be conflated with the former. However, his comments were met with criticism from several individuals, including actress Alyssa Milano and Minnie Driver.

Alyssa Milano, a vocal advocate for the #MeToo movement, took to Twitter to call out Damon's comments, stating that they are "tone deaf" and that the actress has been a victim of sexual harassment. Milano argued that the "micro" behaviors, such as grabbing someone's butt, contribute to a culture of outrage and that women are not outraged simply because someone grabbed their butt, but because they were made to feel that this behavior was normal.

Minnie Driver, who starred alongside Damon in the film "Good Will Hunting," also criticized his comments, stating that they are "Orwellian" and that men should not attempt to differentiate between types of abuse. Driver argued that men simply cannot understand what abuse is like on a daily level and that they should not attempt to speak on behalf of women in this regard.

Matt Damon's comments were also criticized by actor Salma Hayek, who wrote a lengthy essay for the New York Times about her own experiences with Harvey Weinstein. Hayek argued that Weinstein's behavior was not an isolated incident, but rather part of a larger culture of harassment and abuse that has existed in the entertainment industry for years.

In response to the controversy surrounding his comments, Damon attempted to clarify his position, stating that he believes that there is a spectrum of behavior and that the most egregious forms of abuse, such as rape and child molestation, should be dealt with severely. However, Damon's attempts to clarify his position were met with further criticism, with some arguing that his comments were still tone-deaf and that he failed to understand the gravity of the issue.

The controversy surrounding Matt Damon's comments has highlighted the ongoing struggle to address and prevent sexual harassment and assault in the entertainment industry. As the industry grapples with the consequences of its past behavior, it is clear that there is still much work to be done to create a culture of respect and safety for all individuals.

In related news, the #MeToo movement continues to gain momentum, with more women coming forward to share their stories of harassment and abuse. The movement has sparked a national
details.ground_truth: Matt Damon opined this week that the wave of sex harassment and assault claims sweeping Hollywood and society at large include "a spectrum of behavior" and that "none of us came here perfect," and the woman he once dumped on Oprah wasted no time in shutting him down. "Gosh it’s so interesting (profoundly unsurprising) how men with all these opinions about women’s differentiation between sexual misconduct, assault and rape reveal themselves to be utterly tone deaf and as a result, systemically part of the problem," Minnie Driver tweeted. She further unloaded to the Guardian: "I’ve realized that most men, good men, the men that I love, there is a cut-off. They simply cannot understand what abuse is like on a daily level. I honestly think that until we get on the same page, you can’t tell a woman about their abuse. ... It is so individual and so personal, it’s galling when a powerful man steps up and starts dictating the terms, whether he intends it or not.” Damon was also getting zero sympathy from Alyssa Milano, reports USA Today, who tweeted that, "as a victim of each component of the sexual assault spectrum of which you speak," "they all hurt." "We are not outraged because someone grabbed our a---s in a picture. We are outraged because we were made to feel this was normal. We are outraged because we have been gaslighted. We are outraged because we were silenced for so long." (Damon has said he had no idea that Harvey Weinstein routinely sexually harassed and assaulted women; his full interview this week with ABC News is here.)

Microsoft Unveils Kinect: A Revolutionary Motion-Control System

Microsoft has formally announced the name of its highly anticipated motion-control system, previously known as Project Natal. The system, now officially called Kinect, is designed to revolutionize the way people interact with their Xbox 360 console.

During a special event at the University of Southern California's Galen Center, Microsoft demonstrated the capabilities of Kinect, showcasing a wide range of games and experiences that utilize the system's motion-sensing technology. The event, which was part of the E3 Expo videogame industry tradeshow, was attended by thousands of people, who were all given white ponchos with illuminating shoulder pads to wear.

Kinect allows users to play games and interact with their Xbox 360 console without the need for a controller. The system uses a combination of cameras and sensors to track the user's movements, allowing for precise control and recognition of a wide range of gestures and facial expressions.

Microsoft showcased several games that will be available for Kinect, including:

* Whitewater rafting and racing games
* Virtual Tai Chi and a preview of a Star Wars game that lets players fight Darth Vader with a light saber
* A collection of sports games that lets players jump hurdles, run sprints, play volleyball, kick a soccer ball, and bowl
* A Yoga/Tai Chi game (official name to be announced)
* A Soccer Game (official name to be announced)
* Joyride, a racing game where players can steer using their hands
* Kinect Sports, which includes boxing, bowling, beach volleyball, track and field, soccer, and table tennis
* Kinectimals, a game that allows players to interact with 20 different virtual kittens
* Kinect Adventures, which includes river rafting with multiple players that move their bodies in the raft
* Dance Central, a dance-em-up game made by MTV Games

In addition to games, Microsoft also demonstrated the use of Kinect for non-gaming experiences, such as interacting with Netflix and sharing photos.

Kinect is set to launch this fall, and Microsoft will provide more information on pricing and availability at its Monday press conference.

Overall, Kinect has the potential to revolutionize the way people interact with their Xbox 360 console, and Microsoft's demonstration of the system's capabilities has generated significant excitement in the gaming industry.
details.ground_truth: Microsoft unveiled its much-hyped Project Natal motion control system—now renamed “Kinect”—at E3 last night, in a performance long on grandiosity and short on actual revelations. After walking through a set of fake living rooms, the white-poncho-wearing audience was treated to a performance by Cirque du Soleil acrobats dressed as cavemen, as a narrator declared that “history is about to rewritten” and a boy, raising his arms, used his movements usher in the new motion-based way to play games, according to the Wall Street Journal. And what was this grand historical event all about? Well, mostly mimicking the Wii, according to CNET. Microsoft’s camera-based motion controller may have potential, but the games demoed last night mostly looked like analogues for successful Wii efforts, including a yoga game, a racing game, and a Wii Sports-esque game titled—wait for it—Kinect Sports. IGN has a full list of launch titles. Microsoft is expected to reveal more details, including pricing, at its press conference today.

**News Summary**

A diverse range of news stories have captured attention recently, spanning from memorabilia auctions to charitable causes and rare film discoveries.

The first notable story revolves around the personal effects of Ted Kaczynski, also known as the Unabomber, which are currently being auctioned online. The U.S. Marshall's office is selling off items such as his handwritten manifesto, which has garnered a bid of $17,525, and his iconic hoodie, which has reached $20,025 along with a pair of sunglasses. Other items, including a Smith-Corona typewriter and a hand-bowed wood saw, have also attracted bids. The auction aims to raise $15 million in restitution to the victims and their families.

In a stark contrast, a charitable auction has taken place, where celebrities have donated their luggage and other items to raise funds for youth groups across the country. Kellan Lutz, known for his role in the Twilight series, has contributed his travel items, along with those from Taylor Swift and Jessica Simpson, to the eBay auction, which has already raised over $15,000.

Lastly, a rare film reel, Charlie Chaplin in Zepped, is set to fetch a six-figure sum at auction. The film, which dates back to 1917, is the only known surviving copy and features the famous actor bringing down a German Zeppelin during a London raid. The seven-minute reel contains some of the earliest known animation and is believed to have been made as a morale-boosting film for British troops during World War One.

These stories highlight the diverse world of auctions, ranging from the macabre to the charitable and the rare.
details.ground_truth: Get out your checkbooks, kids: There is a slew of famous and infamous items up for auction. The US Marshal's office's online auction of the Unabomber's personal effects ends tomorrow, and Aol's Weird News astutely observes that Ted Kaczynski's 35,000-word handwritten manifesto ($17,525) is currently going for less than the gray hoodie ($20,025) that figured so prominently into his FBI Wanted poster. Bonus: sunglasses included. Looking for more of a bargain? A bow and arrows in a Sears box is currently a steal at $743. And in other bizarre auction news: Balloon boy's dad is trying to auction off the infamous "flying saucer" balloon for $1 million. But don't worry, reports KTLA: Richard Heene has vowed to give all the money to charity (specifically, Japan). A bunch of celebrities are hoping JetBlue loses their luggage ... right into the hands of eager bidders. The Celebrity Baggage Auction will benefit DoSomething.org. Buy a signed Jessica Simpson-brand bag or a leather Coach bag personally used by Rosario Dawson, and get two roundtrip JetBlue tickets, too. Click for more. Maybe eBay is the place to buy stuff: Morace Park bought a film reel for $5.25 that turned out to be the only known copy of the Charlie Chaplin film Zepped. The Telegraph reports that it's expected to fetch six figures in a June 29 auction.

Kevin Spacey, the 58-year-old Academy Award-winning actor, has been embroiled in a sex scandal after multiple men came forward to accuse him of sexual harassment, assault, or attempted rape. The allegations span decades, with the first accusation made by actor Anthony Rapp on October 29, claiming that Spacey made unwanted sexual advances towards him in 1986 when he was just 14 years old.

Since then, over a dozen men have come forward to share their stories, including:

1. Roberto Cavazos, a Mexican actor who said Spacey fondled or tried to fondle him against his will at the Old Vic theater in London.
2. Daniel Beal, who claimed Spacey exposed himself to him outside a hotel in 2010.
3. Justin Dawes, who said Spacey gave him and his friend cocktails and played pornography on the TV in his apartment in 1988.
4. Mark Ebenhoch, who alleged that Spacey instructed one of his young male crew members to solicit him for sex in 1995.
5. Harry Dreyfuss, the son of Richard Dreyfuss, who said Spacey groped his genitals when he was 18 in 2008.
6. Tony Montana, who claimed Spacey groped his genitals in a bathroom in 2003.
7. Kris Nixon, a bartender who said Spacey grabbed his crotch in 2007.
8. An anonymous bartender who accused Spacey of sexual assault in 2008.
9. An anonymous child actor who said Spacey began a sexual relationship with him in 1983 when he was 14.
10. An anonymous production assistant who claimed Spacey sexually assaulted him while driving him to the Baltimore-area set of House of Cards.
11. An anonymous British journalist who said Spacey repeatedly grabbed his genitals and angrily tried to block him from fleeing.
12. "John," who claimed Spacey tried to seduce him at his New York apartment in 1984.
13. Heather Unruh's son, who said Spacey groped his genitals in a bar in Nantucket, Mass. in 2016.
14. Andy Holtzman, who said Spacey committed a "wordless assault" on him in 1981.

Heather Unruh, a former Boston news anchor, held a press conference on November 8 to accuse Spacey of sexually abusing her son. She claimed that Spacey stuck his hand in her son's pants and on his genitals at a bar
details.ground_truth: A former Boston TV news anchor says that last year, when her son was 18, he was at a bar in Natucket, Mass., when he met Kevin Spacey. The actor "bought him drink after drink after drink and when my son was drunk, Spacey made his move and sexually assaulted him," said Heather Unruh at a press conference Wednesday. She says Spacey stuck his hand down her son's pants and onto his genitals, and tried to convince him to go to a party where they could continue to drink, USA Today reports. A bystander asked the teen if he was OK and the teen said no, at which point the woman urged the teen to flee and he took her advice, Unruh said. Unruh, who appeared with an attorney, says her son filed a police report last week and handed over evidence; she says a criminal investigation has been opened but the Nantucket Police Department has not confirmed that. The attorney with Unruh noted that her son's accusations are "well within" both the civil and criminal statutes of limitations. USA Today has a list of the 14 people so far accusing Spacey of sexual assault or harassment. Meanwhile Jon Bernthal, who worked with Spacey on Baby Driver, told a SiriusXM radio show Tuesday that he lost respect for Spacey while working on the film. "I thought he was a bit of a bully," Bernthal said, per People. "I didn’t really care for the way he was behaving toward some of the other people on set."

**West Africa in Crisis: Mali Coup Threatens Stability**

The West African country of Mali is on the brink of collapse after a military coup on March 22 ousted President Amadou Toumani Touré. The coup has created a power vacuum that has allowed a Tuareg rebellion to take control of the northern part of the country, including the key towns of Kidal, Gao, and Timbuktu. The rebels, who are divided into two groups – the National Movement for the Liberation of Azawad (MNLA) and Ansar Dine, linked to al-Qaeda – have imposed Islamic law on the areas under their control.

The international community is scrambling to respond to the crisis, with the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS) imposing sanctions on Mali, including a ban on imports and a freeze on the country's bank account. The sanctions are expected to have a devastating impact on the country, with landlocked Mali relying heavily on imports for fuel, which will now be blocked. The country's electricity grid is also expected to falter in the coming weeks, exacerbating the humanitarian crisis.

The crisis in Mali has significant implications for the entire Sahel region, with over 13 million people in need of food aid due to a recent drought. The African Union has also imposed sanctions on Mali, and the UN Security Council is set to hold an emergency meeting on the crisis.

The Malian military junta, led by Capt. Amadou Haya Sanogo, has refused to relinquish power, despite the sanctions. However, a former minister, Mohamed Ag Erlaf, has identified himself as the chief negotiator for the junta and said that the main rebel group, the MNLA, is open to discussions on the future of the country.

The crisis in Mali has also raised concerns about the preservation of the country's cultural heritage, particularly the ancient city of Timbuktu, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The city's unique mud and wood architecture is under threat, and the rebel takeover could disrupt efforts to preserve the city's manuscripts and literary treasures.

As the situation in Mali continues to deteriorate, the international community must act quickly to prevent a humanitarian disaster and ensure the stability of the region.
details.ground_truth: Since we last checked in, the situation in Mali following a soldiers' coup has spiraled, with rising concerns over food and gas shortages, violence, and indeed the country's "very existence," according to Le Monde. An update: The coup has driven more than 200,000 people from their homes to other parts of the region, the AP reports. The UN worries that major food shortages could be imminent, and "mayhem in these towns and cities is increasing," says a rep. Neighboring countries have established an embargo against the rebels who ousted the president; they've closed off their borders and frozen the country's regional bank account. That has prompted residents of Mali, which imports all its fuel, to rush to gas stations to collect fuel. The country's electricity grid may also be headed for failure. UNESCO is citing a threat to Timbuktu, a World Heritage Site full of "architectural wonders," notes the BBC. Mosques there are "essential to the preservation of the identity of the people of Mali," says the group. Yet "nothing seems to be able to stop" the Tuareg coup, Le Monde reports. West African leaders need to help Mali's military fight back before the instability spreads beyond the country's borders.

**Global Efforts to End Child Institutionalization: A Solvable Problem**

In a concerted effort to bring awareness to the issue of child institutionalization, author JK Rowling has taken on a new role as president of Lumos, a charity dedicated to closing down child institutions worldwide. Founded in 2005, Lumos aims to shed light on the lives of millions of children separated from their families due to poverty, disability, and discrimination.

According to Lumos, an estimated 8 million children globally are institutionalized, with the majority having parents who could care for them. However, due to a lack of resources, support, and alternative care options, families are forced to place their children in institutions, often in atrocious conditions. The consequences of institutionalization are severe, with children suffering from malnutrition, developmental delays, and psychological trauma.

Rowling's personal experience with child institutionalization was sparked by a heart-wrenching photograph of a child locked in a caged bed in a residential institution. This image led her to write letters to influential individuals and eventually establish Lumos. The charity has since made significant progress in several countries, including Moldova, the Czech Republic, and Bulgaria, where institutionalization rates have decreased dramatically.

Lumos estimates that the institutionalization of children can be eradicated globally by 2050, a goal that is both ambitious and achievable. To achieve this, the charity advocates for investment in inclusive education and health, as well as support for vulnerable families. International donors play a crucial role in this regard, and their funding decisions can greatly impact the availability of support for children and families.

Ending child institutionalization is not only a moral imperative but also a sound economic decision. It is more cost-effective to support a child in a family than in an institution, reducing long-term costs and promoting independence in adulthood.

As part of its efforts, Lumos has launched a social media campaign, #Letstalklumos, to create awareness and engage the public in ending child institutionalization. With the support of international donors and a growing body of evidence, Lumos is confident that this goal can be achieved within the next few decades.
details.ground_truth: JK Rowling makes the case today that millions of kids around the world are living in appalling conditions—the stuff of Grimms' fairytales—in institutions that are supposed to be caring for them. Rowling wants to change that, not by improving these orphanages with "pretty murals" or teddy bears, but by eliminating them altogether, she writes in the Guardian. The big challenge is providing support for often-poor families who think they have no choice but to place their child in such an institution. If a child's biological family isn't up to the task, then foster families can step in. But Rowling argues that institutions are absolutely the wrong answer. Rowling has started a charity called Lumos (yes, after one of her Harry Potter spells), and writes that it has made genuine progress in reducing new placements in countries such as Bulgaria, Moldova, and the Czech Republic. She wants to raise awareness and donations with campaigns like this one. "I recently committed to becoming president of Lumos for life," writes Rowling. "It is my dream that, within my lifetime, the very concept of taking a child away from its family and locking it away will seem to belong to a cruel, fictional world." Click for her full column.

**U.S. and Pakistan Agree to Improve Relations**

In a bid to mend strained ties, the United States and Pakistan have agreed to work together in future operations against "high-value targets" within Pakistan's territory. The joint announcement was made during a visit by U.S. Sen. John Kerry, who met with Pakistani leaders to discuss ways to improve their relationship.

**Bin Laden Raid and Its Aftermath**

Kerry defended the U.S. decision to conduct a unilateral operation on Pakistani soil to kill al-Qaeda chief Osama bin Laden, saying that the extreme secrecy surrounding the raid was necessary to protect the lives of U.S. professionals involved. He acknowledged that the operation had caused outrage among Pakistanis, but emphasized that bin Laden and his followers had "violated Pakistan's sovereignty."

**U.S.-Pakistan Cooperation**

As part of the efforts to improve ties, Pakistan has agreed to return the tail of a U.S. military helicopter that was damaged during the bin Laden raid. The handover of the helicopter tail is one of several steps agreed upon by Kerry and Pakistani officials, including increased cooperation on joint operations and intelligence sharing.

**Concerns and Challenges**

However, tensions between the two countries remain, with Pakistani officials continuing to express anger over the bin Laden raid and U.S. officials questioning Pakistan's support for Islamist militancy. Kerry emphasized that the relationship between the U.S. and Pakistan is crucial, citing the importance of Pakistan's assistance in the war in Afghanistan and the potential dangers of destabilizing the Pakistani government.

**Next Steps**

In the coming days, senior White House officials will visit Pakistan to discuss the implementation of a "road map" for U.S.-Pakistan cooperation. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton's visit to Pakistan, which was planned for this month, is still on the table. Kerry said that actions, not words, will be critical in earning the votes of members of Congress who have called for an end to U.S. aid to Pakistan.

**Regional Tensions**

Meanwhile, anti-U.S. sentiment remains widespread in Pakistan, with many Pakistanis viewing the U.S. as having betrayed their country's trust. The visit by Pakistani Prime Minister Yousaf Raza Gillani to China, a trusted ally, is seen as a sign of Pakistan's efforts to diversify its relationships and reduce its dependence on the U.S.
details.ground_truth: The US and Pakistan have agreed to work together in the future against "high value targets" in Pakistan, the countries announced in a joint statement today. The news comes after Sen. John Kerry arrived in Pakistan last night, intending to send a strong message following the death of Osama bin Laden: Cooperate more when it comes to rooting out terrorists, or face "profound" changes in the US-Pakistan relationship—which could include kissing billions in aid goodbye. Kerry is the most senior US official to go to Pakistan since bin Laden's death, but since issuing the joint pledge, he said Secretary of State Hillary Clinton will soon announce her own plans to visit, the AP reports. Kerry's meetings with Pakistani leaders began last night, the Washington Post reports. Many in Washington think Pakistan is harboring Islamist militants, and some members of Congress are calling for the US to sever the billions of dollars in aid it provides to Pakistan. Kerry is in Pakistan to lay out the new stakes following bin Laden's death, and question officials about how bin Laden was able to hide in Pakistan for years. In Kabul, Kerry said there is "some evidence" that the Pakistan government has knowledge of insurgent activities, calling it "very disturbing."

**Deadly VX Nerve Agent Used in Assassination of North Korean Leader's Half-Brother**

In a shocking turn of events, the banned chemical weapon VX nerve agent has been confirmed to be the cause of death of Kim Jong-nam, the half-brother of North Korean leader Kim Jong-un. The 45-year-old was poisoned on February 13th while waiting for a flight at Kuala Lumpur International Airport in Malaysia.

According to Malaysian police, Kim Jong-nam was attacked by two women who coated their hands with the toxic substance and wiped it on his face. One of the women later fell ill and suffered from vomiting, but the exact extent of her exposure is still unclear.

The attack has sparked a diplomatic crisis between Malaysia and North Korea, with Pyongyang denying any involvement in the assassination. Malaysian authorities have accused North Korea of orchestrating the hit, citing the use of VX nerve agent, a substance that is banned by the Chemical Weapons Convention.

North Korea has a long history of developing and using chemical weapons, with estimates suggesting that it has a stockpile of up to 12,000 tons. The country is believed to have the capacity to produce up to 4,500 metric tons of chemical weapons per year.

The use of VX nerve agent has raised serious concerns about public safety, as the substance can remain lethal for a long period of time and can be absorbed through skin contact, inhalation, ingestion, and eye contact. Malaysian authorities have confirmed that the airport has not been decontaminated, and it is feared that the substance may have contaminated the surrounding area.

The assassination of Kim Jong-nam has sent shockwaves around the world, with many speculating that North Korea may have used a hit squad to carry out the attack. The case has also raised questions about the effectiveness of international sanctions and the ability of countries to prevent the use of chemical weapons.

As the investigation continues, Malaysia has placed several North Korean diplomats under surveillance and has sought the extradition of several individuals, including the second secretary of North Korea's embassy in Kuala Lumpur and an employee of North Korea's state-owned airline, Air Koryo.

The use of VX nerve agent in the assassination of Kim Jong-nam has highlighted the dangers of chemical warfare and the need for countries to take stronger measures to prevent the use of these deadly substances.
details.ground_truth: Kim Jong Nam's assassins killed him with a banned chemical weapon, Malaysian police revealed Friday. The country's police chief said toxicology reports on swabs from the face and eyes of the exiled North Korean found VX nerve agent, which the BBC notes is classed as a weapon of mass destruction by the United Nations. He said that one of two women believed to have rubbed the extremely toxic substance on Kim's face with their hands suffered from vomiting after the attack. The New York Times reports that VX agent can be created by mixing two compounds—and police suspect the two women put the substances on Kim's face, one after the other, to create a deadly dose. The police chief said the airport where Kim was attacked is now being decontaminated. North Korea—which is widely suspected to have been behind the killing of leader Kim Jong Un's half brother—never signed the Chemical Weapons Convention that banned VX, the AP notes. Pyongyang denies involvement and says Malaysia's investigation is full of "holes and contradictions." The father of one of the two women being held, Vietnamese citizen Doan Thi Huong, tells the Times his daughter trained as a pharmacist and he has seen little of her in recent years. (Police say that after Malaysia refused to give Kim's body to North Korean diplomats, somebody tried to break into the morgue.)

**RICHMOND HIGH SCHOOL RAPE CASE**

A horrific gang rape and beating of a 15-year-old girl occurred at Richmond High School in Richmond, California, on October 24, 2009. The victim, a sophomore, had left the school's homecoming dance early and was attending a drinking session with a group of classmates. The girl consumed a large amount of alcohol and was eventually attacked by a group of 10 people, ranging in age from 15 to their early 20s.

**Initial Arrests and Charges**

The first suspect, Manuel Ortega, 19, was arrested as he ran from the scene of the attack. He was booked on suspicion of rape, kidnapping, and robbery and is being held on $800,000 bail. A 15-year-old student, who knew the victim, was also arrested and booked on suspicion of sexual assault at Contra Costa County Juvenile Hall.

**Additional Arrests and Suspects**

A 21-year-old Richmond man was arrested on Tuesday night on suspicion of sexual assault and rape. Police initially believed that six young men were involved in the attack, but investigators now believe that as many as 10 assailants committed a "slew of crimes" against the girl, including raping her and stealing her jewelry.

**Witnesses and Social Media**

As many as two-dozen people may have witnessed the rapes or been aware of what was happening, but didn't report it. There are rumors that observers took video of the attack on cell phones and may have posted it online.

**Investigation and Reward**

Police are offering a $20,000 reward for information leading to the conviction of any of the assailants. Authorities are also passing out flyers in the area of the campus and serving search warrants at undisclosed locations. Police Lt. Mark Gagan expressed confidence that the list of suspects will expand, and at the end of the investigation, they will get a clear indication of who was there and who did what.

**Campus Security and Response**

Richmond High has surveillance cameras, but none is working. The school officials said a new system should be in place in the next few months. Four police officers and several school security staffers were on hand at the homecoming dance, but two of the staffers left about 9 p.m. because of a "prior commitment."

**Community Reaction**

**Summary of Recent News**

**Nunes Memo and FBI Surveillance**

In a significant development, the White House has approved the release of a memo created by the House Intelligence Committee, led by Chairman Devin Nunes, that alleges the FBI abused its surveillance authority. The memo, which was initially opposed by the FBI and the Justice Department, claims that the agency obtained a secret court order to monitor a former Trump campaign adviser, Carter Page, without disclosing relevant information. The memo highlights issues with the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA) process, including the omission of material and relevant facts by the government in FISA applications.

**Key Findings of the Nunes Memo**

* The FBI and DOJ obtained a FISA warrant targeting Carter Page, a US citizen and former Trump campaign adviser, without disclosing the role of the DNC and Clinton campaign in funding the Steele dossier.
* The FISA application relied heavily on the Steele dossier, which was compiled by a former MI6 agent with strong anti-Trump views.
* The FBI had suspended Steele as a source in 2016 for unauthorized media contacts, but failed to disclose this information in the FISA application.
* The Steele dossier was minimally corroborated by the FBI, and its credibility was questioned by an internal source validation report.
* The memo also raises questions about the involvement of Bruce Ohr, a senior DOJ official, in the Steele dossier and his wife's employment by Fusion GPS, the firm that commissioned the dossier.

**Other News**

* Twitter users can now add location information to their tweets, which can be deleted at any time.
* The FBI has been under scrutiny for its handling of the FISA process and its relationship with the Steele dossier.

Overall, the Nunes memo has sparked controversy and raised concerns about the FBI's handling of the FISA process and its surveillance authority. The memo's release has significant implications for the ongoing investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 US presidential election.
details.ground_truth: The White House on Friday declassified a partisan and bitterly disputed memo on the Russia investigation, and a House committee immediately made it public. Media outlets were just beginning to assess it. You can read the document here, via the Washington Post. The White House move came over the fierce objections of the FBI and Justice Department, which have said the document prepared by Republicans on the House intelligence committee is inaccurate and missing critical context, per the AP. The memo alleges that the FBI abused US government surveillance powers in its investigation into Russian election interference. Trump, who has called the investigation a "witch hunt," has supported the release of the memo in the apparent hopes that it could help undermine the probe being led by special counsel Robert Mueller. The president, dogged by the unrelenting investigation into his campaign's ties to Russia, lashed out anew Friday at the FBI and Justice Department as politically biased against Republicans. "The top Leadership and Investigators of the FBI and the Justice Department have politicized the sacred investigative process in favor of Democrats and against Republicans - something which would have been unthinkable just a short time ago. Rank & File are great people!" Trump tweeted.

**The Wolfman: A Monster of a Movie**

The long-awaited remake of the classic Universal horror film, "The Wolfman," has finally hit theaters. Directed by Joe Johnston and starring Benicio Del Toro, Emily Blunt, and Sir Anthony Hopkins, the movie attempts to revive the genre with a mix of traditional makeup and computer-generated effects. However, critics have largely panned the film, calling it a failed attempt to bridge the gap between classic horror and modern gore.

The story, based on the 1941 original, follows Lawrence Talbot (Del Toro), a traveling actor who returns to his ancestral home to confront his family demons and discovers he is cursed to become a werewolf. The film's visual effects are praised for their beauty, but the acting, particularly Del Toro's, is lackluster. The movie's reliance on gore and graphic violence is also criticized, making it feel more like a product of the modern horror genre than a faithful update of the classic.

Despite its flaws, the movie has its moments of enjoyment, particularly in its atmospheric and moody setting. The transformation scenes, courtesy of makeup effects legend Rick Baker, are a highlight of the film, but ultimately, they are overshadowed by the movie's campy and silly moments.

**Other News**

* In a rare instance of a movie being made worse by its cast, "The Wolfman" is a disaster, with the usually talented Benicio Del Toro, Emily Blunt, and Sir Anthony Hopkins struggling to elevate the material.
* Director Garry Marshall's "Valentine's Day" is a mess, with a convoluted plot, annoying characters, and a lack of humor.
* "Ajami," an Arab-Israeli drama, is a taut, disturbing, and wonderful film that offers a unique perspective on life in an Israeli ghetto.
* "Red Riding Trilogy," a British/Irish horror series, is a standout, with all-star performances and a gripping narrative that delves into the heart of darkness.

**Classic Werewolf Films**

* The 1941 original "The Wolfman" starring Lon Chaney Jr. is a classic, but not a very good movie.
* "An American Werewolf in London" (1981) is a quasicomic take on the werewolf legend, with a standout performance by David Naughton.
* "Wolf" (1994) is an extended metaphor for American business and American manhood, with Jack Nicholson playing a wolfish man.

**Movie Analysis**

* Critics praise the film
details.ground_truth: Critics aren’t exactly howling over the new remake of The Wolfman starring Benicio del Toro, giving it mostly mediocre or failing marks. Here’s what they’re saying: “The movie is pungent with atmosphere, laying down a thick fog of creepy Victorian murk,” writes Kyle Smith of the New York Post, but the story is toothless, centering on a mystery “so simple that even Marmaduke could have sniffed it out.” “The movie is scary only as regards all its wasted potential,” laments John Anderson of the Wall Street Journal. There are good elements here, but they’re “reduced to a gruesome fondue, accessorized by actorly ham and studio cheese.” The filmmakers obviously loved the original Wolfman, but “nostalgia isn't always the best barometer,” writes James Berardinelli of ReelViews. The makeup is “inexcusably campy,” and scenes from the original are “recreated in a fashion that seems more Monty Python than unsettling.” But Ty Burr of the Boston Globe had fun. “The movie is by no means good,” he writes, “but it’s surprisingly enjoyable: a misty, moody Saturday-matinee monster-chiller-horror special.”

**Sinn Fein Leader Gerry Adams Released After Four Days of Questioning**

Gerry Adams, the leader of the Irish nationalist party Sinn Fein, was released on Sunday, May 4, 2014, after four days of questioning by Northern Irish police regarding his involvement in the 1972 IRA murder of Jean McConville, a 37-year-old widow who was abducted from her home, shot, and secretly buried. Adams denies any involvement in the crime and maintains that the IRA is no longer active.

**Arrest and Release**

Adams was arrested on Wednesday, May 1, 2014, under the terrorism act, and was being questioned at Antrim police station in Northern Ireland. He was released on Sunday evening, after a file was sent to the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) for review. Adams claims that the timing of his arrest was politically motivated, as it occurred in the middle of an election campaign.

**McConville Family's Fight for Justice**

The McConville family, who have been searching for justice for 42 years, expressed their disappointment at Adams' release. Michael McConville, the son of Jean McConville, stated that the family's fight for justice would continue. The McConville case is part of the "Disappeared," a group of people abducted, murdered, and buried in secret by republicans during the Troubles.

**Boston College Tapes and Recent Arrests**

The case against Adams is linked to the Boston College tapes, a series of confessional interviews with former paramilitary leaders. Ivor Bell, a leader in the Provisional IRA in the 1970s, was recently charged with aiding and abetting the murder of Jean McConville, based on an interview he allegedly gave to researchers at Boston College. Several other arrests have also been made recently in connection with the case.

**Reaction from Politicians**

Politicians from various parties have responded to the situation. Prime Minister David Cameron discussed Adams' release with Irish Prime Minister Enda Kenny, while Northern Ireland Justice Minister David Ford said that politicians should avoid interfering in policing. Other parties, such as the Democratic Unionist Party, have rejected claims that the timing of the arrest was political.

**Prosecution Decision**

The decision to release Adams means that prosecution lawyers will review the evidence gathered by police and determine whether to bring charges. The test for prosecution is met if there is sufficient evidence to provide a reasonable prospect of conviction and if the prosecution is required in the public interest. This process may take

**Daycare Workers Charged with Injuring 5 Toddlers with Hot Glue**

Two daycare workers, Lizandra Cosme and Susana Gonzalez, have been arrested and charged in connection with an incident at the Children's Learning Place in Chicago, where five 2-year-old toddlers were injured with hot glue on December 1.

According to surveillance footage, Cosme, 32, applied hot glue to the hands and arms of the five children, causing them to wince in pain. Gonzalez, 27, who was an assistant at the daycare, was seen laughing nearby and did not intervene. The footage also showed Cosme attempting to apply Neosporin to one of the children after the incident.

Cosme was arrested on Sunday and charged with five counts of aggravated battery of a child under 13 years old causing great bodily harm. She was denied bail at a court appearance on Monday and is scheduled to return to court on December 26. Gonzalez was charged with five misdemeanor counts of causing the circumstances of child endangerment.

The investigation found that Cosme brought the hot glue gun to the daycare to use on a Christmas project, and that she was watching 16 children at the time of the incident. The daycare center's spokeswoman, Lissa Druss Christman, stated that the well-being of their students is paramount and that two employees were terminated immediately after the incident.

The state Department of Children and Family Services is investigating allegations of abuse, and the daycare center is cooperating with investigators. The extent of the children's injuries is unknown, but they were all taken to a hospital after the incident.

Cosme's defense attorney claimed that his client "screwed up" but did not act out of malice, citing her 12 years of working with young children. However, prosecutors argued that Cosme's actions were intentional and caused harm to the children.

This incident has raised concerns about the safety and well-being of children in daycare centers, and highlights the importance of proper supervision and oversight in these settings.
details.ground_truth: Two workers at a Chicago day care were fired and arrested after a disturbing incident that left five toddlers injured. Police say surveillance video captured one of the workers burning the 2-year-olds with a hot glue gun while the other woman watched and laughed, People reports. Lizandra Cosme, 32, has been charged with five counts of aggravated battery of a child causing great bodily harm, the Chicago Sun-Times reports. Susana Gonzalez, 27, faces five misdemeanor charges of causing the circumstances of child endangerment over the Dec. 1 incident at the Children's Place day care. It's not clear how seriously the children were injured. Prosecutors say Cosme, who brought the glue gun for a Christmas project, was captured on video applying the hot glue directly to the hands and arms of the three girls and two boys. "Each of the child victims winced and some whined at the hot glue gun application," a prosecutor said during a Monday court appearance. Prosecutors said Cosme tried to cover up the incident, asking a father whether his child had been burned at home, ABC7 reports. They said her actions were discovered when one child's mother, an ER physician, saw the burns and demanded to see surveillance footage. Cosme's lawyer said his client "screwed up" but did not mean to hurt the children.

**Texas Gubernatorial Candidate Wendy Davis Reveals Personal Abortion Story in Upcoming Memoir**

Texas Democratic gubernatorial candidate Wendy Davis has disclosed in her upcoming memoir, "Forgetting to Be Afraid," that she had an abortion in the 1990s after discovering that the fetus had a severe brain abnormality. Davis, who catapulted to national prominence with her filibuster against tough new restrictions on abortion, writes about the difficult decision she made to end the pregnancy.

According to Davis, the fetus's brain had developed in complete separation on the right and left sides, and doctors told her that the baby would be deaf, blind, and in a permanent vegetative state if she survived delivery. Davis writes that she could feel the fetus's body tremble violently, and she knew that she needed to end the pregnancy to spare the baby's suffering.

In addition to the abortion, Davis also writes about ending an earlier ectopic pregnancy in 1994, which was considered medically necessary. The ectopic pregnancy was "technically considered an abortion" in Texas, and doctors had to report it as such.

Davis's decision to reveal her personal abortion story has sparked a range of reactions. Anti-abortion groups, including Texans Right to Life, expressed sympathy for the difficult position Davis confronted but emphasized the value of life. Cecile Richards, president of Planned Parenthood, praised Davis's "unwavering courage" and thanked her for sharing her story.

The revelation is likely to have an impact on Davis's gubernatorial campaign against Republican Attorney General Greg Abbott, who is heavily favored to win the election. Davis's memoir is set to go on sale on Tuesday, and the campaign has been seen as a key test of the party's strength in a state that has trended Republican in recent years.

The memoir also provides a personal glimpse into Davis's life, including her struggles to pay the bills as a young mother, her decision to go to law school, and her rise to national prominence with her filibuster against abortion restrictions. Davis's story is likely to resonate with many voters, particularly women, who are looking for a candidate who is honest and authentic.

Ultimately, the revelation of Davis's personal abortion story is likely to add another layer of complexity to the campaign, highlighting the difficult choices that women face in reproductive health and the importance of access to safe and legal abortion.
details.ground_truth: Texas gubernatorial candidate Wendy Davis' new memoir is dedicated in part to daughters Amber, Dru, and Tate, and those familiar with Davis' life story might be puzzled by the last name on that list. That's because Davis is revealing for the first time that Tate is the unborn child that she and her husband aborted in the second trimester, after doctors discovered a severe brain abnormality. The San Antonio Express-News and the AP obtained advanced copies, and the news is generating headlines given that Davis shot to national fame while filibustering against an abortion bill in Texas. Davis writes that she decided to abort in 1996 after doctors said the baby would be blind, deaf, and in a vegetative state if she survived the pregnancy. Davis also felt the fetus “tremble violently" in the womb, "as if someone were applying an electric shock to her, and I knew then what I needed to do. She was suffering." Her doctor "quieted" the unborn child's heart and delivered her by C-section. Davis and her husband had Tate baptized. After the abortion, "an indescribable blackness followed," writes Davis in Forgetting to Be Afraid, "and when I finally did come through it, I emerged a different person." Davis also writes of a previously disclosed procedure to terminate an earlier ectopic pregnancy in which the embryo was implanted outside the uterus. She says she opted not to talk about either case during her famous filibuster because she feared it would "overshadow the events of the day."

**Human Trafficking Ring Busted in Pittsfield, Massachusetts**

In a shocking case of exploitation, two men, Joseph E. Van Wert, 65, and Randy W. Lambach, 45, have been arrested and charged with human trafficking in connection with a citywide prostitution ring in Pittsfield, Massachusetts. The investigation, which began in the spring of 2017, revealed a staggering operation that involved multiple women, some as young as 15, who were lured into prostitution with promises of money, drugs, and other forms of compensation.

According to police, Lambach, who was identified as the ring leader, would seek out women with severe drug addictions and advertise their services on social media platforms, including backpage.com. He would then set up meetings with prospective clients, collect money, and take a significant cut of the earnings or pay the women in heroin, cocaine, or prescription drugs.

The operation, which allegedly spanned several years, was centered around Van Wert's third-floor apartment in a senior living facility, where some of the meetings took place in exchange for drugs and cash. Lambach also worked as a chauffeur, transporting the women to and from specific locations and waiting until they were done.

The investigation revealed that Lambach had recruited and transported about 10 other women in a similar manner and provided narcotics to those who used drugs to get high before having sex. He allegedly threatened to turn the women in if they stopped working for him and warned them that the police would not believe them because they were "drug addicts and whores."

Some of the women Lambach had recruited were formerly his foster children, and a witness estimated that some of the victims looked to be as young as 15. The investigation is ongoing, and additional charges may be coming.

Lambach has pleaded not guilty to four counts of human trafficking, while Van Wert has pleaded not guilty to conspiracy to commit human trafficking, deriving support from prostitution, maintaining a house of prostitution, and sexual conduct for a fee. The Pittsfield Police Department detective bureau, anti-crime unit, drug unit, and members of the state police detective unit assigned to the District Attorney's Office are assisting with the investigation.

If convicted, Lambach and Van Wert face serious consequences, including lengthy prison sentences and fines. The case highlights the need for continued vigilance and efforts to combat human trafficking and exploitation in our communities.
details.ground_truth: Two men have been charged with human trafficking after police arrested them for running a prostitution ring out of a senior living facility in Pittsfield, Mass. Police say Joseph Van Wert, 65, and Randy Lambach, 45, will face a hearing later this month, the New York Daily News reports. Lambach would allegedly seek out women with drug addictions and post their pictures online, then arrange meetings with clients. Some of these meetings took place in Van Wert's apartment in a senior facility. Van Wert offered his apartment in return for cash or drugs. The Berkshire Eagle reports that the police investigation began last spring in response to citizen complaints about increased prostitution across the city. Lambach would allegedly keep up to 90% of the money the women made, sometimes refusing them cash altogether and paying them in heroin or crack cocaine instead. At least one woman said Lambach drove her to an "appointment" across state lines. Police estimated one of the victims was 15 years old. They were also told that some of the women were Lambach's former foster children. Both men have pleaded not guilty, Lambach to four counts of human trafficking and Van Wert to conspiracy to commit human trafficking, deriving support from prostitution, maintaining a house of prostitution, and sexual conduct for a fee.

**Archaeological Discoveries Uncover Secrets of Wiltshire's Ancient Past**

Excavations in Wiltshire have revealed several significant discoveries, shedding new light on the lives of people living in the region over 4,000 years ago. The latest findings include a 4,000-year-old Bronze Age skeleton, believed to be that of a child, unearthed at Wilsford Henge.

The skeleton, which is approximately 1.5 meters in length, was discovered lying in a foetal position and wearing an amber necklace. Scientific analysis of the skeleton is expected to provide information on the child's gender, diet, pathologies, and date of burial, as well as where they may have lived.

Meanwhile, at Marden Henge, a giant ditch and bank enclosure on the edge of Wiltshire, archaeologists have uncovered a well-preserved, rectangular building dating back over 4,300 years. The house, which is one of the best-preserved of its kind in the UK, features beautifully worked flint arrowheads, decorated pottery, shale and copper bracelets, and a Roman brooch.

Excavations at Marden Henge have also revealed pig bones, suggesting that the house may have been used for feasts and gatherings. The site, which was once one of the largest and most impressive in Britain, is believed to have been a symbol of status and wealth, showcasing the ability to mobilize a massive workforce.

Dr Jim Leary, director of the excavation, believes that the site has been overlooked for too long, with attention instead focused on nearby Stonehenge. He notes that the fertile valleys surrounding Stonehenge have been largely ignored, despite being a crucial part of understanding the region's ancient history.

The Vale of Pewsey dig, a partnership between the University of Reading, Historic England, the Arts and Humanities Research Council, and Wiltshire Museum, aims to gain a better understanding of the people who lived in the areas surrounding Stonehenge. Findings to date include flint arrowheads, decorated pottery, shale and copper bracelets, and a Roman brooch.

The discoveries at Marden Henge and Wilsford Henge are set to be showcased at an open day on July 30, part of the national festival of archaeology, offering visitors a unique glimpse into the lives of people living in Wiltshire over 4,000 years ago.

**Key Discoveries:**

* 4,000-year-old Bronze Age skeleton, believed to be that of a child, unearthed
details.ground_truth: You think Stonehenge is impressive? Archaeologists in Britain are excavating a monument ten times larger than the iconic structure, though it appears to the naked eye to be little more than farmland. A henge is a circular earthwork, and the one in question is Marden Henge, which sits a few miles north of Stonehenge in Wiltshire. National Geographic reports that roughly 4,500 years ago it featured 10-foot-tall earthen berms that encompassed some 40 acres. The Guardian writes that the henge was "far larger than the Avebury or Stonehenge circles, and too large for any imaginable practical use." But over the ages Marden Henge's berms have "slumped" and farmers have worked the land, and archaeologists have gravitated to the more breathtaking Stonehenge. Until now. Jim Leary of the University of Reading has just kicked off a three-year study of the site; his excavations (he worked at the site in 2010, too) are the only ones to have occurred there in nearly 50 years. He hopes the berms will help explain the "insane, utterly unsustainable" construction boom that led to Marden Henge and four other nearby Neolithic monuments, including Stonehenge. "Not nearly enough attention has been paid to the archaeology of the fertile valley in between these places," says Leary. So far he's found the remains of a 4,000-year-old teen wearing an amber necklace just outside Marden Henge, per the BBC; fancy arrowheads; and a stone building within the henge containing the bones of at least 13 pigs, suggesting a huge feast was held there. "For all the attention that has been lavished on Stonehenge over the years, we may well find out that Marden was where it was really at during the Neolithic," he says. (There's a giant "super henge" under Stonehenge.)

**BP Shareholders Reject CEO Bob Dudley's 20% Pay Rise Amid Company's Worst-Ever Loss**

BP PLC's annual general meeting saw a significant backlash from shareholders against CEO Bob Dudley's 20% pay rise, despite the company's record-breaking loss of $5.2 billion last year. A total of 59% of investors voted against the company's executive compensation decisions, including Dudley's pay package, which was valued at nearly $20 million.

Dudley's pay rise was criticized by several institutional investors, including Aberdeen Asset Management and Royal London Asset Management, who argued that it was "simply too high" and sent the wrong message to the market. Glass Lewis, a leading shareholder advisory firm, also recommended that shareholders vote against the pay package, citing concerns over the company's performance.

BP's Chairman Carl-Henric Svanberg acknowledged the strength of feeling among shareholders and promised to review the company's pay terms. However, he refused to adjust Dudley's pay despite the nonbinding vote.

The backlash against Dudley's pay rise is part of a growing trend of institutional investors taking a more aggressive stance on executive pay. Several experts argue that Dudley is merely earning the market rate for international executives, but others have questioned the company's decision to award him a significant pay rise in a year when the company made a record loss.

BP's management has defended the pay rise, citing the company's strong safety and operational performance in 2015. However, the company's decision to award Dudley a significant pay rise has sparked concerns among investors that the board is out of touch with the company's performance and the interests of its shareholders.

The vote is a significant blow to BP's management and a rare case of a top British company being defeated over executive pay. It highlights the growing trend of shareholder activism and the increasing scrutiny of executive pay practices in the UK.

BP's pay policy is subject to a binding shareholder vote every three years, and the company will need to review its compensation plan for next year to address shareholder concerns. The company's decision to review its pay terms is a positive step, but it remains to be seen whether it will lead to significant changes in the company's executive pay practices.

Overall, the vote against Dudley's pay rise is a clear message from shareholders that executive pay practices need to be reformed to reflect the company's performance and the interests of its shareholders.
details.ground_truth: Oil giant BP lost $5.2 billion last year, but the company somehow saw fit to propose maximum bonuses for 2015 for its top executives, including a 20% pay increase for CEO Bob Dudley—a proposal that 59% of shareholders roundly rejected by proxy vote at Thursday's annual meeting, MarketWatch reports. The company had indicated earlier in the day that it may also have to reduce its dividend, the Wall Street Journal reports. Dudley is set to receive the full bonus he was eligible for, which comes to $4.2 million (including $1.4 million in cash and a portion in deferred BP shares), per an earlier MarketWatch report. This amount was bumped up from the $3 million ($1 million in cash) he received in 2014. BP's CFO was also on the list to rake in his full bonus. "We think it sends the wrong message," a rep for shareholder Royal London Asset Management tells the BBC. "It shows that the board is out of touch." Not only did BP suffer straight-up monetary losses as the price of oil fell, it also announced it will be getting rid of about 7,000 jobs and taking other belt-tightening measures. And the Financial Times notes that other energy company execs saw their pay slashed in 2015. But a BP spokesman says "executives performed strongly in a difficult environment in 2015, managing the things they could control and for which they were accountable." Andy Critchlow, writing for the Reuters Breakingviews blog, agrees. "Dudley has to work harder than his predecessors," he writes, noting the CEO has helped the company recover from the 2010 Deepwater Horizon disaster and improved safety protocol. "The mild-mannered American has had possibly the toughest job in the oil industry. His rewards look in line with that task." Carl-Henric Svanberg, chairman of BP's board, says the nonbinding shareholder vote won't alter the payouts they've already decided on, but that the board will take investors' concerns into account when coming up with next year's compensation packages.

**Peru's Top Investigator Suspended for Misleading Public over Serial Killers Claim**

In a shocking turn of events, Peru's top organized crime investigator, Eusebio Felix (also known as Felix Murga in some reports), has been put on indefinite leave after he misled the public by claiming that a gang of serial killers, known as "Pishtacos," had murdered up to 60 people and sold their fat to Italian cosmetics makers for thousands of dollars a liter.

According to police, the alleged killers were apprehended in the mountainous region of Huanuco, where they claimed to have found human fat stored in an empty bottle of Inca Kola, a popular soft drink in the Andes. However, after weeks of doubts and criticism, police in Lima revealed that the investigation had been botched and that there may have been only one murder victim linked to the cocaine trade.

The four alleged killers, who are still in custody, may have bottled the victim's fat to intimidate their rivals in an area rife with drug trafficking and violence. Anthropologists have criticized the police for foolishly believing the Pishtacos legend and using it to explain a strange crime.

Medical experts have also dismissed the theory that human fat is valuable and can be sold for thousands of dollars, pointing out that it can be easily collected from plastic surgery clinics that perform tummy tucks. Doctors have also stated that injecting human fat from one person to another can be potentially life-threatening.

The police's mishandling of the case has led to widespread criticism, with politicians blaming the police for scaring away tourists and medical experts calling the story a "ruse of bad taste." The incident has also raised questions about the police's ability to handle complex investigations and the potential for sensationalism in the media.

Peru's police chief, Miguel Hidalgo, has publicly apologized for the incident and said that the police's image and respectability have been affected. The incident serves as a reminder of the importance of verifying information and avoiding sensationalism in the pursuit of justice.
details.ground_truth: The Peruvian cop who claimed to have busted a crime ring that killed dozens of people to harvest their fat has been suspended for lying. Felix Murga, the country's top organized crime investigator, said that a gang had killed 60 people to sell their fat at $15,000 a liter. Investigators now believe there was just one victim and his murder was linked to drug trafficking, the BBC reports. Murga appears to have revived an ancient Andean legend of killers who roam the mountains extracting fat from travelers, say authorities, who blame him for damaging the police force's reputation and scaring tourists away. "This has been a ruse of bad taste," a local politician tells Reuters.

**Pacific Crest Trail Overcrowding and Hiker Safety Concerns**

The Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) has seen a significant increase in hikers in recent years, particularly since the release of Cheryl Strayed's memoir "Wild" and the subsequent movie adaptation. This surge in popularity has raised concerns about overcrowding, environmental damage, and hiker safety.

According to the Pacific Crest Trail Association, more than 4,000 long-distance hiking permits have been issued in 2016, with over 3,000 people attempting the Northbound thru-hike. This has put a strain on the trail and its surrounding ecosystems, with reports of degraded vegetation, human waste, and financial costs associated with helicopter evacuations and searches.

The PCTA has implemented a permit system with a daily quota of 50 permits to mitigate the impact of large numbers of hikers. However, the effectiveness of this system is being questioned, as the number of hikers is still approaching the daily limit, and there are concerns that the honor system used to regulate hiker numbers may not be sufficient.

Some experts warn that the PCT's fragile desert ecosystems are particularly vulnerable to damage, and that the trail's infrastructure may not be able to cope with the increased numbers of hikers. The PCTA is considering measures to protect the trail and its surroundings, including the installation of shelters and improved waste management systems.

The increase in hiker numbers has also raised concerns about hiker safety. In 2016, there were already 20 rescues in the first 50 miles of the trail, and the PCTA is urging hikers to take responsibility for their own safety and to follow "leave no trace" practices.

Despite these concerns, the PCT remains a popular destination for hikers, and the PCTA is working to promote safe and responsible hiking practices through its #responsiblywild campaign. The campaign aims to encourage hikers to respect the trail and its surroundings, and to take steps to minimize their impact on the environment.

The PCT's popularity has also inspired a new generation of hikers, with many women being drawn to the trail in particular. According to the PCTA, the number of female hikers has increased significantly in recent years, with some reports suggesting that up to 30% of hikers on the trail are now women.

As the PCT continues to attract more hikers, it is essential that the hiking community takes steps to protect the trail and its surroundings, and to promote safe and responsible hiking practices. The P
details.ground_truth: Hikers on the Pacific Crest Trail can expect to encounter harsh-but-beautiful landscapes, extreme fatigue, and—these days, at least—traffic. "You can’t talk about the Pacific Crest Trail without mentioning the crowds," Pete Brook writes for Outside. He should know: Brook currently is trekking the 2,650-mile PCT, which runs from the Mexican border through California, Oregon, and Washington to the border with Canada. But don't take his word for it: The Pacific Crest Trail Association so far this year has issued more than 4,000 permits to people planning on tackling at least 500 miles of the trail. To put things in perspective: 300 people attempted to complete the entire PCT in 2006, according to the AP, with about 120 of those making it. In 2014, 1,000 people set off and about half completed the trail. More traffic means more stress on the land, more improperly disposed of poop, and more inexperienced hikers requiring rescue. So what's with the increase in traffic over the past few years? You can thank Cheryl Strayed and her enormously popular memoir-turned-major-motion-picture Wild. After the book came out in 2012, there was a small increase in interest in the trail, the PCTA's Jack Haskel told the AP in 2015. But after the movie, starring Reese Witherspoon, was released in 2014, interest in the PCT exploded. "Millions are hearing about it now and are being inspired," Haskel said. Strayed, more or less unprepared for the journey, hiked 1,100 miles of the trail as a form of therapy as she grappled with personal demons. "However for others that follow her strategy of deliberate ineptitude, things might not turn out so well," writes DontHikeLikeWild.org. "Our advice to would-be hikers: Grow a spine first. Then get out there and hike the PCT." (This female hiker is the "biggest badass you've never heard of.")

**Pete Davidson on Sobriety, Medical Marijuana, and Mental Health**

Pete Davidson, a cast member of Saturday Night Live, recently opened up about his struggles with sobriety and his reliance on medical marijuana to manage his Crohn's disease. In a heartfelt post on Instagram, Davidson revealed that he has been sober for the first time in eight years, crediting his newfound sobriety to his supportive girlfriend, friends, and his own determination.

Davidson, known for his advocacy of medical marijuana, has used cannabis to cope with his Crohn's disease, which was diagnosed at the age of 17 or 18. He has been open about the benefits of medical marijuana, stating that it is the only thing that helps him eat and perform on SNL. Davidson also credits medical marijuana for allowing him to continue performing on the show, saying that he "wouldn't be able to do SNL if I didn't smoke weed."

The comedian also expressed gratitude for Kid Cudi's music, which he credits with saving his life. In an interview with Power 105.1's The Breakfast Club, Davidson revealed that Kid Cudi's music helped him during a difficult time in his life and that he would have likely taken his own life if it wasn't for the rapper's music.

Davidson's return to sobriety is a significant milestone in his life, and he has encouraged his fans to never give up hope. As he stated in his Instagram post, "Remember to never give up hope because sometimes that's all we got." The comedian's openness about his struggles with sobriety and mental health serves as a reminder of the importance of seeking help and support during difficult times.

In related news, Davidson's first hour-long stand-up special, "SMD," is set to air on Comedy Central on October 29th, and Season 42 of Saturday Night Live premieres on NBC on October 1st.
details.ground_truth: Noticed Pete Davidson's absence from SNL as of late? Well, there's a good reason behind it: The 23-year-old has been focusing on his sobriety. After telling High Times last fall that he couldn't function without the medical marijuana he's prescribed for Crohn's disease, Davidson now says, "I quit drugs and am happy and sober for the first time in eight years" in an Instagram post. Rapper Kid Cudi might have inspired that sobriety, per the New York Daily News. Last fall, Davidson told a radio station, "I would have killed myself if I didn't have Kid Cudi," who'd checked himself into rehab a few weeks earlier.

details.ground_truth: Bryan Singer has already been hit with two lawsuits alleging sexual abuse of minors—and the lawyer who filed both of them, plus other suits against Hollywood bigwigs, says more are coming. "I’ve heard from victims about very recent claims of being sexually exploited in Hollywood," Jeff Herman said at a news conference yesterday, according to Variety. He said one coming lawsuit involves another Hollywood "sex ring." Herman also shared photos showing the second alleged victim (with his face redacted) and Singer at the London premiere of Singer's Superman Returns in 2006; John Doe, then 17, says Singer abused him after the premiere, the New York Daily News reports. As for the strenuous denials from all the Hollywood VIPs named in the various suits, Herman said, "The pushback I’m getting reminds me of the early days of the clergy sex abuse scandal." Meanwhile, in an interview with the Daily Beast, Herman says he himself has vague memories of being sexually abused as a child. He says he's worked on some 800 sex abuse cases in his career, notably involving the Catholic Church and the Boy Scouts.

**Double Shooting in Portland, Texas: A Tragic Incident with a Heartwarming Recovery**

On June 23, 2012, a devastating shooting occurred at Violet Andrews Park in Portland, Texas, leaving one 19-year-old girl dead and another critically injured. Mollie Judith Olgin, a college student, was killed in the attack, while her 18-year-old girlfriend, Mary Kristene Chapa, survived with a gunshot wound to the head.

According to reports, the two girls were shot by an unknown assailant while stopped at the park before a movie date. Chapa, a lesbian and a high school sign language student, was left in critical condition but has been making an "amazing" recovery, according to her brother Hilario Chapa.

The investigation into the shooting is ongoing, with the Texas Rangers assisting the Portland Police Department. While there is no evidence to suggest that the attack was a hate crime, authorities are not ruling it out either. The family of Mollie Olgin has expressed confidence that her killer will be brought to justice.

In the aftermath of the tragedy, the community has come together to support the families of the victims. A candlelight vigil was held in memory of Olgin, and a Facebook event has been set up to keep Chapa in prayers for a speedy recovery. The families have also received support from across the country, with many people expressing their condolences and solidarity.

Chapa's family has set up a donation page to help pay for her medical bills, as she is currently without insurance. The family of Mollie Olgin has also reached out to Chapa's family, and they plan to meet soon.

The case has sparked a sense of outrage and sadness in the community, but it has also highlighted the importance of supporting the LGBTQ+ community and promoting inclusivity. As Mollie Olgin's father Mario Olgin said, "Justice will be served."

Meanwhile, Chapa's recovery is a testament to the resilience of the human spirit. Her brother Hilario has expressed gratitude for the support the family has received, saying that it has lifted their spirits and brought the community together.

As the investigation continues, the community remains hopeful that justice will be served for Mollie Olgin and that Mary Kristene Chapa will continue to make a strong recovery.
details.ground_truth: The dad of the teenage lesbian shot dead in a Texas park called for justice yesterday. He immediately had a "bad feeling" when his 19-year-old daughter, Mollie, failed to show up for work, said Mario Olgin. “It was not like Mollie," he told KIII-TV. "If she had some place to be she was going to be there.” Mollie and her 18-year-old girlfriend, Mary Kristene Chapa, had been shot in the head by an unknown assailant. Mollie died at the scene, but Chapa is making an "amazing" recovery, her brother told NBC Latino. Mollie has just finished her first semester of college and dreamed of becoming a psychiatrist. "She was happy," Olgin recalled. Now, "she's my guardian angel. I know she's looking down on us, in a better place." He's confident police will find her killer. "Justice will be served," he declared. Texas Rangers have joined Portland police in continuing to investigate the crime, but haven't yet discussed any progress. They haven't labeled the shootings a hate crime, but aren't ruling it out.

Scientists have made a groundbreaking discovery in the field of paleontology, unveiling a new species of dinosaur that lived 161 million years ago in what is now northeastern China. The dinosaur, named Caihong juji, is a crow-sized, bird-like creature with colorful feathers that shimmered and shifted in the light like those of hummingbirds.

Caihong's feathers were a key factor in its discovery, with researchers finding microscopic structures in the fossil that indicated the presence of iridescent feathers. The dinosaur's plumage featured a combination of dark and iridescent feathers, with the iridescent feathers covering its head, neck, and chest.

The discovery of Caihong has provided new insights into the evolution of feathers in theropod dinosaurs. The fossil was found to have a unique combination of features, including a shallow skull, a long and shallow maxilla, and a robust premaxilla. The dinosaur's dentary, or lower jawbone, was found to have a distinctive groove and a narrow Meckelian canal.

Caihong's feathers were not only colorful but also highly organized, with each feather having a distinct shape and size. The dinosaur's body contour feathers were proportionally longer than those of other known non-avialan theropods, and its tail feathers were particularly striking, featuring large rectrices with asymmetrical vanes.

Researchers used scanning electron microscopy to analyze the nanostructures present in Caihong's feathers, which were found to be similar to those found in modern bird feathers. The analysis suggested that the dinosaur's plumage was likely black with iridescence on the head, chest, and base of the tail.

The discovery of Caihong has significant implications for our understanding of the evolution of feathers and the behaviors of theropod dinosaurs. The dinosaur's iridescent feathers may have been used for display purposes, such as attracting mates or intimidating rivals. Its asymmetrical tail feathers, which are a key adaptation for flight, suggest that Caihong may have been one of the first dinosaurs to use feathers for flight.

The discovery of Caihong also highlights the importance of exceptional preservation in fossilization. The fossil's exceptional condition allowed researchers to study the dinosaur's feathers and plumage in exquisite detail, providing a unique window into the lives of theropod dinosaurs.

In summary, the discovery of Caihong juji has provided a new and exciting chapter in the story of theropod dinosaurs, highlighting the complexity and diversity of their feathers and behaviors. Further study of the dinosaur's fossil
details.ground_truth: Scientists are now saying there may have been "a more colorful Jurassic World than we previously imagined," thanks to the recent discovery of a fossil in China's Hebei province. Reuters reports that a closer look at the "exquisitely preserved," almost completely intact fossil of a crow-like dinosaur that lived about 161 million years ago revealed that Caihong juji had luminous, brilliantly hued feathers that closely resembled those of hummingbirds. The study in the journal Nature Communications documenting the find explains that "Caihong" means "rainbow" in Mandarin. Scientists think the colorful plumage, which appeared to have covered the bony-crested creature's head, neck, and chest areas, may have kept the dinosaur warm, as well as attracted potential mates; National Geographic compares it to peacock feathers. So how were researchers able to tell what color feathers the creature had from preserved bones? They used high-tech microscopes able to home in on 66 sites on the fossil, detecting tiny cell structures called melanosomes, which underlie pigmentation. Which colors they led to can be found by their shapes, and the melanosomes in Caihong were long and flat like pancakes—similar to those found in hummingbirds, which boast iridescent plumage. Although they can't pick out from the Pantone wheel Caihong's exact colors, researchers say the creature likely sported feathers with a "rainbow glimmer." Study co-author Xing Zu tells National Geographic that Caihong was a predator that spent its days gliding from tree to tree. And "glide" is the operative word, as this dinosaur likely didn't fly: Its feathers were located on its tail, not on its wings like birds, Discover notes. (A study postulated that nearly all dinosaurs had feathers.)
