Across the Arab world, protests and demonstrations continue to escalate as citizens demand change and an end to authoritarian rule. In Libya, security forces fired on pro-democracy demonstrators, killing at least 15 people and injuring many others. Thousands of mourners were shot by snipers in Benghazi as they attended the funerals of other protesters. The Libyan government has been accused of deploying special forces and foreign mercenaries to crush the protests.

In Yemen, police opened fire on thousands of protesters, killing one person and injuring five others. Yemeni President Ali Abdullah Saleh said the country is facing a foreign plot that threatens its future, but protesters have continued to demand his resignation. The crisis in Yemen has been further complicated by the presence of al-Qaida militants.

In Bahrain, thousands of protesters streamed back into the capital's central Pearl Square after the armed forces withdrew from the streets following a bloody crackdown. The royal family appeared to back away from further confrontation following international pressure.

In Algeria, police thwarted a rally by thousands of pro-democracy supporters, breaking up the crowd into isolated groups to keep them from marching. Algerian President Abdelaziz Bouteflika has promised to lift the state of emergency, but a long-standing ban on protests in Algiers would remain in place.

The wave of protests has led to the overthrow of two longtime leaders, Tunisia's President Zine al-Abidine Ben Ali and Egypt's President Hosni Mubarak. The Egyptian military government has taken steps towards a handover of power, including the recognition of a new political party and the promise to end the emergency law.

Overall, the situation in the Arab world remains volatile and unpredictable. While some countries have made progress towards reform, others continue to face significant challenges. The protests have highlighted the deep-seated grievances of the Arab people, including corruption, unemployment, and repressive governments.

Key figures involved in the crisis include:

* Moammar Gadhafi, the Libyan leader who has been accused of using violence to crush the protests
* Ali Abdullah Saleh, the Yemeni president who has been in power for three decades
* King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa, the Bahraini leader who has faced international pressure to address the crisis
* Abdelaziz Bouteflika, the Algerian president who has promised to lift the state of emergency

The international community has called for restraint and dialogue in the crisis, with leaders such as US President Barack Obama and British Foreign Secretary William Hague urging the governments to respect the universal rights of their people.

details.ground_truth: In direct contrast to the "delirious joy" in Bahrain, the death toll keeps climbing in Libya's protests. Moammar Gadhafi's minions killed another 20 people today, bringing the five-day total to at least 104, says Human Rights Watch. Gadhafi has effectively shut off Internet service and forbid media coverage, but witnesses told AP of attacks by police and government loyalists wielding guns, knives, and even anti-aircraft missiles. While the accounts are impossible to verify—and at least one puts the death toll closer to 200—a grim cycle has emerged, reports the New York Times: "Security forces fire on funeral marches, killing more protesters and creating more funerals." In Yemen, meanwhile, President Ali Abdullah Saleh's forces also opened fire on protesters, killing at least one, reports AP.

**RARE NIRVANA PHOTOS AND INTERNET ARCHIVAL EFFORTS**

In recent news, a rare discovery of photographs from Nirvana's first concert in 1987 has made headlines. The photos, which feature the band's early lineup, were unearthed by Maggie Poukkula, the 19-year-old daughter of Tony Poukkula, a member of Seattle band Laytem who hosted the band's first show in his basement. The photos show Nirvana performing covers of Led Zeppelin songs, as well as nascent versions of some of their original songs.

The discovery comes as a part of a larger trend of rare Nirvana artifacts being shared publicly. The band's archives have been extensively explored, with many of their notebooks and audio recordings featuring in the documentary "Montage of Heck" by Brett Morgen. The film has been successful on the festival circuit and is set to return to movie theaters on August 7th.

In other news, the Internet Archive is launching an effort to archive web pages as they are created and preserve links to the versions of those pages that were live at the time. This is part of an effort to fix broken links on the web and ensure that references to archived pages can be used instead of broken links. The goal is to create a more stable and accessible web, where users can still access information even if the original page is no longer available.

The Internet Archive's "No More 404" initiative is currently crawling supported sites to archive their pages and links. This effort aims to preserve the web's history and make it easier for users to access information that may otherwise be lost due to broken links.

**Key Dates:**

* August 7th: "Montage of Heck" returns to movie theaters.
* Ongoing: Internet Archive's "No More 404" initiative crawls supported sites to archive pages and links.
details.ground_truth: Most teens don't find music history in their dad's stuff, but Maggie Poukkula's dad is Seattle musician Tony Poukkula, who was a friend of Kurt Cobain. Her find? Photos of Nirvana's first concert, performed by then 20-year-old Cobain and his new band in March 1987 in Tony Poukkula's basement. "My dad showed me them awhile back, but he never mentioned that's what was going on in the photos," Maggie Poukkula, 19, tells Rolling Stone. "I found out because of all the articles. I didn't realize it was such a historical thing. I thought they were just cool pictures of my dad and Kurt jamming together." She tweeted the photos last week, and the post quickly went viral. That first concert featured the early Nirvana lineup of Cobain, bassist Krist Novoselic, and drummer Aaron Burckhard playing original songs and Led Zeppelin covers. Similar early-days footage of Cobain and Co. featured in the recent documentary Montage of Heck has renewed interest in the grunge band. The Emmy-nominated project includes home movies, never-before-heard music, and intimate interviews with Cobain's family and friends, notes SPIN. After being critically acclaimed at festivals across the country, the film will return to theaters on Aug. 7, adds Rolling Stone. (Want a little more Cobain history? Here's the mix tape he made at 21.)

Clarissa Dickson Wright, the renowned British chef and television personality, has passed away at the age of 66. Born in 1947 in St. John's Wood, London, Dickson Wright was the youngest child of a prominent surgeon who was also an abusive and alcoholic father. Despite her privileged upbringing, Dickson Wright struggled with addiction and was a heavy drinker for many years, eventually becoming bankrupt several times due to her spending habits.

However, after becoming sober at the age of 40, Dickson Wright began a successful career in cooking, which led to her starring role in the popular BBC television show "Two Fat Ladies" alongside Jennifer Paterson. The show, which aired from 1996 to 1999, followed the duo as they traveled the UK in a motorcycle and sidecar, cooking and exploring the country's culinary delights.

After Paterson's death in 1999, Dickson Wright continued to appear on television, hosting several shows, including "Breakfast, Lunch and Dinner" and "Clarissa and the Countryman." She was also an outspoken advocate for rural life and a keen supporter of the Countryside Alliance, which led to her causing controversy with her views on hunting and the badger cull.

Dickson Wright was a complex and multifaceted individual, known for her sharp wit, intelligence, and unapologetic opinions. She was a passionate advocate for traditional British cuisine and was not afraid to speak her mind, often causing controversy with her views on food, politics, and social issues.

Throughout her life, Dickson Wright worked as a cook, a barrister, and a writer, publishing several books, including "A History of English Food" and "Spilling the Beans," her autobiography. She was also a dedicated philanthropist, working with various charities and organizations to support rural communities and promote traditional skills.

Dickson Wright's death has been met with an outpouring of tributes and condolences from the culinary world, her friends, and her fans. She will be remembered as a true original, a brilliant cook, and a fierce advocate for traditional British cuisine and rural life.
details.ground_truth: Both of TV's "Fat Ladies" have sung. Clarissa Dickson Wright, one half of the BBC's "Two Fat Ladies" cooking duo, died in Edinburgh Saturday at age 66, the BBC reports. Wright was a former lawyer who filmed four of the "Fat Ladies" series, going on food-related road trips across the UK in a motorbike and sidecar with Jennifer Paterson, before Paterson died in 1999 from cancer. The New York Times describes Wright as a "rebel," both hosts as "irreverent and eccentric," and the recipes as "sometimes confounding." Wright's eclectic working life also included stints as a cook, an author, and a cookbook shop manager; she also ran a catering business, was a guild butcher, and once worked on a yacht in the Caribbean. In fact, she recently said, "I've had a fantastic life and I've done everything I could have wanted to do and more." It wasn't until her 40s, after she'd recovered from alcoholism, that she got into cooking seriously. As for the perhaps-controversial title of the show that brought her fame? "If you're fat you're fat," she once said. "I hate this modern-day political correctness, that you don't call things by their proper name." Her agent remembers Wright similarly in a statement: "Loved dearly by her friends and many fans all over the world, Clarissa was utterly non-PC and fought for what she believed in, always, with no thought to her own personal cost." There's no word on Wright's cause of death, but the Guardian reports that she had been undergoing treatment at a hospital since the beginning of the year.

**Mountain Walker Dies After Falling Off Tryfan Peak in Snowdonia**

A 53-year-old experienced mountain walker, Jane Wilson, fell to her death while attempting to navigate a route down from Tryfan peak in Snowdonia, Wales. Wilson, an academic engagement librarian at the University of Manchester, was with her husband, Gary, when the incident occurred on March 25.

According to the inquest, the couple decided not to climb to the summit due to approaching dusk and used a smartphone app to guide them down the mountain's west face. Wilson, who was ahead of her husband, fell 20-30 feet down a vertical cliff and tumbled about 150 meters down into a rocky area known as Notch arrete. She sustained severe injuries, including a fractured skull, and died at the scene.

The inquest heard that the couple had been mountain walking and scrambling for six years and were both experienced walkers. However, the inquest also highlighted the risks of relying on smartphone apps for navigation, particularly in areas with complex terrain and limited detail.

Detective Constable Tim Bird, who led the rescue team, said that the couple's change in route was an attempt to traverse the west face to avoid the summit, but it would have been safer to retreat the way they came or go to the North Ridge. Bird also emphasized the importance of carrying a paper map and compass, rather than relying solely on electronic navigation aids.

The coroner recorded a conclusion of accidental death, and the incident serves as a reminder of the risks and challenges of mountain walking. Experts have urged walkers to exercise caution and use a combination of navigation tools, including maps and compasses, to ensure their safety.

The Ogwen Valley Mountain Rescue Team, which responded to the call, has also highlighted the risks of Tryfan's west face, which is responsible for about 30% of the team's incidents. The team's spokesman, Andy Simpson, emphasized the importance of using a map and compass in addition to electronic navigation aids and warned that batteries on mobile phones can run out at critical moments.

The incident is a tragic reminder of the importance of safety and caution when engaging in outdoor activities, particularly in areas with complex terrain and limited accessibility.
details.ground_truth: A hiker's fatal fall in March has been blamed on using a smartphone app map instead of an actual map. The BBC reports Jane Wilson and her husband Gary were hiking on Tryfan in Wales when it started to get dark and they decided to skip the summit. While attempting to descend the mountain, Gary was looking at an Ordnance Survey app while Jane went a short way ahead to make sure the path was safe, according to the Telegraph. The Manchester Evening News reports Gary heard his wife fall, got to safety, and called for help. Rescuers found the 53-year-old librarian's body at the bottom of a 30-foot cliff with a fractured skull. At a hearing Friday, Jane's was ruled an accidental death due, in part, to using an app instead of a map. "Apart from potential difficulties caused by poor detail on an electronic map, batteries on mobile phones have a nasty habit of running out just when you need them most," the Telegraph quotes a spokesperson for Mountain Rescue England and Wales as saying. A detective says the app map "would have been small and not as detailed" and took the couple on a route that was not the safest. A director with Ordnance Survey says hikers should always use the app in conjunction with an actual map "due to the nature of mobile devices when navigating the real world." Authorities also say Jane wasn't wearing appropriate footwear for the hike. (Go inside the futile search for "Inchworm" on the trail.)

"It's way too early to know, or to issue, to pronounce last rites over one performance," Daniels said today. "There's still many of these (debates), too many maybe. ... Mark Warner's work on the Gang of Six, saying, "It's very encouraging, by the way, to hear a guy like Sen. Warner speak to it as clearly as he just did. I wish he were president." More fun on your Sunday dial, as per Politico: Michael Bloomberg on President Obama's 2012 chances: "He would be a credible, formidable candidate. You're going to have a real horse race no matter who the Republican nominee is." Reince Priebus to Debbie Wasserman Schultz on 2012: "It sounds like the new slogan is no longer 'hope and change.' It's, 'Hey, it could've been worse.' Great bumper sticker Debbie, I hope it works for you." David Plouffe on a White House shakeup: "I don't expect that." Obama is "confident in his team, in the direction we've laid out here." Warner on Congress' standoff over a short-term spending bill: "It is embarrassing. The Senate is saying, 'Why should we build schools in Iraq on the credit card but expect that rebuilding schools in Joplin, Mo.,'" will be paid for. Warner blames "a group, more centered in the House, in terms of some of these Tea Party Republicans, who say on every issue, 'We're going to make this a make-or-break.'" Lindsey Graham on Pakistan: "We need to put Pakistan on notice. We're going to have to put all options on the table. including defending our troops. It's now a time of choosing, so I hope they choose wisely."

**Soda Taxes Backfire in Seattle and Cook County: A One-Page Summary**

In recent years, cities across the United States have implemented soda taxes in an effort to reduce sugar consumption and raise revenue. However, two high-profile examples, Seattle and Cook County, Illinois, have shown that these taxes are not only ineffective in achieving their intended goals but also have unintended consequences.

**Seattle's Soda Tax**

Seattle's sugary drink tax, which went into effect on January 1, 2018, adds 1.75 cents per ounce to the price of sweetened beverages. The tax was initially touted as a way to reduce sugar consumption, raise revenue for important projects, and subsidize healthy foods for low-income families. However, the tax has had the opposite effect. A case of Gatorade that cost $15.99 on December 31, 2017 now costs $26.33, an increase of over 64 percent. Retailers like Costco have posted signs directing customers to locations outside the city limits where shoppers can buy the product without paying the massive tax. As a result, people are voting with their feet and buying drinks in the suburbs, depriving Seattle of the revenue it claims to need.

**Cook County's Soda Tax**

Cook County, Illinois, implemented a penny-per-ounce tax on sugar and artificially sweetened beverages in August 2017. The tax was repealed in December 2017 after a tremendous public outcry. The tax had a devastating impact on local businesses, with sales of sweetened beverages declining by as much as 47 percent. Retailers like Costco saw a 34 percent decline in sales of beverages affected by the tax, while sales in neighboring counties increased by 38 percent. The tax also led to a surge in lawsuits, with some retailers facing claims of misapplying the tax.

**Statewide Soda Tax Proposal**

A bill to impose a statewide soda tax in Washington state has been re-introduced, with the goal of discouraging people from consuming sugary drinks. However, opponents of the tax argue that it will hurt local businesses and lead to a decline in sales. A case of soda in Seattle costs nearly double what it did before the tax was implemented, and some are worried that the same outcome will occur if the tax is imposed statewide.

**Conclusion**

The experiences of Seattle and Cook County serve as cautionary tales for cities considering implementing soda taxes. These taxes do not achieve their intended goals of reducing sugar consumption and raising revenue, but rather lead to unintended consequences such as a decline
details.ground_truth: "That new soda tax in Seattle is working out about as well as Chicago's," proclaims a headline at Hot Air. It's not a compliment. The tax, which went into effect Jan. 1, slaps an additional 1.75 cents on each fluid ounce of sugar-sweetened drinks, a group that includes soda, sports drinks, and kombucha. That's nearly double the one-cent levy the Chicago-encompassing Cook County tried, a tax it repealed after about two months. The Chicago Tribune reported at the time that Costco's nine affected locations saw their sales of beverages impacted by the tax fall 34%, while sales jumped 38% at locations just beyond the county line. And Costco is again at the fore of what's happening: Because bulk purchases contain significantly more ounces than an individual one, the impact is starker, as KIRO discovered when it spotted an updated Costco sign for Gatorade in Seattle. The sign details Costco's price for the 35-pack of 16-ounce bottles—$15.99—and separately lists the city's $10.34 tax, for a new total of $26.33. But that's not all the sign says: It also directs consumers to nearby locations that are outside city limits and exempt from the tax. The tax is meant to fight obesity and raise funds for worthy expenditures, but the blog for Citizens Against Government Waste sees the ends quite differently: "Let’s be clear. Soda taxes don’t make people healthier. They don't raise revenue—they drive it outside city limits. They don’t help ease inequality—they make the poor poorer." But some in government seemingly remain hopeful: KXLY reports a bill was reintroduced Monday that would push the tax statewide. (Speaking of Seattle and beverages, the world's largest Starbucks is no longer located there.)

**Man Killed in Hit-and-Run Incident After Trying to Prevent Friend from Leaving Birthday Party**

A 34-year-old man, Jonathan Carlyle Merkley, was killed in a hit-and-run incident early Sunday morning after trying to prevent his friend from leaving his birthday party at the Staybridge Suites hotel in San Diego. According to police, Merkley, a resident of Garden Grove, had been celebrating his 34th birthday with friends at the hotel when the unidentified woman, who was also drinking, decided to leave.

Eyewitnesses stated that Merkley had walked into the path of the woman's BMW and laid down in front of the moving car in an attempt to stop her from leaving. However, the woman did not stop and ran over Merkley, causing major trauma to his chest. The victim died about 45 minutes later at a hospital.

Police have identified the woman who was driving the BMW, but she has not been arrested as of late Sunday morning. An investigation is ongoing, and police are still evaluating the other parties' culpability in the collision.

On Tuesday, police tracked down the woman and interviewed her at a friend's house. A car that "may have been involved" in the crash was also impounded for further examination.

The incident has sparked outrage, with many expressing concern about the value of human life and the importance of taking responsibility for one's actions. Police are urging anyone with information about the incident to come forward and contact the San Diego Police Department or Crime Stoppers at 888-580-8477.

As of now, no arrests have been made in connection with the deadly crash, and the investigation is ongoing.
details.ground_truth: A birthday party in California turned tragic after the guest of honor was run over and killed by his friend. Police say Jonathan Carlyle Merkley was celebrating his 34th birthday early Sunday at a hotel near San Diego when a woman decided to leave the party. Merkley wanted her to stay and reportedly walked toward her BMW and laid down in front of what the Orange County Register describes as a moving car. The woman didn’t stop, ran him over, and kept going. Police cite witnesses as saying both had been drinking, Fox 5 reports. Merkley, who suffered major chest trauma, died about 45 minutes later at a hospital. Police tracked the woman down and a car that "may have been involved" was impounded, cops tell the San Diego Union-Tribune. The woman was not arrested but police said on Tuesday that the investigation is continuing. "There's a lot we still need to find out," says San Diego Police Sgt. Tim Underwood.

**The Humboldt 35: Unraveling the Mystery of Missing Persons in Humboldt County**

A recent investigation by the North Coast Journal has shed light on the phenomenon of missing persons in Humboldt County, California. Dubbed the "Humboldt 35," the list includes 35 individuals who have been reported missing, ranging in age from 1 to 94. While some cases have been resolved, others remain unsolved, leaving families and investigators searching for answers.

One such case is that of Rebekah Martinez, a 22-year-old woman who was reported missing in November 2017 by her mother. However, Martinez was later found to be alive and competing on the reality TV show "The Bachelor." Her case highlights the challenges of tracking missing persons, particularly in a region with a high rate of transient populations.

Humboldt County has the highest per-capita rate of missing persons reports in California, with an average of 717 reports per 100,000 residents between 2000 and 2016. This is significantly higher than the state average of 384. While some cases are resolved quickly, others remain unsolved, with some individuals going missing for extended periods without any apparent leads.

The investigation found that many missing persons cases in Humboldt County are related to the cannabis industry, with some individuals disappearing while working on marijuana farms or in the cannabis trade. This has led to a lack of cooperation from some individuals and families, making it difficult for investigators to locate missing persons.

The North Coast Journal's investigation also highlighted the complexities of tracking missing persons, particularly in a region with a high rate of transient populations. The use of social media and other online platforms has made it easier for families to connect with each other and share information about missing persons cases.

However, the lack of coordination between law enforcement agencies and the Attorney General's database has led to some missing persons cases being listed incorrectly or remaining unsolved. The National Missing and Unidentified Persons System (NamUs) has helped to bridge this gap, providing a national database of missing persons cases and connecting families with investigators.

One family's story, that of Vikki Joseph, whose brother Jeff disappeared in 2014 while working on a cannabis farm, highlights the challenges of investigating missing persons cases. Despite extensive research and investigation, Jeff's case remains unsolved, leaving Vikki Joseph with a sense of frustration and uncertainty.

details.ground_truth: It turns out missing people are pretty easy to find when they appear every week in your living room. The San Francisco Chronicle reports a 22-year-old woman reported missing in November in California was found this week—because she's a current contestant on The Bachelor. The strange story started when Bekah Martinez's mom reported her missing to the Humboldt County Sheriff's Office on Nov. 18. According to BuzzFeed, Martinez's mom said her daughter had gone to the area to work on a marijuana farm and she hadn't heard from her in a week. The Sheriff's Office was unable to find Martinez or contact anyone who might know her, so Martinez was added to the California Department of Justice's list of missing people. That list was the basis for a story in the North Coast Journal about the high number of missing people in Humboldt County. The newspaper shared the story Thursday on Facebook and asked readers if they recognized any of the missing people. Amy O'Brien did. "I was like wait a minute, she looks so familiar," O'Brien tells the Chronicle. "I instantly thought of The Bachelor." The Journal informed the sheriff's office, which contacted Martinez and removed her from the list. "MOM. how many times do I have to tell you I don’t get cell service on The Bachelor??," Martinez tweeted after learning she was a missing person. The Bachelor started filming in September, and it's unclear if Martinez was still filming when she was reported missing. She tweets "the scariest thing" about being a missing person "is that my efforts to conceal The Worst Drivers License Photo Of All Time have been thwarted"

Myrna Arias, a former sales executive for money transfer service Intermex, has filed a lawsuit against her former employer for allegedly firing her after she uninstalled an app that tracked her location 24 hours a day, seven days a week. The app, called Xora, was required for all employees to use on their company-issued iPhones. Arias claims that she was monitored by her boss, John Stubits, even when she was off the clock, and that Stubits would brag about knowing her driving speed and routes.

Arias, who earned $7,250 per month and met all her quotas, researched the app and asked Stubits if Intermex would be tracking her whereabouts when she was off the clock. Stubits allegedly admitted that employees would be monitored while off duty and told Arias that she should tolerate the intrusion because Intermex was paying her more than her previous employer. Arias likened the app to a prisoner's ankle bracelet and informed Stubits that his actions were illegal.

After Arias uninstalled the app to protect her privacy, she was scolded by Stubits and fired a few weeks later. Arias' lawsuit claims invasion of privacy, retaliation, unfair business practices, and other allegations, and seeks damages in excess of $500,000. The app is available on over 140 different types of devices and is offered on a monthly basis for roughly $1 a day per user. However, the app's features raise concerns about employee privacy, as it can track employees' locations even when they are off the clock.

**Industry Trends**

The use of location-tracking apps in the workplace is becoming increasingly common, particularly in service industries. However, employers must be aware of the potential risks and ensure that they have a legitimate business interest to monitor employees outside of work hours. Failure to do so can result in lawsuits and damage to employee relationships.

details.ground_truth: Myrna Arias didn't like the GPS app on her phone that constantly tracked her, so she uninstalled it. The problem: Arias' iPhone was issued by her employer, which required her to run the app constantly, and after she removed it, the California woman was fired. Now she's suing her former employer, money transfer service Intermex, for invasion of privacy, unfair business practices, and retaliation, among other things, Ars Technica reports. Arias started researching the app, then called Xora—its website touts the app's ability to let employers "instantly see where their employees are and where they have been"—and eventually she and some of her colleagues asked a trainer from the app company to clarify. Boss John Stubits then, per the suit, "admitted that employees would be monitored while off duty and bragged that he knew how fast she was driving at specific moments." Arias says she has no problem being monitored while at work, but she complained to Stubits about being tracked in her free time; he was unsympathetic and said she should have no problem because of how much she was being paid by Intermex. "Her manager made it clear that he was using the program to continuously monitor her, during company as well as personal time," the complaint states, noting that he told Arias she needed to keep her phone on at all times in case a customer called. Arias' attorney tells Courthouse News that there was no way to turn the app off, as it would constantly run in the background. "She found it very offensive that they were treating her like a felon," the attorney says. Arias says her boss "scolded" her for uninstalling the program last year, and fired her soon after. (Elsewhere, a woman got fired over a text mistakenly sent to the boss.)

**Freeway Shootings: Two Separate Incidents, Similar Outcomes**

In two separate incidents, law enforcement agencies have dealt with cases of freeway shootings, causing panic among drivers. In Phoenix, Arizona, a judge reduced the bond of Leslie Merritt Jr., a man charged with four freeway shootings that took place in August and September 2015, from $1 million to zero. Merritt was allowed to return home under electronic monitoring after being in solitary confinement for seven months. His defense lawyers argued that ballistic tests raised doubts about the evidence linking him to the crimes.

Meanwhile, in Alabama, a man suspected of firing a gun at motorists on an Alabama highway and exchanging gunfire with officers was killed. The incident occurred in Blount County, northeast of Birmingham, and the suspect's identity has not been released. The vehicle involved in the incident crashed after a pursuit, and the driver was pronounced dead.

Both incidents highlight the dangers of freeway shootings and the challenges law enforcement agencies face in tracking down suspects. While Merritt's bond reduction was a significant victory for his defense team, the case is still ongoing, and his lawyers will continue to argue that he is not responsible for the shootings. In Alabama, the suspect's death has brought an end to the ordeal, but it serves as a reminder of the risks drivers face on the road.

**Key Details:**

- Leslie Merritt Jr. had his bond reduced to zero in Phoenix, Arizona, allowing him to return home under electronic monitoring.
- A man suspected of freeway shootings in Alabama was killed after exchanging gunfire with officers.
- The Alabama incident occurred in Blount County, northeast of Birmingham.
- The suspect's identity has not been released in the Alabama case.
- Merritt's defense lawyers argued that ballistic tests raised doubts about the evidence linking him to the crimes in Phoenix.
details.ground_truth: A judge on Tuesday allowed a man charged in freeway shootings that rattled Phoenix last year to be released from jail amid questions about evidence authorities say links him to the crimes. The judge overseeing the case of Leslie Merritt Jr. reduced his bond to zero and said he can return to his home under electronic monitoring, the AP reports. He was expected to be released later in the day. The reduction of the bond—once $1 million—was a major victory as defense lawyers contend that ballistic tests cast doubt on the claim by authorities that Merritt was behind four of the freeway shootings. "With all due respect your honor, there's no evidence against him to show he's responsible for this," defense lawyer Jason Lamm said. "He is no more the I-10 shooter than, respectfully, you are." Merritt lifted up his shackled arms in celebration as he walked from the courtroom. After the hearing, family members hugged and shook the hands of defense lawyers. Merritt has pleaded not guilty to drive-by shooting, aggravated assault, and other charges. The shootings caused panic on Phoenix-area freeways, where 11 vehicles were hit in August and September. Detectives took Merritt into custody on Sept. 18; in court the next day, Merritt adamantly denied shooting any cars, telling the judge, "I'm the wrong guy." His lawyers immediately began raising questions about the evidence, citing ballistics information and phone records they say provided an alibi for their client. (Also Tuesday, a motorist suspected of shooting at other vehicles on an Alabama highway died after a shootout with officers.)

A woman identified as Snochia Moseley, 26, from Baltimore County, Maryland, killed three people and injured three others before fatally shooting herself at a Rite Aid distribution center in Harford County, Maryland, on Thursday. Moseley, who was a temporary employee at the facility, arrived at work on time and began shooting people both inside and outside the building. The victims were taken to nearby hospitals, and two of them died at Johns Hopkins Bayview Medical Center, while a third victim died at the scene.

The shooting occurred less than a year after a gunman killed three fellow employees at another Harford County business. In the wake of the shooting, Governor Larry Hogan expressed his condolences to the victims and their families, and the state offered its support to the investigation.

Moseley used a 9mm Glock pistol, which was registered in her name, and had multiple magazines for the gun. The motive for the shooting is still unknown, and the police are investigating the incident.

The shooting is one of three high-profile shootings that occurred in the US in the past 24 hours. On Wednesday, a shooting at an office complex in Middleton, Wisconsin, left three people hurt, and a gunman wounded four people at the Masontown Borough Municipal Center in Pennsylvania. In both incidents, the shooters were killed by police.

The Rite Aid distribution center where the shooting occurred is one of the largest in the country, employing over 1,000 full-time and temporary workers. The facility has been closed, and the company is assessing when to reopen it.

The shooting has sparked outrage and calls for action to address gun violence in the US. Former US Representative Gabrielle Giffords, who was shot in the head in 2011, said that the level of gun violence in the country is not normal and that every time a shooting occurs, it should remind people that it is not acceptable.

The police response to the shooting was swift, with deputies arriving at the scene within five minutes of receiving the call. The FBI and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) are assisting in the investigation.

The shooting has also raised concerns about workplace safety and security. The Rite Aid distribution center does not have metal detectors or security cameras, unlike an adjacent building. The company has said that it will provide grief counseling to its employees and will assess when to reopen the facility.

The community is coming together to support the victims and their families, with a family reunification center set up at the Level Volunteer Fire Co

Police say the 26-year-old woman, who has not been identified, died of a self-inflicted gunshot wound to the head. Harford County Sheriff Jeff Gahler said she was a temporary employee at the Rite Aid distribution center where the shooting took place, reports the Baltimore Sun. Police say the suspect killed three people, and three others remain hospitalized with wounds not believed to be life threatening. Details and developments: When, where: The Rite Aid distribution center where the shooting took place is at a business complex in Perryman, about 30 miles from Baltimore, reports WBAL. Deputies got there at 9:06am, within 5 minutes of the first call, said the sheriff. He said that deputies fired no shots and that the shooter used a 9mm Glock semiautomatic handgun. Why: Police aren't speculating about a motive. “Normally, she was a nice person, but she came in in a bad mood,” Mike Carre, an employee at a nearby business, tells the Washington Post, recounting what Rite Aid workers told him. "She wanted to pick a fight," he said. "And then she started shooting.” CNN quotes a law enforcement official who describes her as a disgruntled employee; the site says she worked there as a security guard. The woman lived in Baltimore County, and the gun was legally registered to her.

**Acetaminophen Use in Pregnancy Linked to ADHD in Children**

A recent study published in the American Medical Association journal JAMA Pediatrics has found an association between the use of acetaminophen during pregnancy and ADHD-like behavioral problems in children. The study, which analyzed data from over 64,000 children enrolled in the Danish National Birth Cohort study between 1996 and 2002, revealed that prenatal exposure to acetaminophen may increase the risk of a child being diagnosed with ADHD or exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors.

According to the study, mothers who reported taking acetaminophen for longer periods and later in pregnancy were more likely to have children with ADHD-like behaviors. The data suggests that taking acetaminophen for 20 weeks or more increased the risk of receiving ADHD medication by 50%. However, the study authors agree that their results do not show a cause-and-effect relationship and that more research is needed to confirm the findings.

The study authors, led by Dr. Beate Ritz of the University of California, Los Angeles, suggest that acetaminophen may interfere with maternal hormones that are critical for fetal brain development. However, other experts have pointed out that the study does not provide conclusive evidence and that more variables need to be considered, such as the fact that ADHD runs in families.

The study highlights the importance of not taking a drug's safety during pregnancy for granted and emphasizes the need for more research on the effects of acetaminophen on fetal development. In the meantime, experts recommend that pregnant women should avoid taking acetaminophen frequently or in large quantities and opt for nonpharmacological ways to deal with pain, such as massages, baths, and acupuncture.

**Key Findings:**

* Prenatal exposure to acetaminophen may increase the risk of ADHD-like behavioral problems in children.
* Mothers who reported taking acetaminophen for longer periods and later in pregnancy were more likely to have children with ADHD-like behaviors.
* Taking acetaminophen for 20 weeks or more increased the risk of receiving ADHD medication by 50%.
* The study authors do not find a cause-and-effect relationship between acetaminophen use and ADHD.

**Expert Quotes:**

* "We aren't saying if you take one Tylenol once it will give your child hyperactivity. You should just avoid chronic or long-term use." - Dr. Beate Ritz, University of California, Los Angeles
* "If you are taking a lot, maybe you shouldn't. And really question
details.ground_truth: The bad news about acetaminophen just keeps on coming: A new study finds that use of the drug during pregnancy is linked to "ADHD-like behavioral problems" in children, CNN reports. The Danish study looked at data from more than 64,000 children and found that those who had prenatal exposure to acetaminophen had a 13% higher risk of exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors, a 29% higher risk of being prescribed ADHD medication, and a 37% higher risk of receiving a diagnosis of hyperkinetic disorder, a severe form of ADHD, USA Today reports. The risk increased the later in pregnancy the drug was taken and the longer it was taken. Women who used acetaminophen for 20 weeks or longer had a 50% higher chance of their children being prescribed ADHD medication. But the authors did not find a cause-and-effect relationship, and they note that "exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors" is not the same as actually having ADHD. Right now, doctors consider acetaminophen the "safest" pain reliever for pregnant women, NBC News reports; the study author says pregnant women "shouldn't worry at this point." What could account for the link? The authors think acetaminophen may interfere with key maternal hormones that impact fetal brain development. Another recent study also found that frequent acetaminophen use during pregnancy was linked with a 70% higher risk of behavioral problems in children.

**Bill Cosby Sentenced to Prison, Defense Team Accuses Racism and Sexism**

Actor and comedian Bill Cosby was sentenced to three to 10 years in state prison on Tuesday for drugging and sexually assaulting a woman, Andrea Constand, in 2004. Cosby, 81, was also declared a "sexually violent predator" under state law, requiring him to receive monthly counseling for the rest of his life.

In a dramatic reaction to the sentencing, Cosby's publicist, Andrew Wyatt, made fiery accusations of racism and sexism, comparing Cosby's downfall to the "sex war" happening in Washington, D.C. regarding Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh. Wyatt claimed that Cosby was a victim of a "sex war" and that his trial was the "most racist and sexist trial in the history of the United States."

Wyatt also referenced the persecution of Jesus while talking about Cosby, stating, "They persecuted Jesus and look what happened. Not saying Mr. Cosby's Jesus, but we know what this country has done to black men for centuries." Cosby's team has repeatedly argued that racism played a role in Cosby's conviction, with some members invoking Emmett Till, a black boy murdered after a white woman falsely accused him of whistling at her.

Cosby was taken away in handcuffs after the sentencing and spent his first night behind bars at SCI Phoenix, a new state prison in suburban Philadelphia. He will serve his sentence at the prison, which was chosen due to his special needs and safety issues. Cosby is being housed in a single cell in a unit adjacent to the infirmary and will be allowed phone calls and visitation in accordance with policy.

The sentencing of Cosby comes as the #MeToo movement continues to gain momentum, with many women coming forward to share their stories of sexual assault and harassment. The case against Cosby has been closely watched as a test of the movement's impact on high-profile cases.

In related news, Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh has been embroiled in controversy after two women accused him of sexual assault and misconduct in incidents dating back to the 1980s. Christine Blasey Ford is set to testify before the Senate Judiciary Committee on Thursday, while a second accuser, Deborah Ramirez, has also come forward with her own story of sexual misconduct.
details.ground_truth: Bill Cosby was labeled a "violent sexual predator" by the judge who sentenced him to three to 10 years in prison on Tuesday, but his publicist had a different description: one of the "greatest civil rights leaders" in history and one of the "greatest educators of men and boys." Outside the courtroom in Norristown, Pa., publicist Andrew Wyatt claimed the 81-year-old Cosby had faced the "most racist and sexist trial" in American history, the New York Times reports. Wyatt claimed that three psychologists who testified against Cosby in the sexual assault trial were trying to "make money off of accusing black men of being sexual predators." He also read a statement from Camille Cosby, the disgraced entertainer's wife, alleging that an audio recording played in the trial had been doctored. Wyatt, who previously called Cosby's trial a "public lynching," accused Judge Steven O'Neill of being part of a "sex war" and linked the sentencing to the treatment of Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh. Vox reports that the strangest moment came when Wyatt likened his client to Jesus. "They persecuted Jesus, and look what happened," he said. "Not saying Mr. Cosby is Jesus, but we know what this country has done to black men for centuries." Cosby, who left the Montgomery County Courthouse in handcuffs, spent his first night behind bars at SCI Phoenix, a state prison that opened two months ago in suburban Philadelphia, reports WPVI. (Cosby's mug shot was released Tuesday afternoon.)

**US Secretary of State Hillary Clinton Rules Out Presidential Run**

US Secretary of State Hillary Clinton has ruled out running for president in both 2012 and 2016, ending speculation about a potential second run for the White House. In interviews with New Zealand media, Clinton stated that she is "very pleased" to serve as Secretary of State and does not plan to run for top office.

The comments come in the wake of the Democrats' poor showing in the recent US midterm elections, which has raised questions about the party's prospects for 2012 and 2016. Clinton's husband, former President Bill Clinton, experienced significant losses in 1994, but Hillary Clinton sought to put the Democrats' losses in perspective, stating that they were "big" but "not out of the pattern of historical political elections."

Clinton also defended President Barack Obama, saying that his decisions were essential for the well-being of the American people, even if they were not popular at the time. She acknowledged that the stimulus package and other economic actions taken by the President may not have been popular with the public, but believes that they prevented even worse economic consequences.

During her visit to New Zealand, Clinton was introduced as "President Clinton" by Prime Minister John Key, but he quickly corrected himself and referred to her as Secretary of State. Clinton's visit was seen as an opportunity for her to defuse speculation about her presidential ambitions and to promote US interests in the Asia-Pacific region.

While Clinton has ruled out running for president, she has stated that she believes the US is ready for a female president, and that she hopes that whoever takes on the role will be a woman. Clinton's comments have been seen as a nod to her own potential legacy and a testament to her commitment to promoting women's empowerment.

Overall, Clinton's comments have brought an end to speculation about her presidential ambitions, but have also underscored her commitment to her role as Secretary of State and her support for President Obama.
details.ground_truth: A day after being accidentally introduced as "President Clinton," Hillary seems to be taking pains to kill talk of a future run in New Zealand's media, reports Reuters. Asked whether she'd "ruled out" running through 2016, she replied, "Oh yes, yes. I'm very pleased to be doing what I'm doing as secretary of state." Asked in another interview whether she could be the first female president, she answered, "Well, not me. But it will be someone ..." The significance? Probably not much, writes Laura Rozen in Politico, noting how one of Clinton's answers is tied to her being secretary of state—a post she likely won't hold in 2016. On another front, Clinton said the midterm losses were "big—but not out of the pattern of historical political elections." She added, "I think the president made very clear was he made decisions which were essential for the well-being of the American people."

**Tyson Foods Faces Public Backlash Over Animal Abuse Allegations**

Tyson Foods, one of the world's largest meat producers, is facing widespread criticism after an undercover investigation by animal advocacy group Compassion Over Killing (COK) revealed graphic footage of mistreatment of chickens at its facilities in Virginia. The video, which was taken by an undercover investigator at four Tyson facilities in May and June, shows employees throwing, punching, and kicking chickens, as well as sticking plastic rods through their beaks, a process known as "boning." The footage also depicts workers wringing birds' necks, running over them with forklifts, and leaving injured birds in "dead piles" to die.

Tyson Foods has since fired 10 employees who were involved in the mistreatment and has discontinued the practice of "boning" at two of the facilities. The company has also announced that it is retraining all of its live poultry workers on its animal welfare policies. Christine Daugherty, vice president of sustainable food production for Tyson Foods, described the employees' actions as "disgusting" and "inexcusable."

COK's executive director, Erica Meier, has stated that the company's actions are not enough and that Tyson needs to be held accountable for the systemic problem of animal abuse. Meier has also questioned whether it is possible to humanely kill and eat animals, suggesting that the best way to protect chickens and other animals is to leave them off our plates.

This is not the first time that Tyson Foods has faced allegations of animal abuse. In the past year, at least five undercover investigations by animal rights groups have revealed similar mistreatment of chickens at Tyson-connected facilities. The company has responded to these allegations by condemning the abuse and stressing that the perpetrators are not reflective of Tyson's institutional commitment to animal welfare.

In response to the latest video, Tyson has announced that it will conduct a video conference with managers at its facilities to discuss the handling of live birds and has begun meeting with every employee in its chicken operations to re-emphasize the importance of proper animal handling. However, COK and other animal advocacy groups remain skeptical of the company's commitment to animal welfare and are calling for greater accountability.

* Tyson Foods has fired 10 employees involved in the mistreatment of chickens
* The company has discontinued the practice of "boning" at two of its facilities
* Tyson is retraining all of its live poultry workers on its animal welfare policies
* COK has made formal complaints about

Animal rights activists have infiltrated Tyson Foods to film such videos at least five times in a little over a year.

**Donald Trump's Campaign Shakeup and Polls: A Summary**

In recent days, Donald Trump's campaign has undergone a significant shake-up, with the appointment of new campaign leaders, including Stephen Bannon as campaign chief executive and Kellyanne Conway as campaign manager. However, Trump's lawyer, Michael Cohen, has been at the forefront of defending the candidate's low ratings in the polls, despite evidence to the contrary.

During an interview on CNN, Cohen was pressed by anchor Brianna Keilar about the campaign's struggling poll numbers. When Keilar asked if the campaign was "down," Cohen replied "Says who?" multiple times, despite Keilar's insistence that polls were showing Trump trailing behind Hillary Clinton.

The exchange quickly went viral on social media, with many users taking to Twitter to mock Cohen's response with the hashtag #SaysWho. Despite the backlash, Cohen remained insistent that the campaign was not "down," and instead praised Conway's expertise and understanding of campaign data.

However, the polls tell a different story. In recent weeks, Hillary Clinton has been leading in most swing states, including Virginia and Colorado, and also edges Trump in Iowa. While Trump did win a poll in Indiana, the Monmouth University poll found him ahead of Clinton by a slim 11-point margin.

The campaign shake-up comes as Trump's campaign staff has been under pressure to improve the candidate's ratings in the polls. The hiring of Bannon and Conway is seen as an effort to revamp the campaign and appeal to a broader audience.

In related news, Twitter users have taken to spamming Cohen's tweets with the phrase "Says who?" in response to his claims that the campaign is not "down." The exchange has sparked a wider conversation about the role of polls in politics and the importance of accurately representing campaign data.

Ultimately, the controversy surrounding Cohen's response to the polls has highlighted the challenges facing Trump's campaign as they struggle to regain momentum in the polls. Despite Cohen's efforts to downplay the issue, the evidence suggests that Trump's campaign still has a long way to go to catch up with Clinton's lead.

"Says who?" he asked. "Polls. Most of them. All of them?" she replied. "Says who?" Cohen asked again. When she repeated it was polls, he responded, "OK, which polls?” and Keilar said: “All of them." "What I meant to say is the problem that exists in the African-American community," he later said. (Michael Moore says he "knows for a fact" that Trump never really wanted to be president.)

**South Carolina Deputy Fired for Violently Removing Student from Class**

A South Carolina sheriff's deputy has been fired after a video of him forcefully removing a student from a classroom went viral online. Senior Deputy Ben Fields, a veteran school resource officer and football coach at Spring Valley High School, was terminated on Wednesday, less than a week after the incident.

The incident occurred on Monday when Fields was called to remove a student who was being disruptive in her algebra class. Cellphone videos taken by students show Fields standing before the student, commanding her to stand up or be forcibly removed. When she refused, Fields body-slammed the student to the ground backwards while she was still in her seat, and then dragged her and her desk across the floor.

The student, who has not been identified, has suffered several injuries, including a cast on her arm, neck and back injuries, and rug burn on her forehead. Her attorney said she was only trying to defuse the situation and that Fields' actions were excessive.

Fields, 34, had been a school resource officer at Spring Valley High School for seven years and had no prior complaints against him. However, an internal investigation by the Richland County Sheriff's Department concluded that his actions were "not acceptable" and that he had failed to follow proper training and procedure.

The incident has sparked outrage and criticism, with many calling for Fields to be charged with assault. The NAACP has also called for Fields to be charged, and the school district has blasted his actions as "outrageous" and "reprehensible."

The FBI, the Justice Department, and state law enforcement have opened separate investigations into the incident, including a civil rights probe. The sheriff, Leon Lott, said that while the student's unruly behavior contributed to the incident, Fields' actions were not justified.

Fields has a history of allegations of racial bias and excessive force, but Lott said that he did not believe race was a factor in this case. However, the incident has raised concerns about the use of force by law enforcement in schools and the need for better training and certification for school resource officers.

The incident is the latest in a series of high-profile cases involving police brutality and excessive force, and has sparked a national conversation about the role of law enforcement in schools and the need for accountability and transparency.

**Key Takeaways:**

* Deputy Ben Fields has been fired for violently removing a student from a classroom.
* The student suffered several injuries, including a cast on her arm, neck and back injuries, and rug
details.ground_truth: The school resource officer suspended after videos of him throwing a black female high-school student around a South Carolina classroom went viral Monday has been fired, CBS News reports. Richland County Sheriff Leon Lott announced the firing of Ben Fields Wednesday after an investigation found his "use of force was unacceptable" and he didn't follow procedure during the arrest. According to NBC News, the decision was made after the sheriff's department looked at videos of the incident and interviewed witnesses. "From the very beginning that's what's caused me to be upset, and continued to upset me, is that he picked the student up and threw the student across the room," Lott says. The Department of Justice is also conducting its own investigation into the incident. This isn't the first time Fields, who is white, has faced accusations of excessive force and racial bias. He's been involved in three other such cases dating back to 2005, and he's scheduled to go to trial over a 2013 racial bias incident in January.

**Pentagon Accidentally Retweets Call for Trump's Resignation, Deletes It Quickly**

In a recent incident, the official Twitter account of the Pentagon, @DeptofDefense, accidentally retweeted a post calling for President Donald Trump's resignation. The tweet, which originated from the account @proudresister, also called for the resignation of Alabama Senate candidate Roy Moore and Senator Al Franken, both of whom have been accused of sexual misconduct. The tweet was quickly deleted by the Pentagon's social media team after realizing the mistake.

According to Pentagon spokesman Col. Rob Manning, an authorized operator of the account "erroneously" retweeted the content, which would not be endorsed by the Department of Defense. The operator caught the error and immediately deleted it. Pentagon press secretary Dana White also tweeted a clarification, stating that the retweet was made "erroneously" and did not reflect the views of the Department of Defense.

This incident highlights the potential risks of social media mistakes, especially for official government accounts. While Twitter users often include disclaimers stating that retweets do not constitute an endorsement, the Pentagon's account does not. The incident also comes at a time when President Trump is facing renewed scrutiny over allegations of sexual misconduct, with several women accusing him of behaving inappropriately towards them.

The Pentagon's mistake is not the first time that President Trump has been called to resign over allegations of sexual misconduct. In recent weeks, several figures in Washington and Hollywood have faced accusations of sexual harassment, forcing the White House to answer lingering questions about the president's behavior. White House press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders has stated that the White House has been "clear" that the women who have accused Trump are lying, but has declined to comment further.

Overall, the Pentagon's accidental retweet serves as a reminder of the importance of social media management, especially for official government accounts. It also highlights the ongoing controversy surrounding President Trump's behavior, which continues to be a topic of discussion in the media and among the public.

In her own tweet, Pentagon spokesperson Dana White said the call for Trump's resignation was "erroneously retweeted" and contained "content that would not be endorsed by the Department of Defense."

The movie "The Lovely Bones" is an adaptation of Alice Sebold's bestselling novel of the same name. The film, directed by Peter Jackson, is based on the story of Susie Salmon, a 14-year-old girl who was murdered by her neighbor, George Harvey, in 1973. The film follows Susie's journey as she watches her family and killer from an afterlife, known as the "in-between."

The movie has received mixed reviews from critics, with some praising Saoirse Ronan's performance as Susie, while others have criticized the film's uneven tone and overly simplistic approach to the story. The film's depiction of heaven, a fantastical and CGI-besotted world, has been particularly criticized for being cheesy and lacking depth.

Stanley Tucci's performance as George Harvey has been praised for its unsettling and malevolent portrayal of the killer. However, his character's presence on screen is often overwhelming, leaving the film feeling unbalanced.

The film's themes of grief, loss, and the power of love have been praised, but some critics have felt that the film shies away from exploring the complexities of the novel, particularly the themes of sex and desire. The film's rating of PG-13 has also been criticized for being overly lenient, given the film's depiction of violent and disturbing content.

Despite its flaws, the film has its moments of beauty and poignancy, particularly in its depiction of Susie's relationships with her family and friends. The film's cinematography and special effects have also been praised for their imaginative and visually stunning portrayal of the afterlife.

The film has been compared to Jackson's earlier work, such as "Heavenly Creatures" and "The Lord of the Rings," but has been criticized for lacking the depth and complexity of those films. Overall, "The Lovely Bones" is a film that has both captivated and disappointed audiences, and its reception will likely be divisive.

 Cast:

* Saoirse Ronan as Susie Salmon
* Stanley Tucci as George Harvey
* Mark Wahlberg as Jack Salmon
* Rachel Weisz as Abigail Salmon
* Susan Sarandon as Grandma Lynn
* Rose McIver as Lindsey Salmon

Rating: PG-13 for mature thematic material involving disturbing violent content and images, and some language

 Runtime: 2 hours, 15 minutes

 Release Date: December 11, 2009
details.ground_truth: Peter Jackson's crafts a fantastic vision of the afterlife in The Lovely Bones, say critics, but some feel it came at the expense of the human side of the story of a murdered teen, adapted from the book by Alice Sebold. "By turns warmly sentimental, serial-killer sinister, and science-fiction fantastical, The Lovely Bones was an unlikely book to achieve worldwide success," Kenneth Turan writes in the Los Angeles Times. "Those mismatched elements come back to haunt" the film version, "making the final product more hit-and-miss than unblemished triumph." "Sebold's Lovely Bones is fleshed out with the perilous, irresistible power of sex—a real world of extramarital sex and sex between young lovers in addition to the heinous rape from which moviegoers are shielded." But Jackson "shies from the challenge, shortchanging a story that isn't only about the lightness of souls in heaven but also about the urges of bodies on earth," writes Lisa Schwarzbaum at Entertainment Weekly. "Through Jackson's art" and the magic of star Saoirse Rogan, Richard Corliss writes at Time, "the obscenity of child murder has been invested with immense gravity and grace." "Some books are not meant to be adapted to the big screen," and The Lovely Bones is one of them, writes Claudia Puig at USA Today. What works in the book, she writes, comes off as "artificial and emotionless on-screen."

**Mexico Confronts Allegations of Military Extrajudicial Killings**

A disturbing video has surfaced, appearing to show a Mexican soldier executing a civilian lying face down in the street in Palmarito, Puebla State. The footage, released by Mexican media outlets, shows the soldier shooting the man in the back of the head as he lies on the ground for six minutes before the fatal shot. The video is allegedly from a security camera recording of a clash between soldiers and suspected oil thieves on May 3, 2017, in which four soldiers and six suspected criminals died.

The Mexican defense ministry has condemned the video, urging federal prosecutors to investigate its authenticity and the events depicted. The ministry stated that soldiers had come under fire from vehicles in Palmarito and had detained some of the attackers, but acknowledged that the video appears to show a soldier shooting a detained man.

This incident is the latest in a series of scandals surrounding the Mexican military's use of extrajudicial force. In 2014, soldiers were implicated in the killing of 22 suspects at a warehouse in Tlatlaya, with the rights commission concluding that at least a dozen suspects were executed after they surrendered.

The video has sparked widespread condemnation, with the Mexican National Human Rights Commission calling for a swift and thorough investigation. The incident has also highlighted Mexico's ongoing struggle with violence and impunity, with the country experiencing a "serious crisis" of gang violence and extrajudicial killings.

According to a report by the International Institute for Strategic Studies, Mexico had the second-highest number of murders in 2016 among countries considered in "armed conflicts." The government has disputed this figure, but the incident is a stark reminder of the challenges facing Mexico in tackling its violent crime problem.

As the investigation into the video continues, concerns are growing about the military's use of force and the need for greater accountability. The incident has sparked widespread outrage, with many calling for a thorough and transparent investigation to determine the facts of the case.
details.ground_truth: A video released online appears to show a Mexican soldier executing a suspected oil thief at point-blank range as he lays defenseless on the ground. The heavily edited video, taken from a surveillance camera, shows a car coming under fire during what the military describes as a series of ambushes that left four soldiers and six suspects dead in Palmarito on May 3. As soldiers surround the vehicle, its occupants exit and are forced to lie down in front of the car. Soldiers are then seen dragging a suspect to the same spot. He appears injured but rolls from his back onto his front. Six minutes later, a soldier appears at the side of the frame and appears to shoot the man in the back of the head, per the AP. A dark stain spreads around him, reports Reuters. "There was already concern about the use of excessive force by the military. Now this video seems to give us the proof," says the chairwoman of Mexico's Senate Commission on Human Rights, per the Washington Post, which identifies the man shot as Raul Jimenez Martinez, 46. Mexico's defense ministry, however, says it will cooperate fully with an investigation launched by the attorney general's office on May 4. "Under no circumstances can conduct contrary to the law and human rights be justified," the ministry says. Residents of Palmarito have already taken to the streets to protest the deaths of the suspects, placing the blame on the army, though the defense ministry previously claimed the suspects used residents as human shields.

**Young Man Falls to Death While Taking Photos at Four Seasons Hotel in Manhattan**

On Wednesday, a 20-year-old man, Connor Cummings, fell to his death while trying to take pictures from the scaffolding of the 52-story Four Seasons Hotel in Manhattan. Cummings, a graduate of Morris Knolls High School and a sophomore at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, was with his 18-year-old friend Dimitri Olivares when the incident occurred.

According to police sources, Cummings and Olivares had taken the hotel elevator to the top floor and then climbed up two flights of stairs to the rooftop exit. They managed to access the restricted rooftop area and climbed up a ladder to get a panoramic shot of the city. However, as Cummings was walking backward on a metal catwalk while snapping photos, he lost his footing and fell 25 feet onto the hotel's roof. He was pronounced dead at the scene.

The police believe that Cummings and Olivares were not guests at the hotel and had accessed the rooftop without permission. Despite the incident, Olivares was released without charges after being questioned by detectives.

Cummings was described as a photography buff who loved taking pictures of the city skyline. His family and friends remembered him as a kind and talented individual who was passionate about his hobbies. A statement from Morris Knolls Principal Ryan MacNaughton praised Cummings as a fine student with a bright future.

The investigation into the incident is ongoing, and hotel officials stated that they are working with police to help in the investigation. The incident has left Cummings' family and friends in shock, and a memorial has been set up in his honor.

In related news, Cummings' fraternity brothers at the University of Massachusetts Amherst have posted a message on Facebook mourning his death, describing his "fiery passion" for photography and his commitment to helping others. A childhood friend of Cummings, Mike Pascale, also spoke out about his friend's kind and down-to-earth nature.

The incident has raised questions about the safety of the hotel's rooftop and the need for greater security measures to prevent such incidents in the future.
details.ground_truth: Connor Cummins' family describes him to WABC-TV as a photography devotee—and that enthusiasm may have cost him his life. The New Jersey man, said to be either 20 or 24 years old, and an 18-year-old pal ascended the scaffolding outside NYC's Four Seasons hotel Wednesday night in search of the perfect panoramic picture—but Cummins may have slipped on railings made slippery by the rain and fell nine stories to his death, cops tell NJ.com. An NYPD spokesman tells the New York Daily News that the initial investigation suggests the men weren't guests of the hotel. Cummins, who originally hails from Rockaway and whose Facebook page indicates he's now a student at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, per ABC, landed on one of the hotel's lower-level rooftops around 11:15pm and was pronounced dead at the scene, reports the New York Post. His friend wasn't injured and was questioned by authorities to fill in the blanks; he was released Thursday morning without being charged. "He was a loving boy, that's all I can say," one of Cummins' relatives tells the Post. "I don't know what happened." (There's more suspicion surrounding a Muslim teen's fatal fall off a Seattle building.)

**Truckers' Protest in Washington, D.C.**

A group of truck drivers, known as "Truckers for the Constitution," is planning a three-day protest in Washington, D.C. to express their frustration with federal regulations and the overall political climate in the U.S. The protest, dubbed "Truckers Ride for the Constitution," aims to clog the Capital Beltway and draw attention to the truckers' grievances, including low wages, high fuel costs, and excessive regulations.

According to Earl Conlon, a Georgia trucker handling logistics for the protest, the truckers will intentionally clog the inner loop of the Washington, D.C., beltway beginning on October 11. Conlon stated that the trucks will circle the beltway "three lanes deep" and keep the left lane open for emergency vehicles. The protesters are planning to keep the traffic flowing at the 55 mile-per-hour speed limit.

The truckers are unhappy with the Obama administration's regulations, including Environmental Protection Agency fuel efficiency standards, and the high cost of diesel fuel. They are also demanding the impeachment of President Barack Obama and the arrest of members of Congress who they believe have violated their oath of office.

However, Conlon clarified that the truckers are not seeking impeachment, but rather the arrest of those who have committed "treason" by allegedly funneling weapons to al-Qaida-linked rebels in Syria. He identified House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi and Sen. Dianne Feinstein as politicians he will seek to arrest for alleged acts against the Constitution.

The protest has gained significant attention, with close to 50,000 people liking a Facebook page advertising the event. However, it remains to be seen whether the protest will have a significant impact on D.C. area traffic.

In an update, it is reported that the truckers are no longer planning to arrest members of Congress. Instead, they will be seeking the arrest of those who have committed "treason" through a citizens grand jury. The protesters are hoping to draw attention to their grievances and to spark a movement to restore the U.S. Constitution.

**Key Points:**

* Truckers are planning a three-day protest in Washington, D.C. to express their frustration with federal regulations and the overall political climate in the U.S.
* The protest aims to clog the Capital Beltway and draw attention to the truckers' grievances, including low wages, high fuel costs, and excessive regulations.
* The truckers are unhappy with the Obama administration's regulations and the high cost of diesel fuel.
details.ground_truth: Protesting truckers are planning a shutdown of their own—on Washington's beltway. Starting on Friday morning, they intend to fill the road "three lanes deep" with tractor-trailers, an organizer of "Truckers Ride for the Constitution" tells US News and World Report. In a demonstration against what they view as violations of the Constitution, planners like Earl Conlon are calling for "the arrest of everyone in government who has violated their oath of office." Their grievances include truckers' wages, industry regulations, and NSA spying, the Hill reports. They're also accusing President Obama of treason for allegedly putting weapons in the hands of rebels with ties to al-Qaeda. Organizers have received some 3,000 RSVPs, says one. Moving at the 55mph speed limit, they'll leave a lane open for emergency vehicles; vehicles with "T2SDA," or "Truckers to Shut Down America," displayed will also be allowed to pass. "Everybody that doesn't have a supporter sticker on their window, good luck: Nobody in, nobody out," says Conlon. A Facebook page for the event has more than 55,000 "likes."

**Secret Service Scandal: Agents and Supervisors Fired Over Prostitution Incident**

A scandal has rocked the Secret Service after 11 agents and uniformed officers involving the agency's protective detail were accused of misconduct in Cartagena, Colombia, ahead of President Barack Obama's visit to the Summit of the Americas. The incident, which has sparked outrage and calls for accountability, has led to the firing of three employees, including two senior supervisors, and the suspension of eight others. Their top-secret security clearances have been revoked.

According to investigators, the employees involved in the scandal brought prostitutes to their hotel rooms, engaged in heavy drinking, and visited a strip club. The incident became public after a dispute over payment between one of the employees and a prostitute drew the attention of hotel staff and Colombian authorities.

**Supervisors' Involvement**

Two senior supervisors, David Randall Chaney and Greg Stokes, were among those fired. Chaney, a 48-year-old supervisor in the international programs division, was accused of making light of his official protective work on his Facebook page, joking about a picture of himself standing watch behind Sarah Palin, the 2008 Republican vice presidential nominee. Chaney's comment, "I was really checking her out, if you know what I mean?" was seen as insensitive and unprofessional.

Stokes, the assistant special agent in charge of the K-9 division, was also fired. Both men have worked at the Secret Service for nearly two decades and have served significant time with the presidential protection detail.

**Reaction from Politicians**

The scandal has sparked a heated debate among politicians, with some calling for more firings and resignations. Rep. Peter King (R-N.Y.) said he expected more dismissals and firings in the case, while Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid suggested that the problem lies with the judgment of those involved. President Obama's chief spokesman, Jay Carney, expressed confidence in the director of the Secret Service and asked for patience as official investigations continue.

**Palin's Response**

Sarah Palin, the 2008 Republican vice presidential nominee, responded to the scandal by saying she was disgusted that a Secret Service agent would make jokes about checking out her "backside" and called his behavior "pretty embarrassing." Palin also suggested that the scandal was emblematic of President Obama's poor management.

**Investigation**

The Secret Service has launched a formal investigation into the incident, and agency investigators in Colombia have visited all of the hotels where Secret Service personnel stayed and interviewed each of the maids who cleaned rooms
details.ground_truth: One of the two Secret Service supervisors to lose their jobs so far over the Colombia sex scandal joked that he was checking out Sarah Palin as he guarded her during the 2008 campaign, the Washington Post finds. Senior agent David Chaney posted at least two pictures of himself with Palin on Facebook, one with the comment, "I was really checking her out, if you know what I mean." Chaney has been forced to resign, and another supervisor has been informed that he will be fired. In an interview with Fox News, Palin said the joke is on Chaney. "Well check this out, buddy—you're fired!" she said, calling the scandal "a symptom of government run amok," reports AP. "It's like, who's minding the store around here?" she added. "The president, for one, he better be wary, there, of when Secret Service is accompanying his family on vacation. They may be checking out the first lady instead of guarding her."
