**The Evolution of The Daily Show**

The Daily Show, a satirical news program, has undergone significant changes since its inception. In February 1996, the show premiered on Comedy Central, hosted by Craig Kilborn, with a format that loosely followed a conventional newscast. However, after Kilborn left the show in 1996, Jon Stewart took over as host in 1999, bringing a new direction and sensibility to the program.

Initially, Stewart faced resistance from the writing staff, who were accustomed to Kilborn's style. However, Stewart was determined to create a show that was pointed, purposeful, and intentional, with a focus on satire and social commentary. He brought in new writers, including Ben Karlin and David Javerbaum, who shared his vision for the show.

One of the key moments in The Daily Show's evolution came when Stewart and his team began to focus on the media's coverage of politics. They started to attend campaign events and press conferences, but instead of trying to fit in with the traditional media, they chose to mock and satirize them. This approach paid off when Steve Carell asked Senator John McCain a pointed question on his bus during the 2000 presidential campaign, forcing McCain to go off-script and revealing the disconnect between the media's coverage and the actual politics.

The Daily Show's correspondents, including Carell, Stephen Colbert, and Mo Rocca, played a crucial role in shaping the show's tone and style. They developed their own characters and approaches, often making fun of journalistic tropes and the self-seriousness of the media. The show's writers and correspondents also began to focus on the process behind politics, rather than just covering the surface-level events.

Under Stewart's leadership, The Daily Show became a cultural touchstone, known for its biting satire and irreverent style. The show's impact was felt not only on television but also in the wider cultural conversation, with many politicians and journalists taking notice of its influence.

Today, The Daily Show continues to be a leading voice in satirical news, with a new host, Trevor Noah, at the helm. The show's legacy is a testament to the power of satire and social commentary, and its impact on the media and politics continues to be felt.
details.ground_truth: Jon Stewart took over as host of the Daily Show in January 1999, but when did the program truly start becoming the Daily Show that legions of fans would come to love? Try December of that year, when Steve Carell boarded John McCain's campaign bus "and changed the entire trajectory" of the show, writes Chris Smith at Vanity Fair. The article is a fascinating oral history of the show's beginning that includes interviews with Stewart, Carell, Stephen Colbert, and pretty much everyone involved. The first year was rough for Stewart as he fought with the holdover writing staff from previous host Craig Kilborn and sought to put his stamp on the show. Things came together in that Carell interview, in which he peppers McCain with softball questions, then springs a policy zinger. After a few seconds of awkward silence, Carell says, "I was just kidding! I don’t even know what that means!" And the tense moment subsides. (See the clip.) Carell: "It was making fun of a gotcha moment. And I think that a lot of what we do on The Daily Show is making fun of journalistic tropes, and I think that was one of them." Head writer Ben Karlin: "I remember seeing it in the editing room. I remember Jon called me down, and seeing it and thinking, Yeah, this is what we should be doing. This is the goal. It was one of Carell’s most incredible moments. He asks McCain a question in a way that no journalists were talking to the candidates. And it was like, Oh s---, we are able, in this weird, unintentional way, to add a level of insight to the process that doesn’t exist. That was really, really exciting." Click for the full piece, in which Stewart talks about how that 2000 campaign provided his "aha moment" of how to present the show—by "deconstructing the process."

**Quick Thinking 911 Dispatcher Saves Woman Trapped in Car Trunk**

A Johnston County, North Carolina, 911 dispatcher's quick thinking and resourcefulness may have saved a woman's life after she was locked in the trunk of a moving car. On January 14, 2017, around 4 a.m., 911 Shift Supervisor Tim Medlin received a 911 cell phone call from a woman who reported being kidnapped from Raleigh and trapped in the trunk of a car traveling down I-40. Unfortunately, the call was lost after the woman provided limited information.

Undeterred, Medlin used the 911 center's emergency cellphone to text the woman, who responded, providing her boyfriend's name, Nicholas Mattevi, and the car's make and color. With this information, Medlin was able to locate the phone and provide the location to the Fayetteville Police Department. Mattevi was arrested and charged with false imprisonment.

The woman was rescued unharmed, and her boyfriend was released on a $10,000 bond. The incident highlights the importance of technology in emergency situations and the need for 911 centers to be able to initiate texts from their systems. As a result of the incident, Johnston County's 911 director, Jason Barbour, plans to purchase smartphones for the team to facilitate communication in similar situations.

Medlin's quick thinking and resourcefulness undoubtedly saved the woman's life, and his actions have set a high standard for emergency responders. As Barbour noted, "This is a prime example of technology being used to the fullest at someone's greatest time of need."
details.ground_truth: A North Carolina woman allegedly locked in the trunk of a moving car by her boyfriend was saved by a quick-thinking 911 dispatcher and a lowly flip phone, ABC News reports. The 29-year-old woman called 911 around 4am Jan. 14. The woman had gotten in a fight with her boyfriend, who was "jealous," according to WRAL. Before dispatcher Tim Medlin could get much information from the woman, the call was dropped. He was unable to get her to answer when he called back. Then Medlin remembered a flip phone the 911 communications center keeps around in case the phone lines go down, WTVD reports. He says texting was the only way he could think to contact her without getting "her in trouble." The woman texted Medlin back, letting the dispatcher know she was in a red Mustang headed toward Fayetteville. Verizon provided the location of the woman's phone, and police stopped her and her boyfriend, Nicholas Mattevi, in Fayetteville. Mattevi, 32, was arrested and charged with false imprisonment. "I can go to bed," Medlin recalls feeling upon hearing of the arrest. "We've done our job." He says he's planning to buy a smartphone for the office to replace the old flip phone and make it easier to text if there's a next time. Medlin's boss tells ABC the dispatcher "really stood by our motto: Failure is not an option." (This 911 dispatcher was accused of hanging up on callers.)

**Preserving the Web's Integrity: Internet Archive's Effort**

In a bid to maintain the integrity of the web, the Internet Archive has initiated a massive project to archive web pages as they are created and preserve the pages they reference. This effort aims to prevent broken links from occurring on the web by archiving the versions of these pages that were live when the content was written. The Internet Archive hopes that by doing so, references to archived pages will replace broken links, allowing users to access the original content intended by the authors.

**Tragic Loss of Law Enforcement Officer**

In a separate incident, a Modoc County Sheriff's Deputy was tragically killed in the line of duty. Deputy Jack Hopkins, 42, was responding to a disturbance call in Modoc County, California, when he was confronted and shot by 47-year-old Jack Lee Breiner. The shooting took place on October 19, and Deputy Hopkins was killed instantly. Breiner engaged another deputy in a gunbattle and was subsequently shot and wounded. He has been charged with murder and other crimes related to the killing. The incident is a sobering reminder of the risks and sacrifices made by law enforcement officers every day.

**Key Takeaways:**

- The Internet Archive is working to preserve web pages to prevent broken links.
- A Modoc County Sheriff's Deputy was killed in the line of duty on October 19.
- The suspect, Jack Lee Breiner, has been charged with murder and other crimes related to the killing.
details.ground_truth: Police in Stanislaus County, Calif., say a 20-year veteran of the force was "executed" early Sunday after investigating a stolen van and a suspicious person outside Modesto. Sheriff's Deputy Dennis Wallace was shot twice in the head. Police say a suspect was arrested more than 150 miles away later in the day after allegedly carrying out a carjacking, an armed robbery at a convenience store, and an attempted purse-snatching, the Los Angeles Times reports. David Machado, a 37-year-old described by police as a "known criminal," was connected to the Wallace killing after police in Tulane County recognized his tattoos. Wallace, who was married with children, "was executed," Sheriff Adam Christianson told reporters. "We believe that Dep. Wallace was killed outside of the car and we know for a fact that the gun used in this crime was in direct contact with his head when the trigger was pulled twice." He said Wallace, 53, had been involved in programs including youth soccer and early intervention, NBC News reports. "We need to keep the Wallace family in our thoughts and prayers. We need to keep law enforcement in our thoughts and prayers," he said. The Bradenton Herald reports after a knee injury in 2007, Wallace was off for two years without pay and fought a long legal battle to get his job back.

details.output:
Lauren Bacall, the legendary actress who passed away at the age of 89, left behind a significant estate worth approximately $26.6 million. 

The majority of her estate, including her apartment in the Dakota building and its contents, will be split among her three children: Leslie Bogart, Stephen Bogart, and Sam Robards. Bacall's children, who were also named as executors of her estate, will benefit from her estate in various ways. Sam Robards, who cared for her beloved dog Sophie, was given $10,000 specifically for the pup's care. 

In addition to the $10,000 bequest for Sophie, Bacall left $250,000 to each of her six grandchildren for college, with the stipulation that they wouldn't have access to the funds until they turn 30. She also showed kindness to her employees, leaving $20,000 to Maria Santos and $15,000 to Ilsa Hernandez, who worked as her maid for 14 years.

Bacall's will also included a provision to keep certain personal papers, letters, and diaries private. The actress, who won two Tony Awards, a Golden Globe, and an honorary Oscar throughout her eight-decade career, had a long and storied life, marked by her high-profile relationships with Humphrey Bogart and Jason Robards.

Bacall's generosity towards her family, employees, and even her beloved dog, Sophie, is a testament to her kind spirit. Her estate will be auctioned off this fall, and her artwork will be sold to benefit her loved ones.
details.ground_truth: Papillion Sophie has lived a pampered life, but that doesn't have to end now that her famous owner—Hollywood legend Lauren Bacall—has died. According to estate papers filed last week, Bacall left $10,000 to her son Sam Robards for Sophie's care and upkeep, Page Six reports. This bequeath isn't a surprise, considering Bacall called herself a "dog yearner" in a 2008 interview, Us reports. "I didn't have a dog growing up in the city with a working mother. As an only child, I yearned for someone to talk to," she said. She gave Sophie quite a glitzy life attending book signings and film festivals. Bacall didn't ignore her human family: The New York Daily News reports her $26.6 million estate was mostly split among her three children; her grandkids reportedly got $250,000 each. And two of her employees also received $20,000 and $15,000, respectively. Considering other famous four-legged heirs, Sophie's 10 grand isn't shocking: Leona Helmsley left $12 million to her Maltese and Alexander McQueen left $82,000 to his three dogs. But the oddest may be Dusty Springfield, who gave orders that her cat be serenaded with her own music and "marry" another cat.

**US Politics Update**

The current US presidential election and the Obama administration's policies have been making headlines this weekend.

**Presidential Election Update**

The Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) presidential straw poll was held last weekend, with Mitt Romney emerging as the winner, beating Rick Santorum 38 to 31 percent. However, Santorum has questioned the legitimacy of the poll, accusing Romney's campaign of buying tickets for supporters to vote. Romney's campaign has denied these allegations, calling Santorum's statement "not grounded in the truth." 

Ron Paul has also expressed surprise at Romney's description of himself as a "severe conservative," saying that he is unsure what Romney means by this term and that Romney will have to explain himself.

**Contraception Controversy**

In a separate development, the Obama administration has made a move to address the controversy surrounding birth control and Catholic institutions. White House Chief of Staff Jack Lew stated that President Obama's position on the issue has been consistent and that his recent "accommodation" is as far as he will go to win over Catholic bishops. Lew explained that health insurers would actually pay less for birth control-inclusive plans, as they reduce pregnancies and therefore lower healthcare costs.

**Key Takeaways**

- Mitt Romney won the CPAC presidential straw poll, but Rick Santorum has questioned the legitimacy of the poll.
- Romney's campaign has denied allegations of buying tickets for supporters to vote.
- Ron Paul has expressed surprise at Romney's description of himself as a "severe conservative."
- The Obama administration has made a move to address the controversy surrounding birth control and Catholic institutions.
- White House Chief of Staff Jack Lew has stated that President Obama's position on the issue has been consistent and that his recent "accommodation" is as far as he will go to win over Catholic bishops.
details.ground_truth: Fresh off his loss to Mitt Romney at CPAC, Rick Santorum hit the talk shows this morning, and he's downplaying the victory, reports Politico: "You have to talk to the Romney campaign and how many tickets they bought—we've heard all sorts of things," Santorum told CNN. "Ron Paul has won those (straw polls) because he trucks in a lot of people, pays for their ticket, and they come in and vote. I don't try to rig straw polls." Elsewhere on the Sunday dial, as per Politico: Santorum on his chances: "I think we can do well (in Michigan). I think we can do reasonably well in Arizona, and really make this a two-person race." Sarah Palin on Romney: He's "a great candidate," but "his idea of conservatism is evolving, and I base this on a pretty moderate past that he has had, even in some cases a liberal past. Now that’s a problem. He’s still in the 30 percentile mark ... because we are not convinced." Obama chief of staff Jack Lew on the birth control ruckus: The Friday "accommodation" is as far as the White House will go. "There are others who don't have the same objective, they will have to speak for themselves, (but) this is our plan." Ron Paul on 'severe conservative' Romney: "That was the first time I’ve heard that definition, so I guess Mitt will have to tell us exactly what it means. Obviously, he means he’s a serious conservative, and he was trying to defend himself, or portray himself as such, but I don’t know exactly what he was meaning by that."

**HGTV's Fixer Upper Stars Chip and Joanna Gaines Reach Settlement with EPA Over Lead-Based Paint Violations**

Chip and Joanna Gaines, the popular HGTV couple behind the hit show Fixer Upper, have reached a settlement with the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) over allegations of violating lead-based paint regulations during their home renovation projects.

According to the EPA, Magnolia Homes, the Gaines' company, was found to have conducted home renovations without adequate lead paint protections in 33 different properties in Waco, Texas. The agency alleges that the company failed to comply with several requirements of the Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA) Lead Renovation, Repair and Painting Rule (RRP Rule), including obtaining certification, assigning a certified renovator, and providing homeowners with an approved pamphlet about lead-based paint hazards.

As part of the settlement, Magnolia Homes will pay a $40,000 fine to the EPA. In addition to the fine, the company has agreed to:

*   Produce an educational video highlighting the dangers of lead-based paint, starring Chip Gaines, to be released within 90 days.
*   Spend $160,000 on a lead-abatement project in Waco, Texas, to reduce the risk of exposure to lead-based paint in at-risk residences.
*   Implement a compliance management program to ensure that the company's future renovation projects comply with lead-based paint regulations.
*   Take steps to educate the public about the hazards of lead-based paint and the precautions necessary to reduce those hazards, including through social media and website content.

The EPA notes that Magnolia Homes has taken steps to address the lead-based paint violations, including obtaining certification, training, and renovator certification for staff, and bringing active renovations into compliance. The company has also cooperated with the EPA throughout the investigation.

The settlement is seen as a positive step towards addressing the issue of lead-based paint hazards in older homes, which can have serious health consequences for young children and others. The EPA enforces the RRP Rule to protect children and others who are vulnerable to exposure to lead dust that can cause lead poisoning.

**Background on Lead-Based Paint Regulations**

Lead-based paint was banned in 1978, but it still remains in many homes and apartments across the country. According to the EPA, an estimated 37.1 million homes in the United States contain lead-based paint, with 34.4 million of these homes built before 1978. Lead dust can occur when lead paint deteriorates or is disrupted during home renovation and remodeling activities,
details.ground_truth: Fixer Upper stars Chip and Joanna Gaines are having to pay up for EPA violations seen on their hit HGTV show. The couple's business, Magnolia Homes, has agreed to pay a $40,000 fine after the EPA determined 33 of its home renovations were "conducted without adequate lead paint protections," reports Deadline, which notes the potential fine the company faced was as high as $556,000. The EPA notes Fixer Upper footage "reviewed by EPA did not depict the lead-safe work practices normally required" though "Magnolia took immediate steps to ensure compliance" when it was notified. Chip Gaines, who in March tweeted advice about engaging a professional to check for lead paint when buying or remodeling a home, will also star in a Magnolia-paid video on lead paint safety as part of the settlement, per Vulture. (The couple are expecting their fifth child.)

**Ancient Human Innovations: Shifting Paradigms of Human Evolution**

Recent archaeological discoveries in Kenya's Olorgesailie Basin have sent shockwaves through the scientific community, challenging long-held assumptions about human evolution. The findings, published in three studies in the journal Science, suggest that ancient humans in Africa were far more advanced and resourceful than previously thought.

**Prior to 320,000 years ago**

During this period, early humans in Africa used large, bulky stone tools, characteristic of the Acheulean (Early Stone Age) culture. These tools were typically made from local rock and were used for tasks such as chopping down branches and scavenging carcasses. The climate was characterized by frequent shifts between wet and dry conditions, which likely drove the development of these tools.

**320,000 years ago: Technological Revolution**

Around 320,000 years ago, a significant technological shift occurred, marking the beginning of the Middle Stone Age. This period saw the introduction of smaller, more specialized tools, made from obsidian and other exotic materials that were transported from great distances. The tools were designed for specific purposes, such as hunting and gathering, and were characterized by greater diversity and complexity.

**Complex Social Networks and Exchange**

The discovery of obsidian tools and pigment lumps, including red and black rocks that were drilled into, suggests that ancient humans were engaging in complex social networks and exchange. This exchange was likely driven by the need for cooperation and information sharing in response to environmental stressors such as droughts and earthquakes.

**Rethinking the Human Revolution**

The findings at Olorgesailie challenge the traditional view of the "human revolution" that occurred around 40,000-50,000 years ago. This view posited that modern human behaviors such as symbolism, innovation, and art emerged suddenly in Africa and were linked to the appearance of anatomically modern humans. However, the discoveries at Olorgesailie suggest that these behaviors were present much earlier, in groups that predated our own species.

**Implications for Human Evolution**

The findings at Olorgesailie have significant implications for our understanding of human evolution. They suggest that human evolution was a gradual process that involved the development of complex social networks, technological innovation, and cultural exchange. This view is more consistent with the diversity of human fossils and tools found across Africa, which suggest that multiple human species coexisted and interacted over time.

In conclusion, the discoveries at Olorgesailie have challenged long-held assumptions about human evolution and have shed new light
details.ground_truth: For decades, the human story was one told through signs of modernity—art, tools, burials—found only after Homo sapiens left Africa. Recent discoveries pushing back the date of departure are helping to change that narrative, as are three new studies in Science. Together, they describe the earliest stone tools of their kind in East Africa as well as the earliest evidence for long-distance transport of raw materials in the region, per Science News. Researchers previously believed the Early Stone Age marked by large, primitive tools (hand axes and cleavers) made way for the Middle Stone Age and its smaller tools (spearpoints and blades) around 280,000 years ago. But stone tools found in Kenya's Olorgesailie Basin show hominin groups—it's not clear which species is responsible—had developed these more advanced tools approximately 320,000 years ago. "We see a smaller technology, a more diverse series of stone tools … designed for specific purposes," researcher Rick Potts tells NPR. An expert not involved in the study says the tools are so "prepared and retouched" they suggest the Middle Stone Age likely started even earlier. What drove it? Microscopic and chemical analyses of the region's soil show the landscape was rapidly shifting between wet and dry conditions, meaning hominins might have been forced to adapt. But as the technology also coincides with the emergence of Homo sapiens in Africa, it's possible that the human mind deserves credit. The tools themselves suggest the development of trading networks, too. Some were made of rocks carried from 55 miles away, says Potts. Others were made of obsidian from as far as 30 miles away, per the Conversation. (These stone tools are also making waves.)

The Lowell Thomas Travel Journalism Competition, a prestigious award in the field of travel journalism, announced its winners for 2010. Andrew McCarthy, a freelance writer, was named the 2010 Travel Journalist of the Year for his excellent writing and poignant stories in various travel magazines.

Meanwhile, in Ireland, the economic crisis continues to affect the country. In a personal account, actor and writer Andrew McCarthy visited Doolin, a small village in County Clare, to see how the recession was impacting the local community. Despite the economic struggles, the village remains a hub for traditional Irish music, with musicians from all over the world gathering in the local pubs to play and sing.

The village's residents, however, are feeling the effects of the recession, with many struggling to make ends meet. As one local said, "The party's over and the hangover is feckin' brutal." Despite the challenges, the community remains resilient, and the music scene continues to thrive.

In other news, the New Vineyard Gazette has been sold to Jerome and Nancy Kohlberg, with Richard Reston, the paper's publisher since 1988, retiring. Peter Wallsten has been hired as a new journalist, praised for his ability to discover hidden gems and talk to untalked-to people. The California Supreme Court also ruled that SF Weekly must pay $21 million in damages for trying to drive the rival Bay Guardian out of business.

Andrea Mitchell, a renowned journalist, has been awarded the Sol Taishoff Award for Excellence in Broadcast Journalism, with the judges praising her as a great reporter and commentator who regularly breaks important Washington and national stories.

**Key Takeaways:**

* The Lowell Thomas Travel Journalism Competition announced its winners for 2010, with Afar Magazine, Washington Post, and San Francisco Chronicle taking top honors.
* Andrew McCarthy was named the 2010 Travel Journalist of the Year for his excellent writing and poignant stories.
* The economic crisis in Ireland continues to affect the country, with communities like Doolin struggling to make ends meet.
* The village of Doolin remains a hub for traditional Irish music, despite the economic challenges.
* The New Vineyard Gazette has been sold to Jerome and
details.ground_truth: The year's top travel writer is a familiar face, but more so for acting than writing. Andrew McCarthy of Brat Pack fame (yes, Molly Ringwald's true love in Pretty in Pink) has won the 2010 Lowell Thomas award for Travel Journalist of the Year, notes Jim Romenesko's blog at PoynterOnline. McCarthy's freelance articles have appeared in National Geographic Traveler, Bon Appetit, Afar, and the Atlantic, among others. (His piece on Ireland in the latter is here.) "Word by word, paragraph-by-paragraph, entry-by-entry, McCarthy earns the gold the old-fashioned way—by treating readers to excellent writing and poignant stories," declared the judges.

Donald Trump, the Republican presidential candidate, has been speaking about firearms and mass shootings in the US. In a recent rally in Tennessee, Trump defended Second Amendment rights and argued that mass shootings are caused by mental illness, rather than access to guns. He stated that he has a handgun carry permit in New York and would respond if someone attacked him, comparing himself to Charles Bronson in the vigilante film "Death Wish."

Trump's comments on Saturday were his most extensive about firearms since he launched his campaign in June. He claimed that if teachers had been armed at Umpqua Community College in Oregon, where a gunman killed nine people, the shooting would not have been as tragic. He also criticized "gun-free zones" and said that better mental health care would help curb future shootings.

However, Trump's positions on gun control have evolved significantly over the years. While he now touts the National Rifle Association line, he once backed the ban on assault weapons and longer waiting periods for gun purchases. Trump also criticized President Barack Obama's comments in response to the shooting as "divisive."

In a sit-down interview with Chuck Todd on NBC's Meet the Press, Trump seemed to accept the inevitability of mass shootings in the US. He stated that "no matter what you do, you're gonna have problems" due to the presence of "sick people" who are intelligent and able to break the system. Trump also pointed out that US jurisdictions with the strongest gun control laws are often the worst places, suggesting that stricter gun laws do not work.

Instead, Trump blamed mental illness for mass shootings and argued that increased firearms regulations are not necessary. He also defended his newly introduced tax plan, which independent economists estimate will cost $12tn over 10 years, by pointing to his ability to cut costs.
details.ground_truth: Mass shootings in America aren't a gun problem, they're a mental health problem, according to Donald Trump. He told a rally in Nashville on Saturday that "it's not the guns, it's the people. It's these sick people," the AP reports. "Many states and many cities are closing their mental health facilities and closing them down, and they're closing them because they don't have the funding. And we have to start looking much stronger into mental health," he said. On NBC's Meet the Press on Sunday, Trump explained that gun control wouldn't help because "they can be sick as hell and they’re geniuses in a certain way. They are going to be able to break the system.” He also told NBC that to avoid copycat killings, the media should have followed the local sheriff's advice and refused to name the gunman after the mass shooting in Oregon. At the Nashville rally, Trump spoke against "gun-free zones," saying people "would have been a hell of a lot better off" if there had been "teachers with guns in that room" during the Oregon shooting, and said that since he has a handgun carry permit in New York, anyone who attacks him is in for a surprise, CNN reports. He brought up Charles Bronson's Death Wish, got the audience to chant the name of the movie, and said: "Today you can’t make that movie because it’s not politically correct," reports the Guardian. (Jeb Bush took a lot of heat for saying "Stuff happens" after the Oregon shooting.)

**Kanye West's Financial Woes: A GoFundMe Campaign and a Journey of Self-Discovery**

Rapper, fashion designer, and celebrity Kanye West has been making headlines in recent days with his financial struggles. In a bizarre Twitter rant, West claimed he is $53 million in debt and publicly begged tech billionaires Mark Zuckerberg and Larry Page for financial help. West's plea for funding has sparked a GoFundMe campaign, "Get Kanye Out of Debt," created by Jeremy Piatt, a Kanye fan from Minnesota.

Piatt's campaign aims to raise awareness for supporting the arts and to help West, whom he considers a great artist. Despite the initial enthusiasm, the campaign has garnered only $50 in pledges, a mere 0.000094 percent of West's total debt. Piatt's campaign is not the only one; Robert Kulik from Washington, D.C. also started a GoFundMe campaign, "HELP PAY KANYE'S DEBT!", which, however, appears to have been canceled.

West's financial struggles are not new; in 2016, he similarly claimed to be $53 million in debt and made a public plea for help. However, this time around, his Twitter rant has drawn attention from Silicon Valley, with Box CEO Aaron Levie commenting on West's "classic Series A pitch deck line."

West's financial woes have also led to a controversy surrounding his latest album, "The Life of Pablo," which was released on the struggling music streaming app Tidal. The album was pulled down shortly after its release, and it has reportedly been illegally downloaded over 500,000 times from other sites.

Meanwhile, pop star Taylor Swift has responded to West's claims of her dependence on him, saying that young women should focus on their work and not let others try to undercut their success.

**Kanye's Relationships with Tech Executives**

West's connections with tech executives have been well-documented. He has been known to frequent Apple's southern California office, where he has met with former record executive Jimmy Iovine. West has also been friends with venture capitalist Ben Horowitz, who has expressed admiration for West's entrepreneurial spirit.

West's startup idea, Donda, has been pitched to investors, including Alphabet CEO Larry Page. However, it is unclear whether West's financial struggles are a result of his entrepreneurial ventures or his personal spending habits.

The Kanye West GoFundMe campaign has sparked a discussion about crowdfunding and the role it plays in supporting artists. However, it
details.ground_truth: Kanye West is, apparently, $53 million in debt, but he should be able to pay that off in no time thanks to a GoFundMe campaign started by 36-year-old Minnesotan Jeremy Piatt, CNET reports. "Recently, Kanye let us in on his personal struggle. He is 53 million dollars in debt and it doesn't look like he's going to get Mark Zuckerberg's help that he desperately needs," Piatt writes. "We must open our hearts and wallets for Kanye today. Sure he is personally rich and can buy furs and houses for his family, but without our help, the true genius of Kanye West can't be realized." So far, after just a single day of fundraising, $603 has been raised for Kanye (along with a lot of comments like, "How about you get a job?"). Piatt tells Mashable that he's spoken to GoFundMe, and only Kanye or his team will be able to access any money raised. As for Zuckerberg—West specifically asked him, as well as Larry Page, to donate money to the Kanye cause when he went on his Twitter rant, and then went on to imply that he knows other tech giants who should help him. "All you dudes in San Fran play rap music in your homes but never help the real artists…," he tweeted, followed by, "All you guys had meetings with me and no one lifted a finger to help…." The Guardian did a little investigation into how many "tech guys" Kanye actually knows, and found that he really does have something of an ongoing relationship with Facebook and Apple; has had meetings with investors in Silicon Valley and even had some of them at one of his parties; has had interactions with an Instagram co-founder, the Dropbox chief executive, a co-founder of Rap Genius, and Elon Musk; and is good friends with venture capitalist Ben Horowitz. (Here are some of the dumbest GoFundMe campaigns ever.)

**Search for Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 Enters Intensive Phase**

The search for Malaysia Airlines Flight 370, which disappeared nearly two years ago with 239 people on board, has entered its most intensive phase as authorities scramble to find any clues about the plane's fate. A Chinese ship, Haixun 01, has picked up a pulse signal in the southern Indian Ocean, which has raised hopes that the search team may finally find the missing plane. The signal, which has a frequency of 37.5 kHz, is the same as those emitted by the flight recorders.

**Signal Detection**

The signal was detected on Saturday by the Haixun 01 at about 25 degrees south latitude and 101 degrees east longitude. However, there is no evidence yet that it is linked to MH370. The head of the Australian agency co-ordinating the search, Angus Houston, said that the reported signals "are consistent with the aircraft black box" but "there is no confirmation at this stage that the signals and the objects are related to the missing aircraft."

**Intensive Search**

Dozens of ships and planes have joined the search, with the operation moving into its most intensive phase before batteries on the data recorders fade. The Australian naval vessel Ocean Shield is using a "towed pinger locator" from the US Navy, while HMS Echo, which has similar capabilities, is also searching. They are trying to detect an underwater signal emitted by the data recorders, which fade after 30 days.

**Search Area**

The search area is about 217,000 sq km (84,000 sq miles) in size, with the Haixun 01 searching a location about 1,700km (1,000 miles) north-west of Perth. The area was picked on the basis of analysis of the satellite data.

**Hope and Caution**

While the signal detection has raised hopes that the search team may finally find the missing plane, experts have cautioned that caution is necessary. There have been many apparently promising leads over the past few weeks that have looked like the search team closing in, only for those leads to turn into nothing. Oceanographer Simon Boxall said that "if this is the pulse, this becomes a viable search," but added that it could be a false signal.

**Background**

Flight 370 is believed to have crashed in the southern Indian Ocean, although no confirmed debris has been found. The plane diverted from its planned flight path from Kuala Lumpur to Beijing four weeks ago with 239 people on board
details.ground_truth: Could it be? A Chinese ship searching for Flight 370 has picked up a ping in the southern Indian Ocean, reports CNN and the BBC. Both cite a report in China's Xinhua news agency. The pulse is 37.5 kHz, the frequency for the Malaysian plane's black-box data recorders, says the president of the beacon's manufacturer. All the reports caution that it could turn out to be yet another false hope, however. "This could be a variety of things," says one oceanographer, noting that the frequency is used by lots of instruments. For the record, the Chinese ship detected the signal at 25 degrees south latitude and 101 degrees east longitude, says the state-run Xinhua. "It is yet to be established whether it is related to the missing jet," the report says. The development comes as dozens of ships and planes intensify their sweeps in the search zone, given the finite battery life of the data recorders.

**Summary of News: President Trump's Social Media Use and Controversy**

President Donald Trump has once again sparked controversy with his use of social media, particularly Twitter. In recent days, he has posted several tweets that have been deemed incendiary and have raised concerns among some Republicans.

One of the tweets, which featured a GIF of Trump body-slamming and punching WWE Chairman Vince McMahon with the CNN logo superimposed on McMahon's head, was defended by Trump's homeland security adviser, Tom Bossert. Bossert stated that the tweet was not a threat, and that Trump was simply responding to the "beating up" he faces on cable news platforms.

However, the tweet was later criticized by CNN contributor Ana Navarro, who called it an "incitement to violence" and warned that it could lead to someone getting killed in the media.

In a separate tweet, Trump claimed that his social media use is "modern day presidential" and that he should not be convinced to stop using Twitter. He also accused the media of trying to convince lawmakers that he should stop using social media.

The tweet was met with criticism from some Republicans, including Sen. Ben Sasse of Nebraska, who called the tweets "beneath the dignity" of the presidential office. Sen. Lisa Murkowski of Alaska also tweeted that Trump should be remembered for his accomplishments, not his tweets.

This is not the first time Trump's social media use has sparked controversy. During the 2016 presidential campaign, he repeatedly used Twitter to attack his opponents and the media, and has continued to do so since entering the White House. Trump has also claimed that his use of social media helped him win the 2016 election, and that he will continue to use it to "WIN" in the future.

Overall, Trump's social media use continues to be a source of controversy and concern, with many questioning the impact it has on his presidency and the country as a whole.

**Key Quotes:**

* Tom Bossert: "No one would perceive that as a threat."
* Ana Navarro: "It's an incitement to violence. He is going to get somebody killed in the media. Maybe that will stop him."
* President Trump: "My use of social media is not Presidential - it's MODERN DAY PRESIDENTIAL. Make America Great Again."
* Sen. Ben Sasse: "Those tweets were beneath the dignity of the office."
* Sen. Lisa Murkowski: "Do you want to be remembered for your tweets or your
details.ground_truth: President Trump escalated his Twitter war with CNN on Sunday, and in the process sparked more concern that he is advocating the use of violence against members of the media, the Hill reports. The president tweeted a modified video of a 2007 WWE wrestling match he took part in, obscuring his opponent's head with the CNN logo, which he proceeds to beat with his fists. Trump tweeted the video with the hashtags "#FraudNewsCNN" and "#FNN." A CNN spokesman called it "a sad day when the president of the United States encourages violence against reporters." But on ABC News, Homeland Security adviser Tom Bossert suggested CNN and other critics were overreacting. "No one would perceive that as a threat," he said, adding that Trump is "beaten up in a way on cable platforms that he has a right to respond to." The new video comes just a few days after Trump posted two tweets in which he talked about MSNBC's Morning Joe co-host Mika Brzezinski "bleeding badly from a face-lift." Those tweets sparked condemnation from all over the political spectrum, with even Trump's fellow Republicans calling them unpresidential. On Saturday, however, Trump took to Twitter once again to address the backlash, writing, "My use of social media is not Presidential – it's MODERN DAY presidential," Politico reports. At a press conference Thursday, Deputy White House Press Secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders said Trump "no way, form or fashion has ever promoted or encouraged violence," the Washington Post reports. "In fact quite the contrary."

**Apple's iPhone 7: A Controversial Move**

At the recent Apple keynote event, the company unveiled its latest iPhone 7, which has been met with both excitement and criticism. One of the most notable changes is the removal of the standard 3.5mm aux cable input, also known as the headphone jack. This decision has sparked a heated debate among tech enthusiasts and experts.

According to Phil Schiller, Apple's marketing chief, the decision to eliminate the headphone jack was made with "courage." However, many have mocked this statement, pointing out that courage involves doing something that makes you afraid. In this case, Apple has eradicated the most successful, most widespread, and best-sounding audio standard in the world in favor of its own proprietary system.

The removal of the headphone jack has significant implications for iPhone users. It means that they will need to use a $9 aux-to-Lightning cable dongle to listen to music or use other audio devices. This decision has been criticized for being unnecessary, as there is no technological excuse for removing the headphone jack.

The controversy extends beyond the iPhone 7. Apple's decision has also affected other devices that plug into the phone's headphone jack, such as Square's credit card dongle, temperature sensors, light meters, thermal imaging cameras, and personal breathalyzers. These devices will need to be refurbished with a proprietary Lightning connector, which will come at an additional cost.

Critics have also pointed out that Apple's decision to eliminate the headphone jack is a step backward for the company. In the past, Apple has been known for its "it just works" mantra, where any set of headphones would work seamlessly with any iPhone. However, with the removal of the headphone jack, this is no longer the case.

The controversy has also sparked a debate about the cost of Apple's proprietary technology. The company's AirPods, which are designed to work seamlessly with the iPhone 7, cost $159. This is a significant cost, especially considering that users will need to purchase additional accessories to use their existing headphones.

In conclusion, Apple's decision to eliminate the headphone jack in the iPhone 7 has sparked a heated debate among tech enthusiasts and experts. While the company may have intended to showcase its "courage" in innovation, many have criticized the decision as unnecessary and expensive. As the tech industry continues to evolve, it remains to be seen how Apple's decision will impact the market and user experience.
details.ground_truth: Apple presented its new smartphone Wednesday, and nestled in with the iPhone 7's much-ballyhooed features was the disclosure that the company is getting rid of the phone's headphone jack—news that wasn't exactly met with universal acclaim, or much acclaim at all. Chris Taylor is one detractor of the headphone ditch, writing for Mashable that, in nearly 20 years of attending Apple product launches, he's "never heard anything as ridiculous emanate from that stage as I did Wednesday." Apple marketing head Phil Schiller used the word "courage" to describe Apple's innovation, but that's not how Taylor sees it. "Courage is marching across the Edmund Pettus bridge in Selma in 1965 [or] facing down a tank in Tiananmen Square," he writes. "Courage, by definition, involves doing something that makes you afraid." Apparently Taylor doesn't think Apple was quivering in its workboots when it came up with this plan. He's got plenty of other words instead of "courage" in mind, including "hubris," "arrogance," "greed," and his personal favorite, "stupidity," which he attributes to Apple for taking "one step forward, two steps back" both technologically ("wired almost always sounds better than wireless") and logistically (no more plugging in other peripherals like credit card readers, for example). Meanwhile, although much of the other feedback online is similarly cranky about the headphone jack's demise, Quartz notes that Apple is actually late, not early, in making this move: A handful of Chinese smartphone brands have been fiddling around with the idea for years, and some have already sent these phones, sans jacks, to market.

**United Airlines Introduces New Boarding Process and Enhances Customer Experience**

United Airlines has rolled out a new boarding process aimed at reducing congestion and speeding up passenger loading. The airline has reduced the number of boarding lanes from five to two, with elite and priority passengers in groups 1 and 2 boarding through lane 1 and customers in groups 3, 4, and 5 using lane 2. This change is part of the airline's efforts to improve the customer experience and alleviate overcrowding at gates.

The new boarding process is the latest in a series of changes to the airline's boarding procedures. In 2017, American Airlines and Delta Air Lines also introduced new boarding processes, which involved a more staggered boarding call and a reduced number of boarding groups.

United's new boarding process includes a color-coded system, with lane 1 designated as the "blue" lane for pre-boarding and groups 1 and 2, and lane 2 designated as the "green" lane for groups 3, 4, and 5. The airline will also send out notifications to passengers on their mobile devices when boarding begins, allowing them to wait until their boarding group is called.

The airline has tested the new boarding process on thousands of flights and has received positive feedback from customers. The change is expected to reduce wait times and improve communication between the airline and its passengers.

In related news, United Airlines has a joint business agreement (JBA) with Copa and Avianca, which will provide customers with more choices, new nonstop routes, and reduced travel times. The JBA will also drive economic benefits for consumers and the communities served by the airlines.

Overall, United Airlines' new boarding process and joint business agreement are designed to enhance the customer experience and improve the efficiency of the airline's operations.

**Key Highlights:**

* United Airlines introduces new boarding process with two lanes and a color-coded system
* Elite and priority passengers board through lane 1, while customers in groups 3, 4, and 5 use lane 2
* Airline sends out notifications to passengers on their mobile devices when boarding begins
* Joint business agreement with Copa and Avianca provides customers with more choices and reduced travel times
* New boarding process expected to reduce wait times and improve communication between airline and passengers
details.ground_truth: The "angst" travelers feel at overcrowded gates may soon be eased at United Airlines terminals, an exec for the airline says. That's because United just began a new initiative designed to relieve that particular "passenger pain point," as CNBC puts it, by changing up its departure protocol: The number of boarding lines has been cut from five to two, and overeager customers are now asked to stay out of the boarding area until their boarding group (one of six in total, including pre-boarders) is announced. "It's too congested," a UA rep says of the current procedure, which often results in arriving fliers having to push their way through throngs of departing ones. Forbes notes customers can also opt in to receive push notifications via the mobile app, meaning they can hang out in nearby restaurants or shops until they receive an alert on their cellphones that it's time to board. Over the past year, United test-drove a variety of boarding options on about 12,000 flights, ending up with this system, which is similar to the way American, Delta, and Southwest queue up. United has been using the procedure at LAX since winter and will now implement it at all locations, per USA Today. It's also spreading passengers around more equitably within its six boarding groups, which includes pre-boarders (e.g., families with young kids, service members), first-class passengers and frequent fliers in groups 1 and 2, and then everyone else in groups 3, 4, and 5. "The boarding process was one of the top areas customers told us they wanted improved," another UA rep says in a statement, noting customer and employee feedback guided the airline on the enhancements. (United had some "unfortunate dog incidents" earlier this year.)

**Hurricane Irene Brings Destruction and Disruption to the East Coast**

Hurricane Irene, a massive storm with winds of up to 115 mph, is heading towards the East Coast, bringing widespread destruction and disruption to several states. The National Hurricane Center (NHC) has issued a hurricane warning for the entire North Carolina coast to the Virginia border, while seven states - North Carolina, Virginia, Maryland, New Jersey, New York, Delaware, and Connecticut - have declared states of emergency.

**Storm Track and Projections**

Irene is expected to make landfall along North Carolina's Outer Banks on Saturday, with the storm's projected path taking it through the Mid-Atlantic and Northeast regions. The NHC warns of tidal surges of 5 to 10 feet high in North Carolina, accompanied by "destructive and life-threatening waves." Projections show Irene could inundate the state's coastal areas with 6 to 10 inches of rain, and up to 15 inches in some locations.

**Evacuation Orders and Preparations**

As Irene approaches, officials have issued evacuation orders in North Carolina, Virginia, and other states. Over 200,000 tourists and residents are affected by evacuation orders in North Carolina alone, with states to the north rushing to prepare their own evacuation plans. New York City officials have advised residents to stay out of parks and low-lying areas, while Mayor Michael Bloomberg has ordered nursing homes and hospitals in low-lying coastal areas to evacuate.

**Storm Impacts and Damage**

Irene's powerful winds and heavy rain are expected to cause widespread damage, including severe road flooding and widespread electrical outages. Forecasters have said Irene has an outside chance of growing into a Category 4 storm, with sustained winds topping 130 mph. However, current forecasts predict it will diminish to Category 2 after pummeling North Carolina, with sustained winds up to 110 mph as it plows into Virginia, Maryland, and Delaware.

**Emergency Response and Aid**

The Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) has established depots for emergency supplies, including food, water, generators, and baby formula, in several states. President Obama has declared North Carolina an emergency, expediting federal help. The U.S. Navy has moved 64 ships away from Norfolk, Virginia, to keep them from being damaged by the storm.

**Personal Stories and Impacts**

As the storm approaches, many people are heeding evacuation orders and fleeing the affected areas. Some, however, are choosing to stay and ride out the storm
details.ground_truth: Frightened North Carolinians fleeing Irene are jamming highways as the East Coast steels for what could be the biggest hurricane in decades. At least seven states have now declared a state of emergency, and hundreds of flights are being canceled. Gas stations are running out of fuel and ATMs have been emptied of cash as Americans hit the road for a bumper-to-bumper trip out of Irene's angry path. The Category 3 storm is expected to hit the Southeast sometime tomorrow, and hundreds of thousands of residents have been ordered out of their homes in three North Carolina counties. Norfolk, Va., is also ordering residents to evacuate, and Washington, DC, has been forced to postpone Sunday's planned MLK Memorial dedication. "This is a very, very serious situation," a spokeswoman for Dare County told ABC News. "We have not seen anything like this in the lifetimes of most our residents. Once the storm hits, it will be very difficult to respond to distress calls." Irene is some 700 miles wide now and moving at a slow 12mph, which means it can wreak extensive damage in a region over a long period of time; it could cause up to $13.9 billion in damage on the East Coast. The storm is expected to barrel into New York City on Sunday, packing winds of up to 90mph. New Jersey communities are already being evacuated, and hundreds of street fairs have been canceled and elder care facilities and hospitals in low-lying area will be evacuated in New York today. NYC Mayor Michael Bloomberg says residents in low-lying parts of the city should get out today as the transit system is planned to be shut down tomorrow.

**Miami Gardens Police Chief Stephen Johnson Arrested for Soliciting Prostitute**

Miami Gardens Police Chief Stephen Johnson, 53, was arrested on February 27, 2015, for soliciting a prostitute in Dania Beach, Florida. Johnson, who had been the police chief of Miami Gardens since May 2014, was immediately fired from his position after his arrest.

According to the Broward Sheriff's Office, Johnson was arrested as part of an undercover sting operation targeting solicitation of prostitution. Deputies had placed an ad on Backpage.com offering a "two-girl special" and Johnson called the number, agreeing to pay $100 for a 30-minute session. He was arrested when he arrived at the hotel room, where two detectives were posing as prostitutes.

Johnson claimed that he was overwhelmed with stress on the job, specifically referencing a recent incident involving a 10-year-old girl who was struck by a police car. However, he did not elaborate further on the connection between the incident and his arrest.

Johnson's arrest is the latest in a series of controversies to hit the Miami Gardens Police Department. In January, the department was criticized for the fatal shooting of a mentally ill man, Lavall Hall, and in February, protesters marched into City Hall to express their anger over the shooting.

Johnson had been a veteran cop with 30 years of experience, rising from a rookie cop to police chief in North Miami before taking the helm in Miami Gardens. However, his tenure was marked by controversy, including a scandal involving the harassment of minority employees and customers at a convenience store.

The city has released a statement saying that it remains committed to excellence and integrity, and that Johnson's actions will not reflect negatively on the hardworking officers of the city. Assistant Chief Antonio Brooklen has been named interim chief.

Johnson's arrest also raises questions about his personal life, as he is listed as a pastor at Bethel House of God Church in Hallandale Beach. However, efforts to reach church officials were unsuccessful.
details.ground_truth: A Florida police chief got arrested Friday for allegedly soliciting a prostitute and was promptly fired just 10 months into his tenure, NBC Miami reports. According to an arrest report, Miami Gardens Police Chief Stephen Johnson called a number in a "two-girl special" prostitution ad and negotiated a $100 deal for two women for 30 minutes. Only problem: The woman he allegedly paid at a motel was really a sheriff's detective. "The stress overwhelmed me, and I made a very bad decision," Johnson tells the Miami Herald in his apology. He was hired last May to repair community relations after former top cop Matthew Boyd resigned amid allegations of harassment and illegal tactics. "It was one of the most painful things I've ever heard as a manager," says the city manager who fired Stephens, the Sun-Sentinel reports. "It's like a punch in the stomach."

**Detroit Water Crisis: Activists Protest Water Shutoffs Amid Rising Debt and Poverty**

The city of Detroit is facing a crisis as the Detroit Water and Sewerage Department (DWSD) continues to shut off water services to tens of thousands of residents who are behind on their bills. The issue has sparked widespread protests and condemnation from activists, who argue that the shutoffs are a human rights violation, particularly in a city where nearly 50% of the population lives below the poverty line.

Activists have been using civil disobedience, including blocking trucks and delivery of water to the DWSD, to protest the shutoffs. The protests have resulted in numerous arrests, with at least 30 people detained on Thursday, including eight who were arrested for disorderly conduct. The DWSD has been criticized for targeting low-income residents while ignoring the debts of large commercial entities, such as a golf course and the State of Michigan, which owe hundreds of thousands of dollars.

The crisis has drawn international attention, with a U.N. panel condemning the shutoffs as a human rights violation and calling for the immediate reconnection of water services to disconnected households. The city's water prices are also on the rise, with the average household bill increasing from $65 to $75.

In response to the criticism, the DWSD has announced a $1 million fund to help low-income residents pay their water bills and avoid shutoffs. However, activists argue that this is insufficient and that the department should work with residents to find a solution rather than turning off their water.

The issue has also sparked debate over the nature of water as a human right, with some arguing that it is a fundamental right that should be guaranteed to all individuals, regardless of their ability to pay. The DWSD has maintained that it must pay for water delivery and is a nonprofit enterprise, but activists argue that this is a false distinction.

As the crisis continues, protesters are vowing to take further action, including larger demonstrations and potential boycotts, to draw attention to the issue and push for a resolution. The DWSD has announced plans to intensify its efforts to collect unpaid debts from delinquent commercial customers, but activists argue that this will only exacerbate the problem and further marginalize low-income residents.

The federal government has also been called upon to intervene, with U.S. Rep. John Conyers sending letters to President Obama and the Department of Health and Human Services requesting funding to address the crisis. The issue is likely to remain a contentious and high-profile issue in the coming
details.ground_truth: At least eight people were arrested yesterday in Detroit for protesting water shutoffs in a city where almost 40% of residents live below the poverty line. Dozens picketed in front of a facility affiliated with the city's Water and Sewerage Department to try to stop what activists call a “human rights violation,” reports Al Jazeera America. Police tell MLive that the individuals charged with misdemeanor disorderly conduct range in age from 58 to 70 and include members of the clergy. (Watch video of the protest and arrests here.) The Rev. Bill Wylie-Kellermann, one of the arrested protesters, tells the Detroit Free Press that police tried to "move us forcibly, and we sat down. … We [were] here to appeal to the workers to stop shutting off the water." The coordinator of a local activist group says "police officers should be arresting rapists and murderers instead of arresting peaceful people." A Detroit Police rep counters that protesters still have to follow rules: "You cannot impede pedestrian or vehicle traffic." The Free Press counts 7,210 shutoffs in June, up from 7,556 in April and May combined, as part of what Al Jazeera reports is an effort to recover $175 million in unpaid bills. As of March, nearly half of the city's 323,900 DWSD accounts were delinquent; activists have asked the UN to intervene.

**Nazi War Crimes: Canadian Resident Vladimir Katriuk Dies at 93**

A long-standing dispute over the alleged Nazi war crimes of Vladimir Katriuk, a 93-year-old Canadian resident, has come to an end with his death. Katriuk, a beekeeper of Ukrainian ancestry, was accused of participating in a 1943 massacre in Belarus and was listed as the second most wanted Nazi war criminal by the Simon Wiesenthal Centre.

Katriuk's death was announced by his lawyer, Orest Rudzik, who said his client had been ill for a long time before his passing. The news emerged just hours after the Canadian Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs called on the Canadian government to take steps to ensure Katriuk was held accountable if found guilty of war crimes.

The Canadian government had previously been urged to revoke Katriuk's citizenship, which he obtained under false pretenses by not disclosing his collaboration with the Nazis. However, in 2007, the Harper cabinet decided not to revoke his citizenship due to a lack of evidence of atrocities committed.

Katriuk had denied allegations of war crimes, claiming he was forced to join the Ukrainian Waffen SS battalion and did not participate in operations with the Germans. However, a study published three years ago alleged Katriuk was a key participant in a village massacre during World War II.

The Russian government had recently charged Katriuk with genocide in connection with the 1943 killing of civilians in Khatyn, Belarus. Russia's Investigative Committee had called on Canada to deliver Katriuk to Moscow so he could be tried for alleged war crimes.

The case had sparked tensions between Canada and Russia, with Canada suspending all but low-level diplomatic relations with Russia in response to Moscow's aggression in Ukraine. Jewish groups had long urged the deportation of Katriuk, while the Ukrainian Canadian Congress had urged Ottawa to ignore Russia's demands.

In a related development, German prosecutors have shelved their investigation into another suspected Nazi war criminal, Gerhard Sommer, due to his advanced age and dementia. Sommer was suspected of participating in the 1944 slaughter of civilians in Italy and had been found guilty by an Italian military court in 2005.

Overall, the death of Vladimir Katriuk brings a sense of closure to a long-standing controversy, but raises questions about the accountability of individuals accused of Nazi war crimes and the complexities of international justice in the face of aging suspects and conflicting national interests.
details.ground_truth: There were two men at the top of this year's Simon Wiesenthal Center most-wanted Nazi war criminal list—and there is now no chance either one will face a court. No. 2 on the list, Ukrainian-born Vladimir Katriuk, has died in Quebec at the age of 93, the Toronto Star reports. Katriuk, who moved to Canada in 1951 and worked as a beekeeper for more than 50 years, was accused of being a "particularly active participant" in a massacre in what is now Belarus in 1943, when he was a member of an SS battalion that allegedly carried out many atrocities, the Guardian reports. Russia charged Katriuk with genocide last month, but Canadian authorities refused a request to send him to Moscow, citing Russia's actions in Ukraine, reports the Globe and Mail. Katriuk had long been under suspicion, but the wheels of justice did not move quickly: In 1999, Canadian authorities concluded he had entered the country under false pretenses, but in 2007 a decision to cancel his citizenship was overturned due to lack of evidence, the Star reports. The man at the top of the most-wanted list is also 93 years old. Gerhard Sommer is still alive in a Hamburg-area nursing home, but German prosecutors say they've dropped their investigation because he suffers from dementia and isn't fit to stand trial, the AP reports. In 2005, he was one of 10 former SS officers an Italian court found guilty of taking part in a massacre of 560 civilians in 1944, but German law didn't permit his extradition.

**Google CEO Sundar Pichai Receives Massive Equity Award**

Google's CEO Sundar Pichai has been granted an equity award valued at nearly $200 million, making him one of the world's highest-paid executives. The award, consisting of 273,328 Class C Google stock units, was valued at $199 million at the time of the grant and has a current value of approximately $182 million.

Pichai's new award has lifted the value of his equity stake in Alphabet to over $600 million. This award is one of the largest by a U.S. company and ranks among the highest-paid executive awards in history. The award vests in quarterly increments over the next four years, as long as Pichai remains employed by Alphabet.

In addition to Pichai, other Google executives have also received significant equity awards. Alphabet's Chief Financial Officer Ruth Porat was awarded shares worth about $38 million, on top of a $30 million signing bonus given to her last year. Diane Greene, the head of Google's cloud computing business, was awarded shares worth $42.8 million, in addition to $148 million worth of shares granted last year following Google's takeover of Bebop Technologies.

Pichai's award is worth more than the $187 million Google has agreed to pay the UK government in back taxes since 2005. Critics have calculated that Google generated sales of $34.6 billion in the UK between 2005 and 2014, with an estimated profit of $10.4 billion.

Despite the massive award, Pichai's net worth is still far short of that of Google's founders, Larry Page and Sergey Brin, who are worth $34.6 billion and $33.9 billion, respectively. Eric Schmidt, Alphabet's executive chairman, also holds shares worth $3 billion.

Pichai celebrated his award by watching the Super Bowl and meeting NFL legend Ronnie Lott.
details.ground_truth: Google's parent company appears to be satisfied with how Sundar Pichai has performed during his four months as Google CEO, judging by the gargantuan $199 million equity award revealed in a securities filing. Alphabet stated that Pichai was awarded 273,328 Class C Google stock units on Feb. 3, bringing his holdings to around $650 million, reports the Wall Street Journal. The award makes Pichai the highest-paid CEO in America, according to the Guardian, which notes that Pichai's fortune is dwarfed by those of Google founders Larry Page and Sergey Brin, who are worth around $34 billion each, and by that of previous CEO Eric Schmidt, who's sitting on around $3 billion. (A former Google employee was awarded exactly $6,006.13.)

**Josh Hardy's Family Wins a Medical Miracle: Chimerix Approves Life-Saving Drug**

In a heartwarming story of perseverance and compassion, the family of 7-year-old Josh Hardy has achieved a major victory in their battle to save their son's life. After a social media campaign and media coverage drew national attention to Josh's plight, the pharmaceutical company Chimerix has agreed to provide him with a potentially life-saving drug, brincidofovir, to combat a rare adenovirus infection.

Josh, a first-grader from Fredericksburg, Virginia, has been battling cancer since he was 9 months old and underwent a bone marrow transplant in February. However, the surgery left him without a strong immune system, making him more susceptible to infections. The adenovirus infection has ravaged his body, putting his life at risk.

Chimerix, the company that produces brincidofovir, initially refused to provide the drug to Josh, citing concerns that doing so would slow down the development process. However, after a campaign led by Josh's family and supporters, the company changed its stance and agreed to participate in a clinical trial to test the safety and effectiveness of the drug in children.

The trial, which will involve 20 patients, including Josh, will begin immediately, with the FDA committing to work expeditiously with Chimerix to design a pivotal Phase 3 study. The company has also committed to providing the drug to Josh, who is currently in critical condition at St. Jude Children's Research Hospital in Memphis.

Josh's family and supporters have been overwhelmed by the outpouring of support, which has included online petitions, social media campaigns, and protests outside Chimerix's headquarters. While the road ahead will be challenging, the news has brought hope to Josh's family and a sense of accomplishment to the community.

As Chimerix President and CEO Kenneth Moch said, "We had to figure out a way to make it available to many people in need." The company's decision to participate in the clinical trial and provide the drug to Josh is a testament to the power of compassion and the importance of medical research.

Josh's story has also highlighted the need for more clinical development to assess the potential of brincidofovir in treating adenovirus infections in children. The case has sparked a national conversation about the ethics of pharmaceutical companies and the importance of prioritizing the needs of patients.

For now, Josh's family is grateful for the second chance they have been given, and they are hopeful
details.ground_truth: After a social media campaign—and an avalanche of bad publicity—a drug company has done a U-turn and agreed to give a dying 7-year-old boy an experimental drug that could save his life. The FDA allows "compassionate use"of unapproved drugs in such cases, but the Chimerix pharmaceutical company had insisted that giving the drug to Josh Hardy, who developed a bone marrow disorder after cancer treatment, would delay efforts to get it to market, CNN reports. Chimerix president Kenneth Moch says Josh will be the first patient in a pilot trial for the drug. "Being unable to fulfill requests for compassionate use is excruciating, and not a decision any one of us ever wants to have to make," says Moch, who tells the Fredericksburg Lance-Star that he and his employees have received death threats amid a flood of pleas to help the Virginia boy. Josh is expected to receive the drug within 48 hours, though his mother fears he is running out of time. "Even though he is frail, he has a very strong will about him," she tells Fox News. "But things just keep stacking against him, and we just want to do everything we can to give him the opportunity to make a full recovery."

details.ground_truth: The latest disturbing Hollywood sexual misconduct allegation involves Tom Sizemore, an actor who has already faced drug and domestic violence charges. Multiple cast and crew members tell the Hollywood Reporter that Sizemore, best known for roles in war films including Black Hawk Down, was kicked off a Utah movie set in 2003 for allegedly touching an 11-year-old girl's genitals. They say the day after a Born Killers scene in which the girl sat on Sizemore's lap, she told her parents that he had touched her inappropriately, possibly putting his finger inside her. "At one point her eyes got just huge, like she could've vomited. I was watching her," co-star Robyn Adamson says. "Later, when I was told about what happened, I knew exactly what it was." "There was never any doubt. He was this guy who was already known for making inappropriate comments, being drunk, being high," says production assistant Roi Maufas. "Then this happens. Guys reached for hammers," he says. Producers say Sizemore, who was ordered to leave the set, denied the allegations. Crew members say the girl's parents spoke to police, but ended up not pressing charges, possibly out of fear of ruining her film career. She is now 26 and says she is considering legal action against both Sizemore and her parents. Sizemore, 55, was dropped by his management firm soon after the incident. Former manager Charles Lago tells USA Today that he is "not surprised" by the allegations. "He's the most abhorrent person I've ever met in my life," Lago says.

Linda Hooper, a veteran elementary teacher from Texas, has a remarkable story of adopting her former student, Cruz Riojas, whom she initially met in 1980. At the time, Cruz was a 12-year-old student at Brown Elementary School, notorious for his frequent outbursts and violent behavior. Despite Hooper's initial reservations, she took Cruz under her wing and provided him with love, support, and stability that he had been lacking at home.

Cruz, who suffered years of physical abuse at the hands of his stepfather, found solace in Hooper's home and in her husband Gale, a firefighter. Over time, Cruz began to open up and form a close bond with the Hoopers, who eventually helped him to graduate from high school and even supported him in his college studies.

As Cruz grew older, he started to develop a strong sense of identity and eventually asked Hooper and Gale to officially adopt him, which they did in 1998. Cruz's decision to change his last name to Hooper was a testament to the love and acceptance he had received from his adoptive parents.

Today, Cruz is a successful man with a family of his own, living in San Antonio with his wife Anel and their two children, Hannah and Greyson. Despite his difficult past, Cruz has found happiness and fulfillment in his life, and he credits the Hoopers for their unwavering love and support.

Cruz's story is a powerful reminder of the transformative power of love and acceptance, and the impact that one person can have on another's life. Hooper's selfless act of adopting Cruz has inspired many, and her story serves as a testament to the importance of compassion, empathy, and understanding in breaking down barriers and building stronger communities.

In related news, Alexa Internet has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996, adding to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. However, this news is not directly related to the story of Linda Hooper and Cruz Riojas.

Overall, the story of Linda Hooper and Cruz Riojas is a heartwarming tale of adoption, love, and transformation, and a reminder of the enduring impact that one person can have on another's life.
details.ground_truth: Cruz Riojas came from a troubled home: He was reportedly beaten by his stepfather, lived in a decrepit one-room lean-to with six other family members, and wore the same clothes to school every day, according to the Lubbock Avalanche-Journal. He also caused trouble in school and was known as a “holy terror,” says Linda Hooper, a teacher who first encountered Cruz in the early ‘80s, when he was 12 years old. But then an amazing thing happened: He started spending more time with Hooper, who would give him tasks to do around the classroom. He eventually began coming for visits at her home, where she lived with her husband and four daughters, often running the nearly eight miles between their two houses to get away from his stepfather. After an incident with his stepfather in 1983, Cruz’s mother asked if he could stay with the Hoopers for a few days till everything blew over. "He never left" after that, Hooper says. "I had him from then on." He became a part of the Hooper family, taking on a paper route to make money and doing better in school, placing out of special education classes by the time he graduated. More than a decade later, he was still part of the Hooper family—but he wanted to make it official by asking the Hoopers to adopt him right before his 30th birthday. “He said he wanted to honor us, and it was a big honor," Hooper tells Today. "I’ve always known he loved me, but this really showed he loved us.” The adoption went through in 1998, but Cruz "was my son from the day I kept him,” Hooper says. The family is just now telling their story for the first time. (Click for the full article, or read the story of a police officer who found a baby alive at a murder scene—and adopted her 30 years later.)

The past week has seen a significant escalation in the 2016 US presidential election, with Republican nominee Donald Trump launching a series of attacks on the media and his Democratic rival Hillary Clinton.

"I am not only fighting Crooked Hillary, I am fighting the dishonest and corrupt media and her government protection process," he tweeted. "It is not 'freedom of the press' when newspapers and others are allowed to say and write whatever they want even if it is completely false!" he added. The Wall Street Journal editorial board warned Sunday that Trump's "window for a turnaround" is closing and his GOP supporters now face a "moment of truth" before he is written off as a lost cause. "The tragedy is that this is happening in a year when Republicans should win," they write.
