**Groundbreaking Ear Reconstruction Surgery Gives New Lease of Life to Deaf Boy**

In a remarkable medical achievement, a 9-year-old boy, Kieran Sorkin, from Hertfordshire, has undergone a pioneering ear reconstruction surgery at London's Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH). Born with bilateral microtia, a rare congenital condition where the external ear is underdeveloped, Kieran was deaf and had small ear lobes instead of fully formed ears.

Thanks to the expertise of consultant plastic and reconstructive surgeon Neil Bulstrode and a team of skilled medics, Kieran has been given a pair of new ears crafted from his own rib cartilage. The 6-hour operation involved harvesting cartilage from both sides of Kieran's chest, shaping it into frameworks for his ears, and grafting them onto his head under pockets of skin. The innovative procedure has brought a significant improvement in Kieran's quality of life, boosting his confidence and performance at school.

**The Psychological Benefits**

The surgery is primarily cosmetic, not designed to improve Kieran's hearing, but it has brought huge psychological benefits. According to Mr. Bulstrode, if a patient's confidence is changed at a young age, it can alter their whole trajectory in life. Kieran's reaction to his new ears was immediate and enthusiastic, with "Wow!" being his first word upon seeing them.

**Tissue Engineering Advances**

Advances in tissue engineering hold promise for future ear reconstructions. Scientists at the University College London Institute for Child Health are creating stem cells from patients' fat tissue, which can be induced to make cartilage or bone. This approach could be far less invasive than the current method of harvesting rib cartilage, and research is ongoing to explore its potential.

**A Brighter Future**

Kieran's story is a testament to the power of medical innovation and the dedicated team at GOSH. While he will need a follow-up operation in six months to refine his new ears, he is already delighted with the result. As Kieran looks forward to a brighter future, his family and medical team are optimistic about the progress he will make.

In the UK, about 100 children are born each year with microtia, a condition that affects one in 100,000 babies. Kieran's story serves as a reminder of the importance of medical research and the impact it can have on the lives of children like him.
details.ground_truth: An operation in London has given 9-year-old Kieran Sorkin a pair of ears—made from his ribs, no less. Born deaf with no ears, Kieran was able to hear because of an implanted hearing aid, but he still wanted ears. "I want people to stop asking me questions," he told the BBC. "I'd like just to look like my friends. I'd also like to be able to wear sunglasses and earphones." So surgeons traced an outline of Kieran's mom's ears, removed cartilage from the boy's ribs, and sliced, contoured, and sewed the cartilage. Then they put it in two skin pockets and vacuumed them into the shape of ears. They don't perform any function, but Kieran's plastic surgeon says they can deliver a huge confidence boost: "If you can change the confidence of a patient at this young age, you can change their whole trajectory in life," he said. The ears should last, too, because they're made of cartilage rather than prosthetics, the Guardian reports. Every year about 100 British newborns lack one or both ears (it's called microtia), and the hospital Kieran went to performs the operation about 40 times a year. Scientists hope to one day make ears from the fat tissue of patients, instead of ribs. Kieran might have liked that, since he got so excited on seeing his new ears that he cried "Wow!" and giggled—which made his ribs hurt from the operation. (Click to read about a man who needed surgery to remove a tooth growing in his nose.)

**Trump's Transition Period Marred by Verbal Sparring with Obama**

President-elect Donald Trump and outgoing President Barack Obama have been engaging in a heated exchange on social media amidst a tense transition period. Trump, who had initially praised Obama's efforts to ensure a smooth transition, reversed course on Wednesday, accusing Obama of putting "roadblocks" in his way.

The verbal sparring began after Obama, in a podcast interview, claimed that he could have won the 2016 election against Trump if he had run again. Trump responded with a series of tweets, saying that he was "doing my best to disregard the many inflammatory President O statements and roadblocks" and that the transition was not going smoothly.

However, later in the day, Trump said that he had a "nice conversation" with Obama, who had called him earlier in the day. Trump also emphasized that they had a "good talk" and that he thought they covered a lot of good territory. The two leaders also spoke about the transition, with Trump saying that it was going "very, very smoothly."

The tweets, however, sparked a flurry of reactions from both sides. Trump's transition team said that the tweets "speak for themselves," while the White House declined to comment on the matter. Trump also took aim at Obama's administration, criticizing its decision not to veto a United Nations Security Council resolution condemning Israeli settlements in the West Bank and East Jerusalem.

The exchange between Trump and Obama has cast a shadow over the transition period, which is already marked by unprecedented levels of controversy and tension. The two leaders are set to attend Trump's inauguration on January 20, with Obama committing to a peaceful and smooth transition of power.

In related news, Secretary of State John F. Kerry is set to leave office with a mixed legacy, having achieved successes in curbing Iran's nuclear program and addressing climate change, but facing criticism for his inability to halt the carnage in Syria and to block Russia's growing influence in the region.

Overall, the exchange between Trump and Obama has highlighted the deep divisions between the two leaders and has raised questions about the prospects for a smooth transition of power.
details.ground_truth: It looks like the honeymoon is over between Trump and Obama. "Doing my best to disregard the many inflammatory President O statements and roadblocks. Thought it was going to be a smooth transition—NOT!" Trump tweeted Wednesday. The Los Angeles Times notes Trump's use of "1990s-style sarcasm." Trump had previously commended Obama on his commitment to a smooth transition of power, but a number of things have changed since then, the Washington Post reports. Many saw comments made by Obama during Tuesday's Pearl Harbor anniversary as directed at Trump. The president urged the US not to "turn inward" and to "resist the urge to demonize those who are different." Trump is also upset with the Obama administration for allowing the UN to pass a resolution condemning Israeli settlements, as well as with Obama personally for saying in an interview he could have beaten Trump and won a third term. In addition, the White House is defending the Department of Energy for not complying with a request from Trump's transition team for the names of employees involved in climate policy, ABC News reports. Transition team spokesperson Sean Spicer at first said Trump's tweets "speak for themselves, very clearly," but later complimented members of the Obama administration for being "helpful and generous with their time."

**Villager Compensated for Wild Panda Attack in Northwest China**

A Chinese villager, Guan Quanzhi, has been awarded over $83,000 in compensation after he was attacked by a wild panda in March last year. The incident occurred in Liziba village, in the northwestern province of Gansu, where a panda wandered into the village and was chased by local officials onto Guan's land.

According to reports, the panda bit Guan in the leg, requiring seven hours of surgery to treat the injuries. Despite the panda's seemingly gentle nature, it is a member of the bear family and has a fearsome bite. Guan's son sued local forestry officials and the nearby Baishuijiang National Nature Reserve, which is home to over 100 wild pandas.

Following negotiations, officials agreed to pay compensation of 520,000 yuan ($83,000) to cover Guan's medical bills. Guan is satisfied with the amount, which will also cover potential future operations. The compensation was agreed upon between the forestry bureau at the provincial level, the forestry bureau of Longnan city, and the management bureau of Baishuijiang Natural Reserve.

This incident highlights the importance of conservation measures for wild pandas, which are a major generator of tourist revenue in several parts of China. Despite their lovable appearance, wild pandas are members of the bear family and can be unpredictable. In 2008, a panda mauled a 20-year-old man who climbed into its enclosure at a zoo in southern China.

The number of wild giant pandas has increased by nearly 17 percent over the decade to 2013, reaching 1,864, according to state media. This increase is attributed to conservation measures, although the animals remain under pressure from factors such as habitat loss.

Guan's case serves as a reminder of the risks associated with living near wild panda habitats and the importance of taking necessary precautions to prevent such incidents.
details.ground_truth: Pandas may prefer their meals in the form of bamboo, but that doesn't mean their bite is toothless. A man in China recently received a harsh reminder of that after a wild panda bit his leg after authorities pursued the critter onto his lawn, reports AFP via Yahoo. The creature had made its way into a northwestern village, which is near a sanctuary that's home to about 100 wild pandas. "I saw a panda jump out in front of me, its body completely covered in mud," the man told local news. A struggle ensued, in which the bear wouldn't release the man's leg until a rescuer wrapped a coat around the animal's head. Finally, it fled—but not before causing injuries which, China Daily reports, led to eight surgeries over the past year and may lead to amputation. The man sued three government departments and won an $83,000 payout that will cover his medical costs, his lawyer says. (China Daily has a pretty gruesome photo of the injury.) "As cuddly as they may look, a panda can protect itself as well as most other bears," the World Wildlife Fund notes, per AFP. Indeed, panda attacks have led to some nasty consequences in the past.

**Missing 9-Year-Old Carlie Trent: A Week of Desperate Search**

On May 4th, 9-year-old Carlie Trent was abducted by her uncle, Gary Simpson, from her school in Rogersville, Tennessee. A week later, the search for Carlie continues, with authorities urging the public to remain vigilant and trust that Carlie is in imminent danger.

**Background of the Abduction**

Gary Simpson, 57, allegedly signed Carlie out of her elementary school under false pretenses, claiming her father, James Trent, was in a car accident. However, authorities say this was a lie and Simpson had no authority to pick up Carlie. Simpson had previously had custody of Carlie and her 7-year-old sister, but not for the past two years.

**Items Purchased by Simpson**

Before the abduction, Simpson purchased several items, including camping supplies, girls' underwear, a bikini, clothing, lipstick, nail polish, blankets, and a child's camping chair at a local Walmart. These items confirm authorities' suspicion that Simpson brought Carlie to a remote area or campground.

**Search Efforts**

The Tennessee Bureau of Investigation (TBI) has received over 1,200 leads and is working with law enforcement in 19 states to track possible sightings of Carlie and Simpson. A Missouri Amber Alert was issued after a possible sighting, but it was later canceled. An Amber Alert remains active in Tennessee.

A $10,000 reward is being offered by two Knoxville banking executives, Matt Daniels and Tim Williams, for information leading to Carlie's safe return. The U.S. Marshals Service is also offering a $5,000 reward. Carlie's mother, Shannon Trent, appeals to Simpson to come forward, urging him to "think about what you are doing right now – it's just crazy, it's just insane. You're not going to get away with this. We will find her – we have to find her."

**Description of Carlie and Simpson**

Carlie is described as 4'8" tall, weighing about 75 lbs., with blonde hair and blue eyes. Simpson is balding, about 5'10" tall, and weighs 157 pounds, with brown hair and brown eyes.

**Public's Help Needed**

details.ground_truth: Authorities are combing through more than 1,200 leads in a desperate search for a 9-year-old girl they say was abducted by her uncle May 4, WATE reports. According to the Knoxville News Sentinel, 57-year-old Gary Simpson picked Carlie Trent up from her Tennessee school, telling staff her father had been in an accident. Shortly before, he purchased a child's nightgown, bikini, girls' underwear, lipstick, and nail polish at an area Walmart. The Tennessee Bureau of Investigation has pictures of those items. The TBI says there have been rumors online that Simpson is trying to protect Carlie, but it says that couldn't be further from the truth. "This was not an innocent camping trip, this was a crime," a spokesperson tells WATE. Simpson, Carlie's uncle by marriage, and his wife once had custody of Carlie, but her father James Trent had custody at the time of her abduction. Shannon Trent, who hasn't had custody of Carlie in two years, says she "always had a bad feeling" about Simpson. "I should have stuck with my gut," she tells People. (This boy's summer with his dad turned into his kidnapping.)

**CBS News Overhauls "The Early Show" with New Team**

CBS News has made significant changes to its morning program, "The Early Show," in an attempt to boost ratings and refresh the show. The network has replaced co-hosts Harry Smith and Maggie Rodriguez with Chris Wragge and Erica Hill, who have been anchoring the show's Saturday edition since 2008. Jeff Glor will join the team as the regular news reader, while Marysol Castro, a former weather anchor for ABC's "Good Morning America" weekend edition, will become the new weather forecaster.

The changes come as a result of "The Early Show" consistently ranking behind NBC's "Today" show and ABC's "Good Morning America" in the network morning news ratings. Despite its efforts to revamp the show, CBS News President Sean McManus acknowledged that he doesn't expect immediate ratings changes, but believes the new team will help the show grow.

The outgoing co-hosts, Smith and Rodriguez, will not be leaving the network entirely. Smith will become the primary substitute anchor for Katie Couric's evening newscast, "Face the Nation," and "Sunday Morning," while Rodriguez's future roles at CBS have yet to be determined. Dave Price, the weather forecaster, will also be leaving the show, but his new role at the network has not been announced.

The sudden changes have sparked debate among television critics, with some viewing it as a necessary move to shake things up and others seeing it as a sign of desperation. Despite the uncertainty, the new team is set to take over on January 3, and fans of the show are eagerly awaiting the changes.

**Key Changes:**

* Chris Wragge and Erica Hill to co-anchor "The Early Show" starting January 3
* Jeff Glor to join the team as regular news reader
* Marysol Castro to become new weather forecaster
* Harry Smith to become primary substitute anchor for Katie Couric's evening newscast, "Face the Nation," and "Sunday Morning"
* Maggie Rodriguez's future roles at CBS to be determined
* Dave Price to leave the show, but new role at the network not announced
details.ground_truth: CBS is scrapping its Early Show team, replacing co-hosts Harry Smith and Maggie Rodriguez with the anchors of the show's Saturday edition, Chris Wragge and Erica Hill. Other more minor players also are leaving in the switch, which takes place early next year, the LA Times reports. Former US President George H.W. Bush has passed away at the age of 94. Throughout his life, Bush made significant contributions to the country and the world. He was the last American president to serve in World War II, where he was a young pilot in the Navy and survived a harrowing rescue after being shot down.

Bush's life was marked by his dedication to public service. He served as a congressman, UN ambassador, and CIA director before becoming the vice president under President Ronald Reagan. In 1988, he won the Republican nomination and went on to defeat Michael Dukakis, becoming the 41st President of the United States.

As president, Bush was known for his diplomatic efforts, particularly in the aftermath of the Berlin Wall's collapse and the Gulf War. His administration played a crucial role in the peaceful dissolution of the Soviet Union and the unification of Germany. However, his presidency was also marked by economic challenges, including a recession and high unemployment, which ultimately led to his defeat in the 1992 election.

Despite his electoral loss, Bush maintained a strong sense of loyalty and humility. He famously said, "I will not raise taxes" during his 1988 campaign and later abandoned this pledge, which led to a backlash from conservatives. However, he also showed a willingness to admit mistakes and move forward, as seen in his work with former President Bill Clinton on tsunami relief efforts.

Throughout his life, Bush was known for his wit and humor. He famously said, "I do not like broccoli, and I haven’t liked it since I was a little kid and my mother made me eat it." He also showed a sense of humor about his own legacy, saying, "I plan to put something back into society, and not at the head table, not always in the glamour, certainly not with a lot of news attention."

Bush's commitment to public service extended beyond his presidency. He inspired generations of Americans, including his son George W. Bush, who became the 43rd President of the United States. Even in his later years, Bush remained active, undertaking diplomatic missions and celebrating his birthday with a parachute jump.

President George H.W. Bush will be remembered for his dedication to public service, his commitment to diplomacy, and his sense of humor. He inspired countless Americans with his life and legacy, and his passing is a loss for the nation.
details.ground_truth: With George HW Bush dead at 94, coverage of the life of the 41st president is plentiful. "The Berlin Wall fell; the Soviet Union ceased to exist; the communist bloc in Eastern Europe broke up; the Cold War ended." And Bush's "firm, restrained diplomatic sense helped assure the harmony and peace with which these world-shaking events played out, one after the other." Great detail: In its obituary, the Wall Street Journal notes that Bush flew 58 missions in the Pacific as a Navy pilot in WWII, all with the name of his girlfriend—Barbara—on the side of the plane. He once got shot down, with this video capturing the rescue. Bush is also the only American besides John Adams to be both president and the father of a president. Read his lips: The Guardian recounts some of his Bush's famous quotes, including his "read my lips" pledge to not raise taxes. He also dissed broccoli.

**O.J. Simpson's Parole Hearing: A Step Closer to Freedom**

O.J. Simpson, the former NFL star and actor, may soon be a step closer to freedom after serving over eight years in a Nevada prison. Simpson, 70, will appear before the Nevada Parole Board on Thursday, July 20, in a hearing that could lead to his release from Lovelock Correctional Center as early as October 1.

Simpson was convicted of 12 counts, including armed robbery and kidnapping, related to a 2007 incident in which he and five other men broke into a Las Vegas hotel room and stole sports memorabilia. He was sentenced to 9-33 years in prison.

The parole hearing comes after Simpson expressed remorse for the incident in a 2013 statement, saying, "I wish I'd said, 'Keep it and not worry about it.'" He also claimed to have been a model prisoner, stating, "I've not had any incidents despite all the stories in the tabloids and everything."

The media interest in the hearing is overwhelming, with 30 media organizations set to cover the event. If the Parole Board rules in Simpson's favor, he will be released from prison on a conditional parole, which could be revoked if he commits any further crimes.

Simpson's past is marked by controversy, including his acquittal in 1995 of the murders of his ex-wife, Nicole Brown Simpson, and her friend, Ronald Goldman. He was later found liable for the murders in a civil trial and ordered to pay $33.5 million in damages.

Despite the controversy surrounding his case, Simpson's lawyer, Yale Galanter, believes that he deserves parole, stating, "He deserves parole. It's as simple as that."

The outcome of the parole hearing is uncertain, but if Simpson is released, he will be able to live on his NFL pension, which is estimated to be $25,000 per month. He will also be subject to certain conditions, including regular check-ins with his parole officer.

In the end, Simpson's release from prison will be a significant moment in the ongoing saga of one of the most infamous cases in American history.

* The hearing could lead to his release from Lovelock Correctional Center as early as October 1.

details.ground_truth: OJ Simpson's parole hearing in Nevada will be broadcast live at 1pm EDT Thursday and there's a strong chance that his path to freedom will be clear by the end of the afternoon. Nobody has registered to testify against the 70-year-old Simpson, who has been in prison for nine years on 12 convictions related to a Las Vegas robbery and will appear before the Nevada Parole Board via videolink, NPR reports. Thursday's hearing will cover four concurrent sentences for use of a deadly weapon and two consecutive sentences for assault with a deadly weapon, the Las Vegas Review-Journal reports. Four board members will vote after the hearing and Simpson could be free by Oct. 1 if they are all in favor of parole. If Simpson is indeed released this fall—which appears almost certainly to be the decision—it will "renew the rabid curiosity" in the case and "produce a spectacle unlike anything we’ve seen since he went behind bars," Nancy Armour at USA Today predicts. He will still receive an NFL pension estimated at $25,000 per month.

**Jamaica Decriminalizes Small Quantities of Marijuana: A Step Towards a Medical Cannabis Industry**

Jamaica has made a significant move towards decriminalizing marijuana, a move that has been decades in the making. The country's parliament has passed a law that makes possession of up to 2 ounces (56.6 grams) of marijuana a petty offense, punishable by a fine rather than a criminal record. This change in law paves the way for the establishment of a licensing authority to regulate the cultivation and distribution of marijuana for medical, scientific, and therapeutic purposes.

For years, Jamaica has been a hub for marijuana cultivation and use, with the drug being culturally entrenched in the island's society. However, the country's laws have consistently prohibited its use and possession, leading to numerous arrests and convictions. The new law aims to correct this historical injustice by allowing the use of marijuana for religious, medical, scientific, and therapeutic purposes.

The legislation also paves the way for the development of a lawful industry for medical ganja and industrial hemp. Jamaican officials hope that the island can become a player in the nascent medical marijuana industry, which has seen significant growth in recent years. Local scientists have already made significant contributions to the development of marijuana-derived products, such as "Canasol," which helps relieve pressure in the eyes of glaucoma patients.

The move is part of a broader international trend towards easing restrictions on marijuana use. More than 20 US states allow some form of medical marijuana, and several countries, including Uruguay, Argentina, and Chile, have decriminalized or legalized marijuana use. Jamaica's decision to decriminalize small quantities of marijuana is seen as a significant step towards joining this trend.

While the new law does not mean that Jamaica will soften its stance on transnational drug trafficking or cultivation of illegal plots, it does mark a significant shift in the country's approach to marijuana use. National security minister Peter Bunting has stated that the security forces will continue to rigorously enforce Jamaican law consistent with international treaty obligations.

The decision is seen as a victory for Jamaican cannabis crusaders, who have been advocating for decriminalization for decades. Delano Seiveright, director of the Cannabis Commercial and Medicinal Taskforce, has said that while the law is a significant step forward, there is still much work to be done to develop a full-fledged medical cannabis industry.

In related news, the US state of Alaska has become the third state to legalize the recreational use of marijuana for adults, while the US assistant
details.ground_truth: Alaska yesterday became the third state to legalize marijuana for recreational use, and now a country where you would have thought that was the case already is moving closer to that goal itself. Jamaica's parliament last night approved a law decriminalizing small amounts of pot, the AP reports. People found with 2 ounces or less of marijuana will now simply receive a ticket—not a crime on their record—and cultivation of five plants or fewer is now allowed. A licensing agency was also established to oversee pot cultivation and distribution for medical and scientific efforts. Rastafarians are rejoicing, because the bill also grants them the legal right to use cannabis for sacramental purposes, while tourists who have medical marijuana prescriptions elsewhere can pay for permits to buy a bit of ganja on the island. Jamaica has shied away from decriminalization because it didn't want to risk violating international treaties and provoking US sanctions, the Guardian reports. The process for the new law was described by National Security Minister Peter Bunting as an "elephantine," nearly 40-year effort, ABC Australia reports. "[The law] eliminates an unnecessary source of friction between police and citizens, and ensures that our young people are not gratuitously shackled with criminal records," he said in a statement. Jamaica also hopes to boost its health tourism and medical marijuana industries and make it a major player in the pot product market, the Guardian notes. International exporting, however, remains a no-no. A US counternarcotics official told the AP in an email that "Jamaican law is of course Jamaica's own business," but warned that drug trafficking into the US is still illegal.

**AirAsia Flight 8501 Crash: Search Teams Locate Wreckage, Investigate Cause**

On December 28, 2014, AirAsia Flight 8501 crashed into the Java Sea, killing all 162 people on board. The Airbus A320 was flying from Surabaya, Indonesia's second-largest city, to Singapore when it disappeared. Search teams, led by the Indonesian National Search and Rescue Agency (BASARNAS), have been scouring the northern Java Sea and coastline of southern Borneo to recover the bodies of victims and locate the wreckage.

**Wreckage Located**

On January 3, 2015, Indonesian officials announced that they had located the wreckage of the plane. A sonar-equipped Indonesian Geological Survey vessel detected four massive objects on the ocean floor, including a 18-meter-long piece of the plane's body. While the wreckage is still underwater, officials are hopeful that it will remain largely intact, which would aid in the investigation.

**Investigation Underway**

The Indonesian Transport Ministry has launched an investigation into the crash, which is believed to have been caused by bad weather. A 14-page report from Indonesia's Meteorology, Climatology and Geophysics Agency suggests that icing, caused by bad weather, may have damaged the plane's engines. The report also states that the pilot did not ask for a weather report from the meteorological agency at the time of take-off.

**Airline's Operating License Suspended**

The Transport Ministry has suspended AirAsia's operating license for the Surabaya to Singapore route, citing a violation of the airline's operating schedule. The airline was only authorized to fly on Mondays, Tuesdays, Thursdays, and Saturdays, but Flight 8501 took off on a Sunday.

**Passenger Recovery Efforts**

Rescue teams have recovered 30 bodies from the sea, but many more are believed to be trapped inside the plane's fuselage. Divers are expected to explore the wreckage once it is safely accessible, with officials hoping to find many of the passengers still strapped in their seats.

**Autopsies to be Conducted**

Autopsies will be carried out on some of the bodies, including the pilot and co-pilot, whose remains have not yet been recovered. The investigation is being led by the Indonesian Transport Ministry, with assistance from international experts.

**Family Members Wait with Bated Breath**

For family members of the victims, the wait has been agonizing. Many have expressed hope that the news of the wreckage's location is true and
details.ground_truth: Search teams in Indonesia may have found the main fuselage of the AirAsia plane where most of the remaining victims and the plane's black boxes are expected to be. Authorities found "four big parts of the plane we're looking for," says the official in charge of the search in the Java Sea, reports Reuters. The biggest of the pieces is about 59 feet long and 18 feet wide, reports AP. The pieces have only been detected, not recovered, and divers hope conditions will permit them to reach the objects tomorrow. So far, only 30 of 162 bodies have been recovered, and most of the victims are believed to be still strapped in their seats. Authorities also said today that AirAsia was in violation of its license by flying to Singapore on a Sunday, the day of the crash, reports the Telegraph. As a result, the airline might have its license revoked in Indonesia. There's still no conclusions yet in what caused the crash, but theories continue to center on stormy conditions. "Flight 8501 appears to have been trapped in bad weather that would have been difficult to avoid," says a report by Indonesia's Meteorology, Climatology and Geophysics Agency.

**Preserving the Web: Internet Archive's Efforts to Fix Broken Links**

The Internet Archive is making significant strides in preserving the web by archiving pages as they are created and referencing the pages they link to. The non-profit organization's goal is to fix all broken links on the web, ensuring that users can access the intended content even if the original link is no longer active.

Through its "No More 404" initiative, the Internet Archive is crawling supported websites to preserve their content and referrals. This means that when a referenced page is changed or removed from the web, a link to its archived version will be preserved. This will not only prevent broken links but also allow users to access the content as it was originally intended by the page's authors.

The Internet Archive's efforts are aimed at maintaining the integrity and accessibility of online content, making it easier for users to navigate and retrieve information from the web. By fixing broken links, the organization is helping to preserve the web's historical record and ensuring that valuable information remains accessible for future generations.

The Internet Archive's innovative approach to preserving the web is a significant step forward in addressing the issue of broken links and maintaining online content. By leveraging its technology and expertise, the organization is working tirelessly to create a more reliable and accessible internet for all users.

**Habeas Corpus: A Fundamental Right in Peril**

Habeas corpus, a legal procedure that safeguards citizens from indefinite detention without cause, has been a cornerstone of Western law since the signing of the Magna Carta in 1215. The Founding Fathers of the United States enshrined it in the Constitution, considering it a fundamental right essential to preserving liberty, justice, and democracy.

However, the Military Commissions Act (MCA) of 2006 compromised this core legal value by revoking the right to habeas corpus for individuals detained at Guantanamo Bay and labeled as "enemy combatants." This provision extends to non-citizens, including legal permanent residents of the U.S., anywhere in the world. The MCA has eroded the foundation of the U.S. legal and constitutional framework, putting everyone's rights at risk.

**Guantanamo Bay and the Detainees**

The government has admitted to holding nearly 800 men at Guantanamo Bay, with little or no evidence against most of them. The majority were sold into captivity by warlords for bounties, and only 10 have ever been charged with a crime. Despite numerous legal victories, the Bush administration has prevented detainees from receiving a fair hearing in court.

**Chimpanzees' Right to Habeas Corpus**

In a groundbreaking decision, a New York judge granted a pair of research chimpanzees, Hercules and Leo, the right to have their day in court through a writ of habeas corpus. This marks the first time an animal has been covered by a writ of habeas corpus in U.S. history, recognizing them as legal persons. The ruling could force the university to release the chimps and set a precedent for other research animals.

**Implications and Future Battles**

The Nonhuman Rights Project, which filed the case, sees this victory as a significant step towards gaining bodily liberty for cognitively complex animals. The organization plans to file another case involving a captive elephant and aims to sway judges in other jurisdictions using the habeas corpus ruling. The implications of this decision extend beyond animal rights, as it challenges the notion of personhood and raises questions about the treatment of non-human entities.

**Consequences for All**

The erosion of habeas corpus rights threatens not only the detainees at Guantanamo Bay but also the rights of all citizens. If we do not defend this fundamental right, we risk undermining the foundation of our legal and constitutional framework. The case of Hercules and Leo highlights the importance of reevaluating our treatment of non-human entities
details.ground_truth: The writ of habeas corpus lets prisoners appear in court to make their captors justify why they're being held. Until now in the US, those who've used this legal tactic have been human. But a New York judge yesterday ruled that the writ may be used by two lab chimpanzees at Stony Brook University, Science magazine reports. NY Supreme Court Justice Barbara Jaffe ordered a university rep to address a suit filed by the Nonhuman Rights Project by appearing in court May 6 to argue why the college is holding Hercules and Leo. The group, which says the animals are being detained for biomedical experiments, says its ultimate goal is to have the animals released to the Save the Chimps sanctuary in Fort Pierce, Fla., per the NhRP's website. "This is a big step forward to getting what we are ultimately seeking: the right to bodily liberty for chimpanzees and other cognitively complex animals," Natalie Prosin, the project's executive director, tells Science. The case kicked off in December 2013 with lawsuits that also included two chimps held on private property; each case was struck down, sending the NhRP down the appeals path. Not everyone's convinced the upcoming hearing will mean liberation for the chimps. A Pepperdine University law professor tells Science that the judge likely just wants to hear both sides of the story: "It would be quite surprising if the judge intended to make a momentous substantive finding that chimpanzees are legal persons" without doing so, he notes. Prosin, meanwhile, says her group isn't stopping with the chimps and will also target the release of other animals. (Click to read about what chimps talk to each other about.)

**Deadly Shooting on the Las Vegas Strip Leaves Three Dead and Multiple Injured**

A deadly shooting occurred on the Las Vegas Strip early Thursday morning, leaving three people dead and at least six injured. The incident began with a dispute at a nearby hotel, where two vehicles, a silver-gray Maserati and a black Range Rover SUV, were involved in the altercation. The Maserati sped off at a high rate of speed and the SUV followed, firing shots at the Maserati, which then crashed into a taxi cab that burst into flames.

**Victims and Suspects Identified**

The driver of the Maserati, Kenneth Wayne Cherry Jr., 27, was identified as one of the victims. He was an aspiring rapper and had a passion for cars. The taxi driver, Michael Boldon, was also killed in the incident. The passenger in the Maserati was injured and taken to the hospital for treatment. The suspects in the SUV were described as armed and dangerous, and police have contacted authorities in three neighboring states to locate the vehicle.

**Investigation Ongoing**

The Las Vegas Police Department is investigating the incident, and police have video from traffic cameras at the intersection where the shooting occurred. They are also checking hotel surveillance systems to determine the cause of the altercation and the identities of the suspects. The police have not yet released any information on the suspects or their possible motives.

**Impact on the City**

The shooting has caused significant disruptions to the city, with Las Vegas Boulevard closed for hours and traffic snarled in the area. Tourists and locals alike were shocked by the incident, with many expressing their concern for safety on the Strip. However, the police have reassured the public that the area is still safe and that the incident was an isolated event.

**Recent Violent Incidents on the Strip**

This incident marks the latest in a series of violent incidents on the Las Vegas Strip, including a shooting at a parking garage on February 6 and a stabbing in an elevator at The Hotel at Mandalay Bay. The police have made arrests or identified suspects in each of these cases, and authorities are working to ensure the safety of visitors and residents alike.

**Community Response**

The community has come together to support the families of the victims and to raise awareness about the importance of safety on the Strip. The Professional Drivers Association has donated $5,000 to the Boldon family, and the Nevada Department of Business and Industry is assisting with the investigation.
details.ground_truth: Police in three states are still hunting for a Range Rover whose occupants opened fire on another car on the Vegas Strip this morning and set off a deadly chain reaction. Cops don't know most of the details, but they say it started with a dispute of some kind in one of the nearby hotels, then spilled into the street, reports the Las Vegas Sun. When the Range Rover caught up to a Maserati at a stop light, shots were fired from the SUV that sent the Maserati crashing into a taxi, which exploded upon impact. Three people—the Maserati driver, the taxi driver, and a taxi passenger—were killed, and three others were injured, reports the LA Times. Police are hoping to piece together more details by interviewing a passenger in the Maserati, who was injured in the melee. Earlier reports that gunfire came from both vehicles as part of a moving shootout were incorrect. The AP collects this quote from a tourist visiting from Manchester, England: "This doesn't happen where we come from, not on this scale. We get stabbings, and gang violence, but this is like something out of a movie. Like Die Hard or something."

**Survival Story: Teenager Autumn Veatch Found Alive After Small-Plane Crash**

In a miraculous turn of events, 16-year-old Autumn Veatch was found alive after a small-plane crash in the rugged North Cascades mountains in Washington state. Autumn, a Bellingham High School junior, was one of three passengers on the plane that went missing on Saturday afternoon. The plane, a Beech A-35, was traveling from Kalispell, Montana, to Lynden, Washington, but lost contact with air traffic control near Omak, Washington.

Autumn survived the crash and was forced to spend two days in the wilderness before finding her way out. She followed a drainage down to a river, then the river, via a trail, downstream to the nearest road, Highway 20. She eventually caught a ride with hikers to the nearest community, Mazama, where she contacted authorities.

Autumn was found on Monday afternoon, dehydrated and suffering from minor injuries. She was taken to Three Rivers Hospital in Brewster, where she was treated for rhabdomyolysis, a breakdown of muscle tissue. Despite her ordeal, Autumn seemed happy to see her family and friends, who had been searching for her.

The fate of Autumn's two step-grandparents, Leland and Sharon Bowman, who were also on the plane, remains unclear. Authorities have not confirmed their conditions, but their plane has not been found. The search for the wreckage continues, aided by Autumn's description of the crash site.

Autumn's survival story is a testament to her resourcefulness and determination. Despite having little outdoor training, she used her wits to find her way out of the mountains and contact authorities. Her story has brought joy to her family and friends, who had been searching for her and feared the worst.

The crash has also highlighted the challenges of searching for missing persons in rugged terrain. The North Cascades mountains are known for their difficult terrain, making it a challenging search area. However, with the help of Autumn's description of the crash site and the use of technology, searchers were able to locate her and bring her to safety.

Autumn's story has also sparked a sense of community, with friends and family coming together to support her and her family during this difficult time. As Autumn begins her recovery, she will be surrounded by loved ones who are grateful for her safe return.
details.ground_truth: A 16-year-old girl in Washington state is bruised and dehydrated but very much alive after surviving a plane crash and a long trek through what authorities describe as some of the state's most rugged terrain. Autumn Veatch was on a small plane with her step-grandparents that crashed after departing Kalispell, Mont., on Saturday afternoon, the Bellingham Herald reports. There was no sign of the plane or its occupants until a motorist found Veatch on State Route 20 yesterday afternoon and took her to a general store, where employees called authorities, the AP reports. She was hospitalized with minor injuries. Her father tells the Herald that after the plane crashed into a mountain, it caught fire and she was unable to get her step-grandparents out. She spent about a day near the plane waiting for rescue before following a stream and then a trail out of the woods, her father says. Friends say both father and daughter are completely overwhelmed. "She did joke that it was a good thing she'd watched all those Survivor shows that she didn't like, but her dad made her watch anyway," a family friend tells the Seattle Times. Authorities are still searching for the missing Beech A-35. (In Colombia last month, a mother and baby survived four days in the jungle after a plane crash.)

**Environment, Music, and Technology: Recent Developments**

In the past few months, there have been several significant events in the realms of environmental regulation, music, and technology.

**Environmental Regulation: The Lacey Act**

The U.S. Justice Department has been cracking down on companies that allegedly import and trade in endangered wood, particularly ebony from Madagascar. In a recent raid, federal marshals seized wood from the Gibson Guitar Corporation in Tennessee, which could be charged with trafficking in illegally obtained wood under the Lacey Act. The law, amended in 2008, prohibits the trade in endangered plants and animals. Companies are required to certify the legality of their supply chain, and the Environmental Investigation Agency (EIA) has concluded that Gibson knowingly imported tainted wood.

**Music Industry: The Impact of the Lacey Act**

The Lacey Act has had a significant impact on the music industry, particularly guitar makers. Many companies, including Gibson, have been forced to re-examine their supply chains and source wood from sustainable sources. However, some companies are concerned about the retroactive nature of the law, which could require them to prove the provenance of wood acquired decades ago. Guitar dealers and owners are also worried about the law's requirements, which could lead to a "nightmare" of compliance.

**Technology: The Wayback Machine**

In a separate development, Alexa Internet has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996. The data is added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period, providing a valuable resource for researchers and historians. The Wayback Machine has become an essential tool for understanding the evolution of the internet and its impact on society.

**Key Takeaways**

* The U.S. Justice Department is cracking down on companies that import and trade in endangered wood under the Lacey Act.
* The Lacey Act has had a significant impact on the music industry, particularly guitar makers, who are required to prove the provenance of wood acquired decades ago.
* The Wayback Machine has become an essential tool for understanding the evolution of the internet and its impact on society.
* The Lacey Act aims to reduce illegal logging and promote sustainable sourcing, but its implementation has been met with challenges and unintended consequences.

**Conclusion**

These recent developments highlight the complex relationships between environment, music, and technology. As the world grapples with the challenges of sustainability and compliance, companies and individuals must adapt to new regulations and technologies. The Lacey Act and the Wayback Machine are two examples of the impact of these changes on different industries and aspects of society
details.ground_truth: Allegations of illegal wood imports prompted the Justice Department to raid Gibson Guitar facilities in Tennessee last week. NPR aired the complicated story involving the 100-year old Lacey Act, which prohibits imports of endangered species, including plants, into the US. No formal charges have been pressed, but it appears the issue lies with Gibson possibly importing banned Madagascar ebony. Gibson insists the wood confiscated by marshals was legally acquired rosewood from India. Adding some gusto to the story, right-leaning sites assert that Gibson rival CF Martin uses the same wood in some of its guitars, but the company was never investigated. The Landmark Report thinks that's fishy, given that Martin's CEO is a Democratic donor and Gibson's CEO is a GOP backer. Raising the octave even higher, it is possible Michelle Obama ran afoul of the Lacey Act when she presented France's Carla Bruni with a Gibson guitar that may have contained banned wood. However the story plays out, many are frustrated with the retroactive aspect of the law. "It's a nightmare," says a dealer. "I can't help it if they used Brazilian rosewood on almost every guitar made prior to 1970."

**Middle East Conflict Escalates as Israel and Hamas Trade Fire**

A deadly conflict between Israel and Hamas has escalated in recent days, with both sides trading fire and suffering losses. The violence began on Wednesday, when Israel launched a military operation in Gaza, targeting Hamas militants and sites in the coastal territory. Hamas responded with a barrage of rockets fired into Israel, including one that landed near Jerusalem for the first time in decades.

**Israel Prepares for Possible Ground Invasion**

In preparation for a possible ground invasion, Israel's cabinet has authorized the mobilization of up to 75,000 reservists. The move comes as the Israeli military continues to pound Hamas sites in Gaza with airstrikes and artillery shells. The conflict has killed at least 30 Palestinians and three Israelis, with dozens more injured.

**Egypt's Role in the Conflict**

Egypt's President Mohamed Morsi has ordered his prime minister to lead a delegation to Gaza, in an effort to mediate a truce between Israel and Hamas. The visit is seen as a significant development, as Egypt has traditionally served as a mediator in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. However, the Egyptian role in the conflict has been complicated by the rise of the Muslim Brotherhood, which has close ties to Hamas.

**International Response**

The international community has urged both sides to de-escalate the conflict. The United States has called on Hamas to stop its rocket attacks, while the United Nations has condemned Israel's military action. The UK has issued a statement of support for Israel, while other countries have condemned the violence.

**Casualties and Damage**

The conflict has resulted in significant casualties and damage on both sides. In Gaza, at least 30 Palestinians have been killed, including five children and a pregnant teenager. In Israel, three Israelis were killed in a rocket strike on Thursday, with dozens more injured. Hamas has fired over 300 rockets into Israel, with many intercepted by the country's Iron Dome missile defense system.

**Possible Outcomes**

The conflict has raised concerns about a possible ground invasion by Israel, which could have significant consequences for both sides. The situation remains volatile, with both sides trading fire and suffering losses. The role of Egypt in the conflict will be critical in determining the outcome, as the country seeks to mediate a truce between Israel and Hamas.

**Key Players**

* Benjamin Netanyahu, Prime Minister of Israel
* Mohamed Morsi, President of Egypt
* Hamas, Palestinian militant group and political movement
* Israel Defense Forces, Israeli military
* United States, international community

details.ground_truth: A brief, planned ceasefire failed to materialize in Gaza today, as death continued to rain down on the strip. Israel had offered to hold its offensive to accommodate a visit from Egyptian Prime Minister Hesham Kandil, provided Hamas held its fire as well. But Hamas said it would keep firing, and one Israeli defense minister complained that 50 rockets had flown in from Gaza during Kandil's visit, CNN reports. A Hamas-run TV station reported that Israel had kept firing as well, but Israel says it took a break for at least two hours. In Palestine the death toll has risen to 21, including 13 civilians, Reuters reports. Among those civilians were seven children and a pregnant teenager. Israel's death toll held steady at 3, despite the hundreds of rockets fired into Israel. But Hamas did up the ante by firing rockets near Tel Aviv for the first time. Air-raid sirens sounded (the last time they did so was during the Gulf War) and civilians ran for cover, though the missiles exploded harmlessly, with one going into the sea. "There will be a price for that escalation," Ehud Barak vowed, according to the New York Times. Israel has now hit 250 Gaza targets, while taking further steps toward a ground invasion, calling up 16,000 reservists, the Wall Street Journal reports. It's also steadily sending troops and armored vehicles to the border, notes CNN—between 1,500 and 2,000 are already there, one official said. Israel claims the attacks have weakened Hamas' military capabilities and caused its attacks to lighten, but Hamas denied that, saying it had hit multiple Israeli targets today. Kandil emerged from his meeting with Hamas expressing solidarity, saying the new Egyptian regime would be more active in helping the Palestinians. "The time has changed," he said. "No longer the Israeli occupation will be able to carry out their attacks against the Palestinians without being held responsible. That time is far bygone." For its part, the Obama administration says it's seeking help from Arab countries in calling on Hamas to relent. "The onus rests squarely on Hamas ... to stop its rocket attacks," says a State Department spokesman. UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-Moon is planning diplomatic visits to Jerusalem, Ramallah, and Cairo within days.

**Rabies Outbreak in Maine: Woman Fights for Her Life After Rabid Raccoon Attack**

A recent surge in rabies cases has left residents in Maine on high alert. In two separate incidents, a woman and a man were attacked by rabid animals, highlighting the dangers of this deadly disease.

**Incident 1: Rachel Borch's Terrifying Encounter**

On June 3, 21-year-old Rachel Borch was jogging on a trail near her home in Hope when she encountered a ferocious-looking raccoon charging at her with its teeth bared. Borch, who had nothing to protect herself with, was forced to use her bare hands to defend herself. The raccoon latched onto her thumb, and she was able to drown it in a puddle of muddy water by holding its head underwater. Despite the traumatic experience, Borch was able to escape without serious injury.

However, the raccoon later tested positive for rabies, and Borch has since received six shots as a precautionary measure. Her family has also noted that if there hadn't been water on the ground, Borch may not have been able to defend herself.

**Incident 2: Man Bitten by Rabid Fox**

Earlier this week, a Wiscasset man was bitten on both hands by what is believed to be a rabid fox in Topsham. This incident highlights the importance of taking precautions when interacting with wildlife.

**Rabies Outbreak in Maine**

According to the Maine Center for Disease Control, there have been 20 animals that have tested positive for rabies in 2017, including raccoons, red foxes, and skunks. In 2016, 64 animals tested positive for the disease. The CDC warns that infected animals typically start showing signs within two weeks, and humans can start exhibiting symptoms within a few weeks or months.

**Prevention and Precautions**

Hope Animal Control Officer Heidi Blood emphasizes the importance of taking precautions when interacting with wildlife. She notes that even dead animals can carry the rabies virus, which can live outside the body for 24 hours. Blood advises people and their pets to avoid animals that appear delirious or drunk-like, as they may be infected.

Under Maine law, cats and dogs must be vaccinated against rabies, even if they stay indoors. The CDC also recommends avoiding contact with wild animals, especially those that appear to be acting strangely.

**Conclusion**

The recent surge in rabies cases in Maine serves as a reminder of the importance
details.ground_truth: Rachel Borch was out for a jog in the woods near her home in Hope, Maine, when a "ferocious-looking" attacker with beady eyes and tiny teeth made a beeline for her, reports the Camden Herald. Borch, 21, knew immediately that something was wrong with the raccoon that was charging toward her. "Imagine the Tasmanian devil," she tells the Bangor Daily News of the "terrifying" June 3 incident. Yanking out her headphones, she began "dancing" around the animal on the narrow path, but she tells the Herald she knew that, one way or another, the animal was going to bite her. She figured her hands would be the best spot, so she offered them up. The raccoon chomped down on her thumb and stayed there, scratching at her legs and arms as she screamed. Now on her knees, she spotted her phone lying submerged in a mud puddle, and had an idea: drown it. "With my thumb in its mouth, I just pushed its head down into the muck," she tells the News. When the raccoon finally stopped moving, she yanked out her finger and raced the three-quarters of a mile home. Two days later, the state confirmed the animal was infected with rabies. Borch got rabies shots, and an animal control officer adds an unpleasant footnote: The infected raccoon may not be alone. "Not to scare people," Heidi Blood tells the News, but "when there’s one, there’s typically another." (Normally nocturnal like raccoons, a crazed beaver attacked a woman paddle-boarder.)

Recently, NASA scientists have made significant discoveries about the history of Mars, providing new insights into the planet's past and its potential for supporting life.

According to a study published in the journal Science, evidence suggests that a massive ancient ocean once covered nearly half of the northern hemisphere of Mars, making the planet a more promising place for alien life to have gained a foothold. The ocean, which was a mile deep in places and held 20 million cubic kilometers of water, would have been equivalent to the size of the Atlantic Ocean on Earth. The study used infrared maps to show that water near the Martian ice caps is enriched with deuterium, a heavy isotope of hydrogen, indicating that Mars lost a vast amount of water in the past.

The researchers calculated that the amount of water was enough to create a global ocean that covered the entire surface of Mars to a depth of 137 meters. However, the ocean probably never covered the entire planet and instead pooled into a much deeper ocean in the low-lying northern plains. The study provides an answer to a long-standing question about how much water Mars had when it was young and how it lost that water.

Further analysis of the Martian atmosphere revealed that the planet was once a warm and wet world, with streams, winding river deltas, and long-standing lakes. The scientists used data from three of the most powerful infrared telescopes in the world to study the Martian atmosphere over six years and found that the concentration of deuterium in water left behind on Mars is higher than on Earth.

The findings imply that Mars was habitable in the distant past, and the search for life on Mars will continue with the European Space Agency's Exomars rover, which will be sent to the red planet in 2018. The rover will look for chemical signatures of life, possibly emanating from microbes living deep beneath the Martian soil.

While the study suggests that life was possible on Mars, it does not make it inevitable. Astronomers estimate that the ocean lasted for millions of years, but over time, the Martian atmosphere thinned, and the planet lost much of its insulation. The ocean receded and eventually froze, leaving behind the Martian polar caps.

The study's findings are consistent with previous research, which has shown that Mars had standing water for 1.5 billion years, longer than it took for life to emerge on Earth. The evidence continues to mount that Mars was once far wetter and warmer than it is today, making it a more promising place for life to have existed.

James Kasting,
details.ground_truth: Imagine all the water in the Arctic Ocean. Now, imagine all that and more on Mars. If you visited the Red Planet roughly 4.3 billion years ago, that's what you likely would have found, say NASA scientists. Their new study arrived at that "solid estimate ... by determining how much water was lost to space," says lead author Geronimo Villanueva. The Guardian reports the scientists used three powerful infrared telescopes to analyze two forms of water in Mars' atmosphere: H2O and HDO, in which deuterium (aka "heavy" hydrogen) has taken the place of one hydrogen atom. As National Geographic explains, Mars' gravity is weaker than our own; that allowed hydrogen to escape from the atmosphere and into space over time, boosting the amount of deuterium in its water. Scientists compared the ratio of HDO to H2O in Martian water today with that in water from a 4.5 billion-year-old Mars meteorite to arrive at their conclusions, a press release states. They ultimately calculated that Mars was once home to 20 million cubic kilometers of ocean. It could have covered every inch of the planet in water 450 feet deep, but the scientists think a different scenario is more likely: that the ocean covered roughly a fifth of the planet, was located in the northern hemisphere, and was as much as a mile deep. While National Geographic observes the findings back up "reams of earlier evidence that water once existed on the surface" of Mars, another study author points out the assessment of the degree of water lost indicates something new. "The planet was very likely wet for a longer period of time than was previously thought, suggesting it might have been habitable for longer," a scientist says. (Mysterious plumes on Mars are stumping scientists.)

**UNIVERSITY RESCUES STUDENT AND FAMILY FROM ISIS ONSLAUGHT**

In 2014, Swedish chemistry professor Charlotta Turner received a desperate text message from her graduate student Firas Jumaah, who was hiding with his family in a disused bleach factory in Iraq. Jumaah, a Yazidi from Iraq, had initially entered the war zone after his wife called to say that ISIS fighters had taken over their next-door village, killing all the men and enslaving the women.

Turner, who was Jumaah's PhD supervisor at Lund University, was shocked by the situation and immediately got in touch with the university's security chief, Per Gustafson. Gustafson arranged a rescue operation, hiring a security company to extract Jumaah and his family from the war zone.

The rescue operation was a success, with two Landcruisers carrying heavily-armed mercenaries driving into the area where Jumaah was hiding and speeding him and his family away to Erbil Airport. Jumaah credited Turner with saving his life, saying he felt "privileged" and "VIP" during the rescue, but also like a "coward" for leaving his mother and sisters behind.

Turner's actions were not just a personal gesture of goodwill, but also a testament to the university's commitment to its students' safety and well-being. As Gustafson noted, "It was a unique event. As far as I know, no other university has ever been involved in anything like it."

Jumaah and his family were eventually reunited in Sweden, where Jumaah completed his PhD and now works for a pharmaceuticals company in Malmö. The family has almost finished paying the university back for the rescue operation.

Turner's actions in 2014 were sparked by a phone call with Jumaah, who was crying and desperate for help. As Turner recalled, "He was very sad and crying... I could hear that the situation was hopeless and they had to flee." Turner's response was immediate and decisive, as she contacted Gustafson and arranged the rescue operation.

The story of Charlotta Turner and Firas Jumaah's rescue is a remarkable one, highlighting the importance of academic institutions taking responsibility for their students' safety and well-being, even in the face of extreme danger.
details.ground_truth: Four years ago, a chemistry professor got a text from her grad student: If I'm not back in a week, cut me from the doctoral program. Charlotta Turner called him right away: "He was very sad and crying,” the 48-year-old prof at Lund University in Sweden tells NBC News. "I could hear that the situation was hopeless and they had to flee." The student, Firas Jumaah, was visiting his native Iraq to help family members during a brutal 2014 ISIS attack targeting Yazidis—a religious minority that includes his family. The terror group had just enslaved and massacred Yazidis by the thousand in nearby Sinjar. Now Jumaah and family were planning to flee to the mountains. "I had no hope at all," says Jumaah, per the Local. "I was desperate." But Turner took action. She spoke to Lund University's then-security chief, who contacted a company that sent mercenaries into northern Iraq. Only days later, four armed mercs on two Landcruisers blazed into the place where Jumaah was hiding, and rushed him to Erbil Airport with his wife and two young kids. "I have never felt so privileged, so VIP," he says. "But at the same time I felt like a coward as I left my mother and sisters behind me." Seeing his colleagues back in Sweden, he was speechless: "I just cried," he says. Yet Jumaah finished his PhD and found work at a Malmo pharmaceuticals company, and his family survived. The bill: roughly 60,000 kroner ($6,613), which his family has nearly finished paying. “If they told me to pay 200,000 kronor, I would,” says Jumaah. (The UN is finding fresh ISIS horrors.)

**Armed Jewelry Store Robberies Span Multiple States**

A string of armed jewelry store robberies has taken place across the Southeastern United States, with the most recent incident occurring on Monday at a Jared Vault in Mebane, North Carolina. Authorities believe the robberies are connected to a female suspect, described as white, in her 20s or 30s, 5-foot-8 and 130 pounds. She is considered armed and dangerous.

The movie "Focus" has received mixed reviews from critics. The film, starring Will Smith and Margot Robbie, follows a con artist and his young apprentice as they navigate the world of high-stakes scams. Critics have praised Smith's performance, noting that he has been allowed to play to his strengths as a charming leading man, a role he hasn't had since 2005's "Hitch." Smith's character, Nicky Spurgeon, is a suave and confident con artist who takes on a young apprentice, Jess Barrett, played by Robbie.

The film's directors, Glenn Ficarra and John Requa, have managed to craft a movie that approaches the modest goals and effortless allure of a classic Hitchcock film. The movie's tone is lighthearted and playful, with a focus on the con artist's charm and wit. Critics have noted that the film's success lies in its ability to balance humor and drama, creating a fun and entertaining ride for the audience.

However, some critics have also pointed out that the film's plot is absurd and ridiculous at times, with a lack of cohesion and logic. The movie's resolution is also criticized for being absurd and ludicrous. Despite this, the film has received praise for its performances, particularly from Smith and Robbie, who bring a youthful energy to the film.

The casting of the film was a long and arduous process, with several actresses vying for the role of Jess Barrett. The film's producers finally settled on Margot Robbie, who brought a modern spin to the character. The film's mix of longtime star and up-and-coming actress has been praised as a tasty element in the success of the movie.

In an exclusive interview, critic Mick LaSalle noted that the film's emotional core, the relationship between Nicky and Jess, lacks impact, mostly because the audience can't believe a word they say. However, LaSalle also praised the film's ability to balance humor and drama, creating a fun and entertaining ride for the audience.

Overall, "Focus" is a fun and entertaining movie that relies on the charm and wit of its lead actors. While it has its flaws, the film's lighthearted and playful tone makes it a great watch for audiences looking for a fun summer movie.
details.ground_truth: Will Smith stars as Nicky, an established con man who takes on a new apprentice, Australian newcomer Margot Robbie's Jess, in Focus. They both love the con game, but who's playing who? Here's what critics are saying: Richard Corliss at Time is a fan. "The mix of longtime star and minx on the rise is one tasty element in the success of a movie that approaches the modest goals and effortless allure of a 60-year-old Hitchcock," he writes. Smith "takes a welcome break from glowering sci-fi roles" and writer-directors Glenn Ficarra and John Requa "deserve some credit in letting Will be Will in the star's first charm barrage since 2005's Hitch." Steven Rea at the Philadelphia Inquirer admits Smith "shows some of the movie-star wattage that's been missing from his recent pics. He charms." There's also "combustible chemistry" between Smith and Robbie, but the movie just "disappoints," Rea writes. "Even if you're willing to forgive its sinkhole plotholes and farthest-fetched conceits, the film ... ultimately makes no sense." Betsy Sharkey, on the other hand, says this "rom-com-con" is "an irresistible reminder of all the reasons we first fell for the Fresh Prince so many years ago." Writing at the Los Angeles Times, she notes the romantic role of Nicky fits Smith perfectly, while Robbie is "more than holding her own" as his match. "The scams are Rubik's Cube complicated, but what keeps you guessing is whether the romantic connection between Jess and Nicky is real or just another con." But Mick LaSalle at the San Francisco Chronicle isn't convinced. In a review titled "Will Smith just stole $12 from your pocket," he argues Focus is "ridiculous in every detail. It's a movie with no truth that teaches nothing and shows nothing, that has only its audacity to recommend it." Nicky and Jess lack "impact," partly because "Smith is not a strong leading man," he says. Viewers are left "waiting for the trick, which is not the same as being fooled."

**Mega Millions Jackpot Reaches Record-Breaking $636 Million**

The Mega Millions jackpot has reached a staggering $636 million, making it one of the largest lottery prizes in U.S. history. The prize was estimated to be $586 million on Monday, but a surge in ticket sales pushed it to a record-breaking figure. The jackpot is now the largest in the US history, just shy of the record $656 million Mega Millions prize won in March 2012.

**Winning the Jackpot: What to Do**

If you're the lucky winner, here are some essential steps to take:

1. **Sign the ticket**: Make sure to sign the ticket to prevent anyone else from claiming the prize.
2. **Take a selfie**: Document the moment with a photo of yourself and the ticket.
3. **Find a safe place to store the ticket**: Consider stashing the ticket in a safe deposit box or a portable home safe.
4. **Seek professional advice**: Consult with an experienced attorney and a certified financial planner to help you make informed decisions about the prize.
5. **Plan a trip**: If you want to stay out of the spotlight, consider taking a trip to a destination like South America or the U.S. Virgin Islands.

**Don't Give Up: Check for Smaller Prizes**

Even if you didn't win the top prize, you might still be eligible for smaller prizes, including the $1 million runner-up prize. Check your tickets again to see if you might have won a smaller prize.

**Odds of Winning**

According to Virginia Lottery Director Paula Otto, the odds of winning are in favor of the winner, with 65-75% of possible number combinations covered by ticket sales. However, she notes that you don't know you have a winner until 100% of the combinations are covered.

**Record-Breaking Jackpot**

The current jackpot is the largest in the week before Christmas, with a record-breaking $586 million on Monday. The prize is expected to continue growing until the next drawing on Friday, reaching at least $950 million if no one wins.

**Prize Options**

If you choose to take the lump-sum cash option, you'll receive $316 million. However, if you opt for the 30-year annuity, the prize will be distributed over 30 annual payments.

The Mega Millions jackpot has captivated the nation, with many hoping to win the record-breaking prize. Will you be the lucky winner?

details.ground_truth: They volunteered to fight fires, but authorities say they were also purposely setting fires over a period of almost two years. After a tip led to a yearlong investigation, 10 volunteer firefighters in North Carolina's Robeson County were arrested Tuesday and charged with arson and related crimes, ABC 11 reports. **Global Chocolate Battle: EU Ruling on Kit Kat's Shape**

A long-running legal dispute between global chocolate giants Nestlé and Mondelez (the parent company of Cadbury) has taken another turn in the European Court of Justice. The court has dismissed appeals from both companies, sending the case back to the EU's trademark office for further review.

The dispute revolves around the shape of Nestlé's Kit Kat finger wafers, which the company has been fighting to trademark in the EU since 2002. The shape, a four-fingered wafer bar, is a distinctive feature of the Kit Kat brand, but Mondelez, which owns Cadbury, has argued that it is not unique enough to be trademarked.

In 2012, the EU trademark office granted Nestlé the trademark, but Mondelez appealed, arguing that the shape is not distinctive enough to be protected. A lower EU court ruled in 2016 that the trademark office was wrong to reject Mondelez's complaint, but Nestlé appealed, arguing that the shape is distinctive enough to be trademarked across the EU.

The European Court of Justice has now ruled that Nestlé has failed to show that consumers in enough EU countries recognize the shape as distinctive. However, the court has also dismissed Mondelez's appeal against some of the grounds for the lower EU court ruling, leaving the door open for the trademark agency to consider new evidence and potentially preserve protection for Kit Kat's shape.

The ruling has implications for brands operating across the EU single market, where there are varying histories in national markets. The case has also been mirrored by a dispute between the two multinationals in the British courts, where Nestlé objected to a trademark for the purple color used by Cadbury to wrap its Dairy Milk chocolate bars.

The outcome of the case is not yet clear, but it is likely to have significant implications for the chocolate industry in the EU. The ruling could potentially open the door to copycat products, but it could also pave the way for new trademark protections for distinctive shapes and designs.

**Related Developments:**

* The Norwegian company Kvikk Lunsj, which has been making a similar four-fingered wafer bar since 1937, may benefit from the ruling, as it has been embroiled in the dispute over Kit Kat's shape.
* Lindt, the Swiss chocolatier, lost a trademark battle over its Easter bunnies with a red ribbon, which are iconic in many countries but not all.
* Tobler
details.ground_truth: "Four trapezoidal bars aligned on a rectangular base" may not be instantly recognizable. Put that setup in chocolate form, however, and many people would be able to distinguish it as a KitKat. Even if that's the case, the European Union's highest court just ruled that the shape of the sweet treat isn't unique enough to merit trademark protection, meaning other confectioners can potentially produce KitKat knockoffs, the Guardian reports. Nestle, which makes KitKats, has been fighting Cadbury (and then Mondelez, which ultimately bought Cadbury) in the courts for more than a decade on this issue. The BBC notes that Nestle first applied for trademark protection for the candy's shape in 2002, and it was granted that protection four years later. Cadbury's pushback began in 2007, and Wednesday's decision by the European Court of Justice essentially tells the EU trademark office it now has to "reconsider" its original decision. What that means, however, is that if the EU Intellectual Property Office finds, based on more recent evidence, that the KitKat bar's shape is recognizable in enough countries, it can retain the trademark protection, per Reuters. The brand name itself of "KitKat" is not being contested. (Atari accused Nestle of ripping off one of its popular video games in an ad for KitKat.)

**Dinosaurs' Roars: A Sound Revolution**

For decades, the image of a roaring Tyrannosaurus Rex has dominated our imagination. However, new research suggests that dinosaurs may not have sounded as ferocious as we think. According to a study published in the journal Evolution, dinosaurs likely made sounds similar to those of birds, with low, throaty whooshes of air rather than high-pitched screams.

Scientists from the University of Texas, Midwestern University, and other institutions analyzed the vocalizations of birds and crocodiles to understand the evolution of closed-mouth vocalization. This behavior, where birds make sounds with their beaks closed, is rare in small birds but common in larger species. The researchers discovered that closed-mouth vocalization has evolved at least 16 times in archosaurs, a group that includes birds, dinosaurs, and crocodiles.

The study's lead author, Tobias Riede, suggests that the capacity for closed-mouth vocalization was present in some extinct nonavian dinosaurs, potentially used for mating displays. This idea is supported by the fact that many dinosaurs had large body sizes, similar to those of modern birds that use closed-mouth vocalization.

The research also highlights the importance of understanding how living birds vocalize to make sense of what nonavian dinosaurs sounded like. According to Julia Clarke, a co-author of the study, "To make any kind of sense of what nonavian dinosaurs sounded like, we need to understand how living birds vocalize." This new perspective on dinosaurs' sounds is a far cry from the roaring T-Rex we're familiar with, and suggests a more nuanced and complex picture of these prehistoric creatures.

In contrast to the traditional depiction of dinosaurs, a study on the sound design of Jurassic Park reveals that the movie's sound effects were created using a range of animal noises, from baby elephants to geese. Sound designer Gary Rydstrom used a combination of animal recordings and creative manipulation to create the iconic sounds of the film.

The study also notes that the sound of dinosaurs is still a mystery, with many species yet to be discovered. As Chad Eliason, a co-author of the study, said, "Looking at the distribution of closed-mouth vocalization in birds that are alive today could tell us how dinosaurs vocalized." Further research is needed to shed light on this fascinating topic and to create a more accurate picture of the sounds of the ancient world.

In conclusion, the study of dinosaurs' sounds is a rapidly evolving field, with new research challenging our traditional understanding of these prehistoric
details.ground_truth: Dinosaurs may have been much more like modern birds than we knew—and not just because some had feathers. A new study suggests that mighty dinosaurs of yore didn't roar, contrary to every dinosaur movie you've ever seen. Instead, they made a decidedly less scary sound called a "closed-mouth vocalization." Think of a dove cooing or perhaps an ostrich doing this grunting thing. As the scientists explain in the journal Evolution, per UT News, the sound actually comes out of the neck area after air is pushed through an esophageal pouch. In fact, the animal's mouth is shut the whole time. Lots of birds do this today, and because birds descended from dinosaurs, scientists suspect they may have communicated in a similar way. The fossil record can't prove it, though the Washington Post notes that previous research meshes with the idea, including the belief that dinosaurs had air sacs instead of vocal chords. “A cool thing about this work is the demonstration that closed-mouth behavior evolved many times,” says the lead author. “That suggests it can emerge fairly easily and be incorporated into mating displays" among different types of animals. A co-author says the study gives the impression of "a very different Jurassic world. Not only were dinosaurs feathered, but they may have had bulging necks and made booming, closed-mouth sounds." But if so, the makers of Jurassic Park didn't get it all wrong: The hiss of the film's velociraptors came from a goose, per Vulture. (Scientists have grown chickens with dinosaur legs.)
