**Convicted White-Collar Criminal Sentenced for Staged Insurance Scam and Conspiracy to Commit Wire Fraud**

A 43-year-old convicted white-collar criminal, Shannon Egeland, was recently sentenced to an additional three years and ten months in prison after he staged an insurance scam by persuading his 17-year-old son to shoot him in the leg on July 31, 2014. Egeland was originally sentenced to a 10-year term for his role in a high-profile mortgage fraud case, but the staged shooting was intended to delay his prison sentence and benefit from a disability insurance policy.

Egeland, who lost his left leg in the shooting, appeared in court on a prosthetic limb and expressed remorse for his actions, stating that the pain was on his son and that he would haunt him for the rest of his life. However, prosecutors described Egeland as a "menace to society" who was motivated by greed and recklessly manipulated his son.

The insurance scam was part of a larger scheme in which Egeland had applied for disability insurance just a week before the shooting, falsely claiming that he had never been arrested or indicted for a felony or misdemeanor in the past 10 years. The scam was quickly detected, but Egeland's actions caused significant emotional and psychological harm to his son.

Egeland has a history of white-collar crime, having previously been convicted of mortgage fraud and other charges, including delivery of a controlled substance and perjury. He was also accused of shoplifting from a local store. The judge in the case ordered Egeland to pay $90,011 in child support and urged him to seek mental health treatment and counseling while in prison.

The case highlights the severity of Egeland's crimes and the harm caused to his son, who was manipulated into participating in the scam. The additional sentence of three years and ten months is a reflection of the seriousness of Egeland's actions and the need for him to face consequences for his crimes.
details.ground_truth: A white-collar crook is facing extra prison time for having his son shoot him in the legs with a 20-gauge shotgun, the BBC reports. Shannon Egeland, 43, was already facing 10 years for taking part in a vast mortgage-fraud scheme when he had his 17-year-old son shoot him by a road in Caldwell, Idaho, in an attempt to avoid prison. "The psychological and emotional destruction defendant caused this minor child is unimaginable," US attorney Scott Bradford wrote in a sentencing memo. Back in 2009, Egeland was co-owner of an Oregon development company that defrauded banks out of $20 million in loans that he and a partner used to fund a lavish lifestyle. Facing prison, Egeland took out disability insurance and had his son aim for his legs. Egeland told police he'd been attacked after stopping to assist a pregnant driver, but investigators got suspicious when his pricey BMW, his wallet, and his cellphone weren't taken, the Idaho Statesman reports. Then they learned about his recent insurance, and the whole thing unravelled. Now Egeland is facing an extra 3 years and 10 months for his roadside scheme and had his leg amputated from the shotgun blast. Bradford called him a "self-absorbed, opportunistic narcissist," the Oregonian reports, but Egeland—who is now on three medications for mental health—has cast his own judgment. "What bothers me the most is my son—the pain is on him,'' he says. "If I could take it all back, I would, but I can't. That will haunt me the rest of my life.''

**Tesla Autopilot Crash: A Fatal Incident and Ongoing Investigations**

On March 23rd, a fatal crash involving a Tesla vehicle occurred on the 101 Highway near Mountain View, California, resulting in the death of 38-year-old Apple engineer Walter Huang. The incident has sparked a series of investigations and raised concerns about the safety of Tesla's Autopilot system.

According to Tesla, Autopilot was engaged at the time of the crash, with adaptive cruise control follow-distance set to minimum. However, the driver's hands were not detected on the wheel for six seconds prior to the collision, and several visual and one audible hands-on warning were received earlier in the drive. Tesla attributed the severity of the crash to a crushed crash attenuator, a highway safety barrier that was not repaired after a previous accident.

Investigations are ongoing, with the National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) and California Highway Patrol (CHP) inspecting the vehicle and reviewing data from the crash. Huang's family has reported that he complained to the dealership about the Autopilot system's tendency to veer towards the same barrier on multiple occasions, but the issue could not be duplicated at the dealership.

Tesla has defended its Autopilot system, stating that it has improved safety and reduced crash rates by as much as 40% in the US. The company also cited data showing that Tesla owners have driven the same stretch of highway with Autopilot engaged over 85,000 times since its introduction in 2015, with no similar crashes occurring. However, the incident has raised concerns about the limitations of Autopilot and the potential for human error.

In related news, the California Department of Transportation (CalTrans) has released a statement saying that replacement work for the damaged barrier was scheduled, but storms delayed the repair. The incident has also reignited debate about the safety of autonomous vehicles, with Tesla stating that its Autopilot system "unequivocally makes the world safer for vehicle occupants, pedestrians, and cyclists."

The incident serves as a reminder of the complexities and challenges involved in developing and implementing advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS) like Autopilot. As investigations continue, it remains to be seen what lessons can be learned from this incident and how they will impact the development and deployment of autonomous vehicles in the future.
details.ground_truth: The Tesla Model X that slammed into a highway barrier in California last week and killed the 38-year-old Apple engineer at the wheel had been on autopilot at the time of the crash, Tesla confirms in a blog post. But the company says driver Walter Huang "received several visual and one audible hands-on warning earlier in the drive," adding that "the driver’s hands were not detected on the wheel for six seconds prior to the collision." Tesla also laid some blame on state highway officials, asserting that "the reason this crash was so severe is because the crash attenuator, a highway safety barrier which is designed to reduce the impact into a concrete lane divider, had been crushed in a prior accident without being replaced." The fact that the vehicle was on autopilot is likely to be controversial because, as ABC News notes, driver Huang had reportedly been concerned about it. His brother told KGO that Huang had brought his Model X back to the dealership complaining that when he used the feature, the car kept swerving toward the very same barrier that it eventually struck in the fatal collision. The NTSB is investigating the crash, including the fire that broke out after the collision. The accident follows another in Arizona in which a self-driving Uber struck and killed a pedestrian.

**Renowned Actor and Comedian Robin Williams Passes Away at 63**

Robin Williams, a beloved actor and comedian, has passed away at the age of 63. Known for his boundless energy, incredible talent, and generosity of spirit, Williams left an indelible mark on the entertainment industry. He will be remembered for his iconic roles in films such as "Good Will Hunting," "Mrs. Doubtfire," "Aladdin," and "Hook," as well as his stand-up comedy routines that captured the hearts of audiences around the world.

**A Legacy of Joy and Imagination**

Williams' career spanned over three decades, during which he established himself as a versatile actor who effortlessly transitioned between comedy and drama. He was particularly beloved by a generation of young people who grew up watching his films, which often tackled complex themes and issues with humor and sensitivity. His ability to connect with his audience and bring joy to their lives was a hallmark of his career, and his legacy will continue to inspire and delight fans for years to come.

**A Stand-up Comedian at Heart**

Williams' roots as a stand-up comedian were evident in his performances, which were characterized by their rapid-fire pace, physicality, and creativity. He was a master of improvisation, and his ability to think on his feet was legendary. As a journalist who first met Williams in the 1970s, he recalled the comedian's performances at the Comedy Store, where he would effortlessly shift between characters and accents, leaving audiences in stitches.

**A Complex and Turbulent Life**

Despite his many successes, Williams struggled with personal demons throughout his life. He was known to be a complex and troubled individual, and his struggles with addiction and mental health were well-documented. His death was met with shock and sadness, and many are left to wonder what might have been if he had been able to overcome his struggles.

**A Final Farewell**

As the entertainment industry mourns the loss of a beloved figure, fans around the world are taking to social media to pay tribute to Williams' legacy. His iconic films will continue to be celebrated and enjoyed, and his stand-up routines will remain a testament to his incredible talent and generosity of spirit. Robin Williams will be deeply missed, but his legacy will live on in the hearts of all who were touched by his work.
details.ground_truth: After Robin Williams' sudden death, the tributes are rolling in, with many recalling a comic genius grappling with private challenges. A sampling: When it comes to comedy, "there wasn't a faster brain on the planet," writes Tony Hicks in the Contra Costa Times. We'll be hearing "a litany of tributes about one of the great comedians who ever lived. Much of it will be some of the same adjectives used when any great performer dies—especially before their time. But in Williams' case, most of it will be true." Early in his career, "it was clear that Mr. Williams was one of the most explosively, exhaustingly, prodigiously verbal comedians who ever lived," AO Scott writes in the New York Times. And beyond that, "his essential persona as an entertainer combined neediness and generosity, intelligence and kindness, in ways that were charming and often unexpectedly moving." Williams may have gotten his start long before Aladdin, but he gave a "generous and humble" gift to the millennial generation, writes Daniel D'Addario at Salon. "Williams will be remembered for his edgy comedy and for dramatic turns, but for people my age, Williams was our childhood," from Mrs. Doubtfire to Jumanji to Flubber. In the Los Angeles Times, Kenneth Turan recalls a moment when, facing an onslaught of photographers, Williams asked him—in the voice of a fly from a 1950s sci-fi flick—to "help me, Ken. Help me." The experience stuck with him and seems particularly resonant today, he writes.

**MURDER SUSPECT ON THE RUN IN VERMONT CAPITAL**

A murder suspect, Jayveon Caballero, 29, is currently on the run after allegedly shooting a former Vermont Frost Heaves basketball player, Markus Austin, 33, to death in Montpelier, Vermont's capital city. This is the first murder in the city in almost a century, with the last reported murder occurring in the 1920s.

According to police, Caballero and Austin had been involved in an altercation outside a bar in Barre earlier in the night. During the altercation, Austin allegedly assaulted Caballero's girlfriend, Desiree Cary, 22, who was later arrested on unrelated drug charges.

Following the altercation, Caballero confronted Austin outside Austin's apartment in Montpelier and shot him. Witnesses reported that Caballero appeared to show sympathy for Austin immediately after shooting him, but then drove away.

A Vermont State Police investigation revealed that Caballero had boarded a Greyhound bus in White River Junction, headed to New York City, but it is unclear if he got off at a stop before reaching the city. Police are now reaching out to other states to help locate Caballero, who is wanted for second-degree murder.

A $500,000 bail has been set for Caballero, but he remains at large. Police are urging anyone with information about his location or the shooting to come forward.

The victim, Markus Austin, was a former member of the Vermont Frost Heaves, a semi-pro basketball team. The shooting has shocked the community, particularly in the small city of Montpelier, which has a population of just 7,500 residents.

The investigation is ongoing, and police are working to locate Caballero and bring him to justice.
details.ground_truth: Police in the country's smallest state capital are investigating something that hasn't happened there in living memory: a murder. Police in Vermont say they have issued an arrest warrant for 29-year-old Jayveon Caballero, who is accused of fatally shooting Markus Austin in Montpelier early Sunday after an altercation, the Times Argus reports. Witnesses told investigators that Austin, 33, assaulted Caballero’s girlfriend, Desiree Cary, during the altercation outside a bar in the nearby city of Barre early Sunday. Police say Caballero later confronted Austin outside his apartment and shot him dead. The body was found in the parking lot of Austin's apartment complex. A Vermont State Police spokesman says Caballero, who knew Austin before the shooting, fled the town and probably the state after the shooting, WCAX reports. The police spokesman says Caballero is believed to have boarded a Greyhound bus bound for New York City on Sunday morning, which made numerous stops in other states along the way. Later Sunday, Cary, 22, was arrested on apparently unrelated charges of selling heroin and crack cocaine. With around 7,500 residents, Montpelier has the smallest population of any state capital, the AP notes. Police Chief Anthony Facos says this is the city's first murder since a woman shot her husband in the 1920s.

**Super Blue Blood Moon Eclipse on January 31, 2018: A Rare Celestial Event**

On January 31, 2018, a rare celestial event will take place, offering stargazers a unique opportunity to witness a Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse. This event is a combination of a blue moon, a supermoon, and a total lunar eclipse, making it a rare and spectacular occurrence.

**What is a Blue Moon?**

A blue moon is the second full moon that occurs in a single month. This event is relatively rare, occurring about once every 2.7 years. On January 31, 2018, the second full moon of the month will coincide with the total lunar eclipse, making it a blue moon total lunar eclipse.

**What is a Supermoon?**

A supermoon is a full moon that occurs when the moon is at its closest orbital point to Earth, known as perigee. This event makes the moon appear 14% bigger and 30% brighter than a regular full moon. On January 31, 2018, the supermoon will also coincide with the blue moon and total lunar eclipse, making it an extra special event.

**When and Where to See the Super Blue Blood Moon Eclipse**

The super blue blood moon total eclipse will be visible from eastern Asia, across the Pacific to western North America. The best time to see the eclipse is on the night of January 31, with the totality visible from 11:30 AM UTC (Coordinated Universal Time). The eclipse will last for approximately 77 minutes.

**Live Streaming and Viewing Options**

For those unable to witness the event in person, there are live streaming options available. The Virtual Telescope project will provide a live stream of the Jan. 1 supermoon rise over Rome's skyline and the Jan. 31 eclipse from Australia.

**Photographing the Super Blue Blood Moon Eclipse**

For those who want to capture the event, NASA recommends using a daylight white balance for DSLR cameras and adjusting the exposure slider for smartphone cameras. Additionally, taking pictures that put the moon in context with local landmarks can add a sense of place and originality to the photos.

**Conclusion**

The Super Blue Blood Moon Total Eclipse on January 31, 2018, is a rare and spectacular celestial event that offers stargazers a unique opportunity to witness a combination of a blue moon, supermoon, and total lunar eclipse. Whether watched in person or via live stream, this event is sure to be an unforgettable experience
details.ground_truth: Look up at the sky on Jan. 31 and you may witness a sight unseen for 150 years. For the first time since 1866, a total lunar eclipse will occur with the blue moon, or second full moon of the month, which—like the one visible on New Year's Day—will also be a supermoon. Got all that? When the moon is at or near the closest point to Earth in its orbit (aka, a supermoon), it appears 14% bigger and 30% brighter than full moons that occur at the farthest point in the moon's orbit, though the moon will lose brightness as it enters Earth's shadow, according to NASA. Because of the way Earth's atmosphere bends light, the eclipsed moon will take on a reddish hue, something often referred to as a blood moon, making this rare celestial event "a super blue blood moon eclipse," according to the Miami Herald. The next blue moon eclipse won't occur until 2028. The eclipse will last for 3.5 hours, with the total eclipse stretching for 77 minutes, reports Sky & Telescope. Those along the Pacific Rim from Alaska and northwestern Canada to central and eastern Asia, Indonesia, New Zealand, and Australia will get the best view of totality during the middle of the night. However, partial phases of the eclipse should be visible under a clear sky in central North America, western Asia, India, the Middle East and eastern Europe, per Space.com. North American viewers should look up as the moon sets on the morning of Jan. 31, while those on the opposite side of the world should see a partial eclipse as the moon rises. Not in an ideal viewing area? The Virtual Telescope plans to livestream the total eclipse from Australia, which you can watch here, per Quartz. (Bogus glasses damaged a solar eclipse viewer's eyes.)

**Syrian Protests Continue with Largest Gathering in Hama**

The ongoing protests in Syria have reached a boiling point, with the city of Hama witnessing the largest gathering of protesters yet, amounting to over 500,000 people. The protests, which began after a series of peaceful demonstrations, have been met with a fierce crackdown by security forces, resulting in numerous deaths and arrests.

**US Ambassador Visits Hama to Show Solidarity**

US Ambassador to Syria, Robert Ford, visited Hama on Thursday to show support for the protesters and demonstrate the US's commitment to democracy. Ford's delegation met with local residents, including those wounded in the security forces' crackdown, and was greeted with a warm welcome. The visit was seen as a significant gesture of support for the protesters, who have been fighting for their right to express themselves peacefully.

**Reports of Mass Arrests and Deaths**

Activists report that 13 protesters have been killed across the country, including five in the central city of Homs, two in the capital's commercial neighborhood Midan, and six in the Dumair area, east of Damascus. The opposition has accused security forces of using snipers to target protesters.

**International Diplomats Show Support**

The US and French ambassadors have shown their support for the protesters, with Robert Ford visiting Hama and Eric Chevallier, the French ambassador, also present in the city. However, the Syrian government has accused the US of interfering in its affairs and has summoned the US ambassador to Syria, Imad Mustapha, for talks.

**Humanitarian Crisis Unfolds**

An estimated 1,000 Syrians have fled Hama in fear of another military crackdown, with many seeking refuge in the nearby town of Salamiyah. The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights has reported mass arrests and killings in the city, with at least 23 civilians killed since Tuesday.

**Calls for Assad to Quit**

The protests are calling for President Bashar al-Assad to quit and an end to the Baath Party's decades-long grip on power. The opposition sees Hama as a symbol of resistance against injustice and a model for peaceful demonstrations.

**Government Response**

The Syrian government has attempted to downplay the situation, with the state-run newspaper Al-Watan claiming that the situation in Hama was calm and that protesters had been told to clear the streets to avoid a military operation. However, activists and human rights groups have disputed this account, reporting widespread violence and arrests.

details.ground_truth: America's ambassador to Syria visited one of the cities worst-hit by the crackdown against anti-government protesters yesterday, reports CNN. Robert Ford went to the city of Hama "to make absolutely clear with his physical presence that we stand with those Syrians who are expressing their right to speak for change," an embassy spokeswoman says. An estimated 500,000 people joined an anti-government rally in the city last Friday. The Syrian government accused Ford of going to the city without authorization, saying the visit is "clear evidence of the US involvement in the ongoing events in Syria and its bids to aggravate the situations which destabilize Syria.'' An embassy spokeswoman says the Syrian government was told a delegation would be visiting Hama, although no Syrian "handlers" were taken and officials weren't informed about the ambassador's presence. The city has been a symbol of opposition since 1982, when a crackdown ordered by the present Syrian leader's father killed around 20,000 people, al-Jazeera notes.

**Midterm Elections Under Cyber Threat: Multiple Incidents Raise Concerns**

As the United States prepares for the upcoming midterm elections, concerns about cyber threats and foreign meddling have reached a fever pitch. Recent incidents have highlighted the vulnerability of the country's election systems and the potential for manipulation.

In one notable incident, the Democratic National Committee (DNC) initially detected what it believed was a sophisticated attempt to hack into its voter database. However, it was later revealed that the attempt was actually an unauthorized "simulated phishing test." The test, which was designed to look like a legitimate login page, aimed to trick users into handing over their login details. The DNC, with the help of cloud service providers and security research firms, was able to thwart the potential attack.

Meanwhile, Microsoft has reported that hackers tied to Russian intelligence have targeted the Senate and conservative think tanks in the United States, creating fake websites in the process. This incident is part of a broader trend of continued influence operations by Russia and other foreign actors using social media.

The White House cybersecurity coordinator, Rob Joyce, has warned that the cyber age has changed elections forever, with many systems vulnerable to manipulation. He noted that the focus has shifted from using the cyber realm to steal secrets to using it to impose national power.

The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) is currently investigating an incident in which a voter file was compromised, with one official describing it as "further proof that there are constant threats as we head into midterm elections." The voter file is considered the party's most sensitive information.

In response to these incidents, Microsoft, Facebook, and other companies have taken steps to detect and prevent disinformation efforts and foreign meddling. As the midterm elections approach, the country remains on high alert for new hacks and foreign interference.

**Key Takeaways:**

* The Democratic National Committee initially detected a potential hacking attempt into its voter database, but it was later revealed to be an unauthorized simulated phishing test.
* Microsoft has reported that hackers tied to Russian intelligence have targeted the Senate and conservative think tanks in the United States, creating fake websites.
* The White House cybersecurity coordinator has warned that the cyber age has changed elections forever, with many systems vulnerable to manipulation.
* The FBI is investigating an incident in which a voter file was compromised.
* Companies are taking steps to detect and prevent disinformation efforts and foreign meddling ahead of the midterm elections.
details.ground_truth: The FBI is reportedly investigating a sophisticated—but unsuccessful—attempt to hack into the Democratic National Committee's voter database. The DNC learned of the attempt on Tuesday, CNN reports. The attack, which was detected by a cloud service provider and a security research company, involved a fake login page intended to gather usernames and passwords for a service called Votebuilder, which hosts the DNC's voter database. It's a method called "spearphishing," according to the New York Times. "It was very convincing," Mike Murray of the cybersecurity firm Lookout tells CNN, adding that it would be tough to tell the real page from the fake one, even if they were side-by-side. The fake login page was ultimately removed by the cloud hosting company DigitalOcean. The company's chief security officer says the threat was identified before the attack was launched. "We see no evidence that any sensitive data was stolen," he says. A DNC official tells the Times that it is not clear who the hackers were in this case. But, the paper recalls, a 2016 hack of the DNC was ultimately traced to Russia. This latest attempt, DNC Chief Security Officer Bob Lord tells the Times, "is further proof that there are constant threats as we head into midterm elections and we must remain vigilant in order to prevent future attacks." Earlier this week, Microsoft reported that it had uncovered new Russian hacking attempts targeting conservative organizations in the US.

**Boston Marathon Bombings: A Tragedy Unfolds**

On April 15, 2013, the Boston Marathon was marred by two explosions near the finish line, leaving three people dead and numerous others injured. The victims of the bombings have been identified as Martin Richard, an 8-year-old boy from Dorchester, Krystle Campbell, a 29-year-old restaurant manager from Medford, Massachusetts, and a Boston University graduate student from China, whose name has not been released pending family approval.

Martin Richard was watching the race with his family, including his mother, Denise, and 7-year-old sister, who sustained serious leg and head injuries, respectively. His father, Bill Richard, released a statement expressing his grief and gratitude for the support of friends and strangers. A vigil was held at Garvey Park in Dorchester for Martin, where hundreds attended to pay their respects.

Krystle Campbell was waiting near the finish line to cheer on a friend who was running. Her parents, William and Patty Campbell, were initially told by doctors that their daughter had survived the blast, but later realized it was not their daughter. Krystle was a 2001 graduate of Medford High School and worked in the food service industry.

The third victim, a Boston University graduate student, was watching the race with two friends near the finish line. One friend was injured and listed in stable condition, while the other was not hurt. The student's family has requested that their name not be released.

In the aftermath of the bombings, the city of Boston was gripped by a sense of shock and grief. Mayor Thomas Menino vowed that the city would not be intimidated by terror and promised to provide support to those affected by the tragedy. The FBI has confirmed that there are no known additional threats, and officials are working to find those responsible for the bombings.

The bombings have brought out the best in the people of Boston, with strangers coming together to support each other and offer aid. The Salvation Army has set up mobile kitchens to provide food and emotional support, while the First Corps of Cadets Armory has become a staging area for runners looking for their gear bags and trying to make travel arrangements.

President Obama has praised the first responders and others who helped the injured, saying that the response to the bombings is a testament to the character of the American people. Pope Francis has also sent condolences to the victims and their families, calling for unity and resolve in the face of evil.

As the city of Boston begins to heal and recover,
details.ground_truth: Another fatality in the Boston bombings has been identified: The family of 29-year-old Krystle Campbell says she was killed as she and a friend were cheering on another friend who was racing, reports WCVB. "This is just a waste," says her mother. “Everyone that knew her loved her. She was always smiling. You couldn’t ask for a better daughter. She was the best.” Campbell was a restaurant manager, reports the Boston Herald, which notes an especially wrenching detail: Her father was initially told she was alive, but when he got to the ICU, he realized it was his daughter's friend who had survived. Campbell is the second of the three people killed to be identified. The first was 8-year-old Martin Richard, and his father, Bill, released a statement today thanking supporters and asking for privacy, reports WBZ. "My dear son Martin has died from injuries sustained in the attack on Boston. My wife and daughter are both recovering from serious injuries. We thank our family and friends, those we know and those we have never met, for their thoughts and prayers. I ask that you continue to pray for my family as we remember Martin. We also ask for your patience and for privacy as we work to simultaneously grieve and recover. Thank you." The third victim was a grad student at Boston University, but the person's identity is being withheld until the family says it's OK to release, reports USA Today.

**Global Obesity Crisis: A Growing Health Concern**

A recent study published in the Lancet medical journal reveals that the world is facing a growing obesity crisis, with nearly 30% of the global population, or 2.1 billion people, being either obese or overweight. This staggering number is a 28% increase from 1980 to 2013, and a 47% rise in children and adolescents over the same period.

The United States leads the world in obesity, accounting for 13% of the global obese population, despite having only 5% of the world's population. Other countries with high obesity rates include those in the Middle East and North Africa, Central America, and the Pacific and Caribbean islands. In some of these countries, such as Tonga and Kuwait, obesity rates exceed 50% among men and women.

The causes of the obesity crisis are multifaceted, but experts point to the increased availability of cheap, high-calorie foods and sedentary lifestyles as major contributing factors. Obesity is a major risk factor for heart disease, stroke, diabetes, arthritis, and certain cancers, and is estimated to kill 3.4 million adults annually.

The problem is not limited to developed countries; in fact, two-thirds of the world's obese population resides in developing countries. The Middle East and North Africa region has the highest obesity rates, with more than 58% of adult men and 65% of adult women being overweight or obese. Bahrain, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Oman, and Kuwait have seen significant increases in obesity rates.

There is a growing concern that the world's children are being affected by the obesity crisis, with a 47% rise in children and adolescents being overweight or obese over the past 33 years. The availability of cheap, high-calorie foods and sedentary lifestyles are contributing to this trend, and experts warn that obesity is appearing at increasingly young ages.

The World Health Organization has launched a commission to study rising obesity among children worldwide and suggest solutions. The global community must come together to address this growing health concern, which has far-reaching consequences for individuals, communities, and societies.

**Key Statistics:**

* 2.1 billion people worldwide are either obese or overweight (28% increase from 1980 to 2013)
* 13% of the global obese population resides in the United States
* 58% of adult men and 65% of adult women in the Middle East and North Africa region are overweight or obese
* Obesity rates exceed 50%
details.ground_truth: There are more obese or overweight people in the world today than there were people of any weight in 1935, the most comprehensive look at worldwide obesity in decades warns. The 188-country study found that there are 2.1 billion overweight or obese people in the world, making up around 30% of the world's population, up from 20% in 1980, USA Today reports. Not a single country has recorded a decline in obesity over the last 30 years. "We hoped there would be some examples of success that you could latch onto," a study co-author says. "But there's a complete lack of success stories in bringing down obesity." The study published in British medical journal the Lancet found that the Middle East and north Africa had seen the biggest weight gains, though the US still has the largest number of obese people, with 87 million of the world's 671 million obese people, reports Reuters. Other countries, however, have even higher obesity rates, including Tonga, where more than 50% of all adults are obese. "Two-thirds of the obese population actually resides in developing countries," says one of the researchers; they warn that obesity rates are rising among rich and poor countries, men and women, and adults and children alike.

This week, several news stories have caught the attention of the public. The most notable story revolves around Hollywood's Tom Cruise, who has reportedly written an emotional letter to his ex-wife, Katie Holmes. The letter, which was sent as a gesture of goodwill, is said to have been a year in the making, with sources claiming that Cruise has finally come to terms with the end of their marriage.

According to insiders, the Mission Impossible star's letter was a heartfelt and sincere attempt to rekindle their friendship, if not more. Despite the couple's tumultuous past, Holmes is said to have been genuinely touched by Cruise's words, with sources claiming that she has been "cathartically" affected by the letter.

details.ground_truth: It's been a year since Katie Holmes filed for divorce from Tom Cruise, and apparently Cruise is feeling sentimental. A source tells UK magazine Look the actor sent Holmes an "emotional letter" looking to, at the very least, be friends again, Australia's News Network reports. "It's like he's finally recognized that Katie will always be the love of his life," the source says. Even so, "It won't have been an over the top letter—it has been a year and I'm sure Tom will have been aware that Katie wouldn't be interested in running straight back into his arms." Sources close to Holmes say she was "touched" by the letter, which doesn't sound promising for Cruise. "Tom would no doubt take her back in a second, but I don't think that's what Katie wants," says one. (Just to keep things in perspective, we'll point out that Look is the sort of magazine that also runs headlines like, "Royal Bodyguard Warns: 'Kate Middleton's Baby Is In Danger!'") In other news, Huffington Post notes that it's Cruise's 51st birthday.

Summary:

Canadian billionaire Barry Sherman and his wife, Honey, were found dead in their Toronto mansion on December 15, 2017. Barry Sherman, the founder of generic pharmaceuticals giant Apotex Inc., and Honey, a philanthropist, were discovered hanging from a railing surrounding their basement lap pool. The police initially classified the deaths as "suspicious," but not as a homicide. Egyptians have returned to the polls for the final round of voting in the country's parliamentary elections, a crucial step in the transition to democracy after the ouster of former President Hosni Mubarak. The Muslim Brotherhood, an Islamist group, is expected to consolidate its gains in the elections, with analysts suggesting that the group may win a majority of seats in the new parliament.

The Brotherhood's Freedom and Justice party has been leading the polls, winning roughly 40% of the seats in the first two rounds of voting. details.ground_truth: The final round of voting began in Egypt today, and it looks like the Muslim Brotherhood could walk off with a controlling majority of seats in Parliament's lower house. The Muslim Brotherhood's Freedom and Justice Party, the country's mainstream Islamist party, had already won a high percentage of the seats awarded in the first and second rounds of voting. Two of the nine provinces voting today are Brotherhood strongholds, where some of the party's most well-known candidates are running, the New York Times reports. A plurality, not a majority, was originally predicted for the Freedom and Justice Party. A majority would allow the party to govern alone, but the Brotherhood has repeatedly said it plans to form a coalition government, and has aligned itself with more liberal parties rather than the ultraconservative Salafis, who are in second place with up to 25% of the seats so far. Also today, prosecutors began presenting their case against former Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak, Bloomberg notes. His trial resumed last week after a two-month break; state TV today showed Mubarak being carried to and from the courtroom on a gurney. "He deserves an end of humiliation and indignity," said the chief prosecutor, according to the AP.

**Local News Summary**

A dramatic incident occurred in Mendon on Wednesday evening around 8 p.m. A Toyota Corolla driven by Nabila Altahan was suspended from a telephone guy wire after she reacted quickly to a GPS system's instructions to turn around. According to State Police, Altahan had passed her intended destination and the GPS device suddenly directed her to turn around. In her haste, she left the road and the car became lodged on the guy wire of a telephone pole.

Fortunately, neither Altahan nor her passenger was injured in the incident. The scene was witnessed by a bystander at the nearby miniature golf course, who reported seeing Altahan jump out of the car as it was suspended from the pole. State police and the Rutland City Fire Department responded to the scene.

This bizarre incident serves as a reminder to drivers to remain calm and cautious when following GPS directions, and to prioritize their safety on the road.
details.ground_truth: Police in Vermont say a car ended up almost vertical when the driver swerved quickly in response to her GPS ordering her to "turn around." The car was suspended almost vertically on guide wires attached to a utility pole (see a photo here) in Mendon on Wednesday night, reports the AP. Police say 30-year-old Nabila Altahan of Dorchester, Mass., was headed west on US Route 4 when she passed her intended destination and reacted quickly to the instructions, leaving the road at a significant enough speed to propel the vehicle up the wires. She wasn't injured. (This driver followed her GPS into a lake.)
